title: Acupuncture for Combat-Related Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
summary: This randomized clinical trial demonstrates that verum acupuncture effectively treats combat-related posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) with superior efficacy compared to sham treatment, producing a large treatment effect (Cohen d = 1.17) and significant symptom reduction from baseline to posttreatment. Beyond symptom alleviation, verum acupuncture improves the underlying psychobiology of PTSD by enhancing fear extinction—the ability to suppress learned fear responses—as evidenced by significant reductions in fear-potentiated startle during extinction trials in the verum but not sham group. The data reveal a meaningful correlation between symptom reduction and improved fear extinction, suggesting that acupuncture's therapeutic benefit operates through normalization of fear-processing mechanisms disrupted in PTSD. Although sham acupuncture also produced moderate symptom reduction (d = 0.67), verum acupuncture's larger effects indicate genuine acupuncture-specific mechanisms rather than placebo alone. These findings establish acupuncture as an emerging intervention with strong preliminary data for PTSD treatment, particularly for combat-related cases, and suggest it favorably modulates the neurobiological processes underlying traumatic stress responses.

title: Evaluating the feasibility of delivering a pain management programme for adults living with sickle cell disease
summary: Pain is a dominant feature of sickle cell disease that significantly compromises quality of life and is associated with vaso-occlusive crises. Structured pain management programmes (PMPs) have been recommended in clinical guidelines as a therapeutic approach for managing pain in this population. Implementation of a sickle cell pain management programme demonstrated substantial clinical benefits, achieving moderate effect sizes in reducing pain interference, anxiety, depression, and pain-related worry while simultaneously improving self-efficacy and pain acceptance. The programme also produced modest improvements in health-related quality of life. Notably, following participation in the programme, patients reported reductions in both the frequency of self-reported vaso-occlusive crises and hospital admissions. These findings establish the feasibility and effectiveness of structured pain management interventions in addressing the multidimensional burden of pain in adults with sickle cell disease.

title: Menopause: Why is Everyone Talking About it Now?
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: Menopause represents a natural physiological transition that produces individually variable symptoms rather than constituting a disease state. While menopause occurs naturally with aging, iatrogenic menopause can be induced by surgical interventions, radiation therapy, or certain medications. The management of menopausal symptoms and outcomes employs a multimodal therapeutic approach including hormone replacement therapy (MHT), nonhormonal pharmacological interventions, and supervised lifestyle modifications. Each of these treatment strategies has demonstrated efficacy in improving menopausal outcomes and symptom burden. This comprehensive approach to menopause management reflects current clinical understanding that symptom control and quality of life improvement require personalized, multifaceted interventions tailored to individual patient needs and preferences.

title: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) for pain control in women with primary dysmenorrhoea
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) is a non-pharmacological approach for managing primary dysmenorrhoea that works through multiple mechanisms including blocking pain signal transmission to the brain, inhibiting nociceptive transmission, and stimulating endogenous opioid release to reduce uterine muscle ischaemia. Both high-frequency and low-frequency TENS demonstrate effectiveness in reducing menstrual pain compared with placebo or no treatment, with low-frequency TENS showing somewhat greater pain reduction (mean difference −2.04 compared to −1.39 for high-frequency). The primary adverse effect associated with TENS is mild skin irritation or redness around electrode sites, making it a relatively safe alternative to conventional analgesics such as ibuprofen and acetaminophen for women with dysmenorrhoea. These findings suggest TENS may provide a useful non-medication option for managing the characteristic cramping and lower abdominal pain associated with primary dysmenorrhoea, particularly for women who cannot tolerate or prefer to avoid oral medications.

title: Phase II Trial of Pembrolizumab and Epacadostat in Recurrent Clear Cell Carcinoma of the Ovary: an NRG Oncology Study GY016
summary:
# Summary

This Phase II trial evaluated the combination of pembrolizumab (a PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor) and epacadostat (an IDO-1 inhibitor) in patients with recurrent ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), a cancer subtype with poor prognosis and limited treatment options. Epacadostat inhibits IDO-1, an enzyme found in approximately 56% of ovarian tumors that catalyzes tryptophan metabolism into kynurenine, a metabolite that suppresses antitumor immune responses and correlates with reduced CD8+ tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. By blocking this pathway, epacadostat decreases kynurenine levels and downregulates regulatory T cells, thereby enhancing antitumor immunity when combined with pembrolizumab's checkpoint blockade. The combination achieved an objective response rate of 21% with a median progression-free survival of 4.8 months and overall survival of 18.9 months; however, the study closed prematurely before completing biomarker analyses. Common adverse events included electrolyte abnormalities, gastrointestinal toxicities (pain, nausea, vomiting), and in one case, a grade 3 allergic reaction to epacadostat, limiting its clinical utility in this population.

title: Early versus delayed oral feeding after major gynaecologic surgery
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary:
# Summary

This Cochrane systematic review examined whether early oral feeding after major gynecologic surgery improves postoperative recovery compared to delayed feeding protocols. Early oral intake probably results in faster resumption of bowel function, particularly a slight reduction in time to first stool passage and earlier return to solid diet, while showing no harmful effects on postoperative complications despite traditional surgical concerns about vomiting and paralytic ileus. Early feeding may reduce hospital stay by approximately one day and probably decreases infectious complications, though effects on nausea, abdominal distension, and nasogastric tube placement remain uncertain due to low-certainty evidence. Participant satisfaction was higher with early feeding in one study, though quality of life outcomes showed no significant differences between groups. These findings suggest that early oral intake is a safe and potentially beneficial alternative to traditional delayed feeding protocols following major gynecologic surgery, including procedures for endometriosis management.

title: Self-management of primary dysmenorrhea-related pain: cross-sectional study on non-pharmacological interventions
summary: This cross-sectional study examined non-pharmacological self-management strategies employed by women to manage primary dysmenorrhea-related pain. Heat therapy emerged as the most widely adopted intervention, used by 61.5% of participants, followed by herbal tea consumption (42.4%) and massage (30.9%). These non-pharmacological methods collectively demonstrated efficacy in reducing menstrual pain symptoms, offering accessible alternatives for pain management outside of pharmaceutical approaches. The findings highlight the prevalence and importance of self-directed, non-drug interventions in dysmenorrhea management, suggesting that many women prefer or supplement their pain management with these lifestyle-based strategies. This research underscores the value of incorporating knowledge about effective non-pharmacological options into patient education and clinical counseling for women experiencing primary dysmenorrhea.

title: Intravenous Magnesium for the Management of Chronic Pain:An Updated Review of the Literature
summary: Magnesium functions as an analgesic agent by blocking N-methyl-D-aspartate receptors, thereby modulating pain signaling pathways and reducing nociception. Intravenous magnesium administration has emerged as a promising therapeutic approach for managing chronic pain conditions, with particularly strong evidence supporting its efficacy in treating renal colic and pelvic pain. Since pelvic pain is a cardinal symptom associated with endometriosis, intravenous magnesium represents a potential treatment option for endometriosis-related pain management. The mechanism underlying magnesium's analgesic effects involves its interaction with pain transmission pathways, though further investigation is needed to fully elucidate the neurobiological mechanisms by which magnesium attenuates pain signaling. Current evidence suggests intravenous magnesium infusion is an emerging and promising therapeutic modality that warrants consideration as a complementary approach for chronic pain management in conditions such as endometriosis.

title: A rare case of synchronous bilateral ovarian cancer with combined clear cell and mucinous carcinomas and brain metastases
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of synchronous bilateral ovarian cancer combining clear cell carcinoma in the left ovary with mucinous carcinoma in the right ovary, occurring in a 56-year-old postmenopausal woman with stage IVB disease. The patient developed brain metastases as a complication of disease progression, indicating aggressive tumor behavior. Treatment with taxane/platinum-based chemotherapy following surgery provided temporary symptom reduction and disease control. This case highlights the challenging clinical management of synchronous primary bilateral ovarian cancers with mixed histologies and demonstrates the potential for early metastatic spread to the central nervous system. The combination of multiple rare features—bilateral presentation, histologic heterogeneity, and brain involvement—underscores the importance of comprehensive staging and aggressive multimodal therapy in advanced ovarian malignancies.

title: Noninvasive targeted modulation of pain circuits with focused ultrasonic waves
summary: This study demonstrates that transcranial focused ultrasound can noninvasively modulate the anterior cingulate cortex, a key brain region involved in chronic pain processing, offering a potential alternative to invasive neurosurgical approaches like cingulotomy and deep brain stimulation. Active ultrasonic stimulation produced clinically significant pain reduction in 60% of subjects on the first day following treatment, with average pain reductions of 60% immediately post-intervention and 43% by day seven, substantially outperforming sham stimulation which benefited only 15-20% of subjects. The findings suggest that targeted ultrasonic modulation of deep brain affective circuits can effectively treat chronic pain without requiring surgical intervention, representing a novel noninvasive strategy that could have important implications for managing refractory pain conditions, including endometriosis-related pain. The sustained pain relief observed over multiple days following a single treatment session indicates potential for clinical utility in pain management. This approach bridges the efficacy of invasive neurosurgical interventions with the safety profile of noninvasive techniques, establishing focused ultrasound as a promising therapeutic tool for chronic pain conditions resistant to conventional treatments.

title: Pelvic Floor Physical Therapy is Self-Reported as a Minimally Effective, and Sometimes Harmful, Treatment for Pudendal Neuralgia: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined the clinical effectiveness of pelvic floor physical therapy (PFPT) for pudendal neuralgia, a chronic pain condition affecting the pelvic region. Although PFPT is a recommended treatment approach for pudendal neuralgia, self-reported outcomes revealed minimal therapeutic benefit, with participants reporting an average Patient Global Impression of Change score of 4.6, indicating no to minimal improvement. The majority of participants (66%) experienced either no change or minimal improvement, while concerning findings showed that 12% of patients experienced symptom worsening following PFPT treatment. Patients who exclusively received PFPT demonstrated particularly poor outcomes compared to those receiving multimodal approaches. These findings suggest that while pelvic floor physical therapy remains a standard treatment recommendation for pudendal neuralgia, its clinical efficacy may be limited, and in some cases, the intervention may exacerbate symptoms. The results highlight the need for more comprehensive treatment strategies and individualized assessment before initiating PFPT for pudendal neuralgia management.

title: Impact of equine interactions on human acute pain perception: Two cross sectional studies
summary: This research demonstrates that equine-assisted interventions significantly reduce acute pain perception through multiple complementary mechanisms. Direct interactions with horses—whether through equine-assisted psychotherapy, equine-assisted learning, or simple horse interaction without structured therapy—produced statistically significant reductions in pain ratings across two cross-sectional studies. The pain reduction appears to operate through both physiological and psychological pathways, including the release of β-endorphins (natural painkillers), cognitive distraction from pain sensation, induction of positive emotions, and stress reduction. Horses' unique capacity as social prey animals to detect and respond to human emotional and physical cues may enhance their therapeutic efficacy, promoting feelings of relaxation, happiness, confidence, and improved emotion regulation. These findings support the growing recognition that psychosocial factors and alternative therapies, when combined with conventional biomedical treatment, offer more effective pain management than pharmacological approaches alone, particularly avoiding the adverse effects of chronic opioid use such as hyperalgesia and addiction.

title: Mujeres andinas: actitudes en el uso de plantas para el tratamiento de eventos ginecológicos*
summary: This study documents Andean women's attitudes and practices regarding medicinal plants for treating gynecological conditions, with findings revealing both therapeutic benefits and significant health risks. Medicinal plants including ginger, rue, fennel, and red clover are widely used to manage menstrual pain, vaginal infections, inflammatory conditions, and labor-related discomfort, with users reporting high satisfaction and confidence in traditional medicine approaches. However, the indiscriminate use of these plants poses substantial health risks by altering vaginal pH and microbiota, increasing susceptibility to secondary infections and potentially masking serious underlying conditions like endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome that require comprehensive medical intervention. While plant-based therapies offer the advantage of reduced toxicity compared to conventional treatments and show promise in combined therapeutic approaches, women's perception of symptomatic improvement often reflects only superficial symptom relief rather than true disease resolution. The study emphasizes that although medicinal plants provide genuine palliative benefits for gynecological disorders, their unsupervised use can lead to serious health consequences, highlighting the need for healthcare guidance to balance traditional medicine practices with appropriate clinical management of chronic gynecological conditions.

title: Perioperative Opioid Use in Urogynecologic Mesh Removal
summary: This study examined perioperative opioid use patterns in urogynecologic mesh removal patients and their relationship to pain outcomes. Patients presenting for mesh removal who had preoperative narcotic use were younger, had higher baseline pelvic pain scores, and frequently presented with comorbid chronic pain conditions including endometriosis, chronic pain syndrome, and joint or muscle pain—with pain comorbidities increasing the odds of preoperative narcotic use 6.5-fold. While mesh removal successfully reduced dyspareunia across all age groups and provided initial improvement in pelvic pain scores, outcomes diverged significantly by age and comorbidity status: younger patients experienced less durable improvement in generalized pelvic pain, and those with preexisting chronic pain conditions showed less sustained benefit. Most critically, only 27% of patients using narcotics preoperatively discontinued opioid use after surgery, with 87% of those continuing postoperative narcotics having a prior pain diagnosis, suggesting that opioid-induced hyperalgesia or pain centralization may perpetuate narcotic dependence despite successful surgical intervention. These findings indicate that younger age, comorbid pain conditions, and preoperative opioid use are predictors of prolonged postoperative narcotic use and diminished durable pain relief following mesh removal.

title: Comparisons of approaches to pelvic floor muscle training for urinary incontinence in women
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) is an evidence-based first-line treatment for multiple forms of urinary incontinence in women, including stress, urgency, and mixed incontinence. Weakness in the pelvic floor muscles impairs bladder control and causes involuntary urine leakage, which PFMT addresses by improving muscle strength, endurance, and coordination. This training approach effectively reduces urine leakage and produces modest improvements in incontinence-related quality of life, with benefits potentially enhanced by increased training frequency and individual clinic supervision. While various delivery methods—including e-health platforms, group supervision, and indirect coordinated training—all provide some benefit, evidence suggests direct PFMT with individual supervision offers slightly superior quality-of-life outcomes. Intravaginal or intrarectal training devices used adjunctively are generally well-tolerated, though they carry a small risk of adverse effects including vaginal discharge, spotting, and discomfort. Overall, PFMT represents an accessible, effective conservative approach to managing urinary incontinence with quality-of-life benefits that vary modestly based on training intensity and supervision method.

title: The efficacy of traditional Chinese medicine sequential therapy in non-alcoholic fatty liver disease
summary:
# Summary

Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) sequential therapy demonstrates significant efficacy in treating non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), with an overall treatment effectiveness rate of 94.23% compared to 79.17% in control groups. The intervention produces marked improvements in hepatic function and lipid metabolism, substantially reducing liver enzyme markers (ALT, AST, γ-GT) and serum lipid levels (LDL-C, triglycerides, total cholesterol) in treated patients. Beyond liver-specific outcomes, TCM sequential therapy improves quality of life scores and engages multiple therapeutic mechanisms including acupoint stimulation to reduce insulin resistance and oxidative stress, herbal interventions (licorice, coix seed, Asteraceae) to clear pathological dampness and heat, and exercise modalities such as Tai Chi that reduce metabolic parameters including waist circumference and blood glucose. The evidence suggests that TCM therapies work by modulating lipid metabolism and insulin sensitivity while addressing the underlying TCM pathophysiology of NAFLD involving dysfunction of the spleen, stomach, gallbladder, and kidneys, indicating that sequential therapy represents a valuable complementary approach to conventional disease management strategies.

title: The Effect of Osteopathic Visceral Manipulation on Quality of Life and Postural Stability in Women with Endometriosis and Women with Pelvic Organ Prolapse: A Non-Controlled Before–After Clinical Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and pelvic organ prolapse significantly impair quality of life through mechanisms involving visceral dysfunction, adhesion formation, and postural abnormalities. Visceral nociceptors become activated by inflammation and ischemia, contributing to central sensitization and chronic pain, while tissue scarring and pelvic floor dysfunction disrupt the pelvic-lumbar complex and postural stability. This clinical study evaluated osteopathic visceral manipulation as a treatment approach, incorporating techniques such as diaphragm and uterine relaxation alongside pelvic floor training. Visceral manipulation produced statistically significant improvements in quality of life for both endometriosis patients (p=0.0001) and pelvic organ prolapse patients (p=0.0093), with no reported side effects, suggesting the treatment works partly by attenuating sympathetic nervous system activity and reducing somatic and visceral dysfunction. The underlying neurophysiological mechanisms involve spinal cord-mediated somatic-visceral convergence at lamina I, where abnormal nervous system activity creates maladaptive reflex arcs that perpetuate organ dysfunction. These findings support osteopathic manipulative treatment as a complementary physical medicine intervention for managing symptoms and restoring quality of life in women with these gynecological conditions.

title: A recombinant adenovirus vector encoding the light chain of human coagulation factor VII and IgG1 Fc fragment to targeting tissue factor for colorectal cancer immunotherapy in the mouse model
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study evaluated a novel recombinant adenovirus vector (benc) encoding human coagulation factor VII light chain (hfVII-LC) and IgG1 Fc fragment as a targeted immunotherapy for colorectal cancer, which frequently overexpresses tissue factor. The benc vector was designed to specifically target tissue factor, a protein overexpressed in more than 50% of colorectal cancers, using HT-29 colon cancer cells as a model system in SCID mice. Treatment with the benc vector achieved complete inhibition of tumor growth following intravenous injection in all tested animals and demonstrated a preventive effect against lung metastases when administered intratumorally, with only three of five treated animals developing observable lung tumors compared to the control group. The dual-component design combining the ligand-binding properties of factor VII light chain with the immune-recruiting Fc fragment enabled effective suppression of both primary tumor growth and metastatic dissemination. These results suggest that tissue factor-targeted adenovirus vectors encoding factor VII-Fc fusion proteins represent a promising immunotherapeutic approach for treating colorectal cancers with tissue factor overexpression.

title: The effectiveness and safety of physical activity and exercise on women with endometriosis: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, and reduced quality of life through the activation of macrophages and mast cells, leading to inflammation, oxidative stress, and pain. This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that physical activity and exercise interventions significantly improve multiple outcomes in women with endometriosis, including reduced pain intensity, enhanced quality of life, improved mental health, and treatment of pelvic floor dysfunction. Specific exercise modalities such as Hatha yoga, progressive muscle relaxation, the Jacobson method, and the multimodal Physio-EndEA program all showed high efficacy in reducing pain and stress while improving emotional well-being, control, self-image, and social support. Meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials confirmed that physical activity and exercise interventions produce significant improvements in pain reduction and quality-of-life domains including emotional well-being and sense of control compared to control groups. Emerging delivery methods including telehealth and virtual reality-delivered exercise also demonstrated efficacy in reducing pelvic pain, offering accessible alternatives for symptom management. These findings support physical activity and exercise as safe, effective therapeutic approaches for managing endometriosis-related symptoms and improving quality of life in affected women.

title: Comparative efficacy of topical commercial Chinese polyherbal preparation for vulvovaginal candidiasis: a network meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Vulvovaginal candidiasis, caused by *Candida albicans* overgrowth, presents with pruritus and abnormal leukorrhea that significantly impact quality of life. A network meta-analysis comparing topical commercial Chinese polyherbal preparations (CCPPs) found that combination therapy with conventional antifungal treatment (clotrimazole, miconazole, or nystatin) substantially improved clinical outcomes compared to conventional treatment alone. Fufang Shajiziyou suppository combined with conventional treatment demonstrated superior efficacy in achieving negative fungal conversion and reducing recurrence rates, while Kangfu gel most effectively relieved pruritus and Baofukang suppository most effectively reduced leukorrhea when combined with conventional therapy. The active compounds in these preparations—including matrine and osthole, which inhibit yeast-to-hypha transition, and botanical extracts that enhance local immune function and promote epithelial repair—explain their therapeutic mechanisms. Overall, topical CCPPs represent a stable, accessible complementary approach that reduces symptom resolution time and adverse reactions when integrated with standard antifungal therapy.

title: Pain science education concepts for pelvic pain: an e-Delphi of expert clinicians
summary:
# Summary

This e-Delphi study identifies essential pain science education (PSE) concepts for managing persistent pelvic pain conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and vulvodynia. Persistent pelvic pain is a complex condition associated with multiple dysfunctions—including pelvic floor, urinary, bowel, and sexual dysfunction—alongside significant negative impacts on physical, psychological, social, and sexual wellbeing. Pain science education effectively improves pain intensity, disability, and pain knowledge by promoting a shift from biomedical to biopsychosocial conceptualization, helping patients understand that pain does not always indicate tissue damage and involves multiple influencing factors including psychosocial, cultural, and stigma-related contributors. Expert consensus identified 125 learning concepts across general persistent pelvic pain and specific conditions, with particular emphasis on addressing pelvic pain stigma, shame, and taboo that reduce health-seeking behaviors and treatment engagement. Notably, while endometriosis carries pathological contributions to pain, clinicians de-emphasize pathology-pain relationships in favor of teaching broader pain mechanisms such as peripheral and central sensitization, whereas patients value recognition of specific pathological drivers, highlighting a critical gap between clinical practice and biopsychosocial guideline recommendations.

title: An 8-Week Course of Bifidobacterium longum 35624® Is Associated with a Reduction in the Symptoms of Irritable Bowel Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

An eight-week course of Bifidobacterium longum 35624® significantly reduces irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) symptoms through multiple mechanistic pathways. The probiotic strain directly binds to inflamed colonic mucosa and strengthens gut barrier function, while simultaneously modulating the brain-gut-microbiota axis and inducing regulatory T cells that dampen inflammatory responses. Clinical outcomes were substantial, with a 43.5% reduction in total symptom severity scores and over 60% of participants achieving clinically meaningful symptom relief across abdominal pain, bloating, diarrhea, and gas passage. The treatment improved quality of life by reducing IBS symptom interference in daily activities, with both patients and physicians rating the probiotic's effectiveness as good to satisfactory. These findings support targeting dysbiosis-driven visceral hypersensitivity and intestinal barrier dysfunction as viable therapeutic approaches for IBS symptom management.

title: A keep‐it‐simple embolisation approach to treat pelvic congestion syndrome without compromising clinical effectiveness
journal: Journal of Medical Imaging and Radiation Oncology
summary: Pelvic congestion syndrome, caused by incompetence of gonadal veins leading to venous reflux and pelvic varicosities, is a significant source of chronic pelvic pain in women. The keep-it-simple (KIS) embolisation approach, which selectively targets only refluxing veins (predominantly the left ovarian vein), proves effective in treating this condition while offering substantial procedural advantages over extensive approaches, including shorter procedure times, reduced radiation exposure, and fewer coils deployed. Treatment with the KIS approach resulted in impressive symptom improvements across multiple domains, with mean pain reduction of 5.2 points on the visual analogue scale (from 7.8 to 2.7), and relief of associated symptoms including back pain (89%), leg pain/heaviness (90%), bladder symptoms, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, and dysmenorrhea in high proportions of patients. Notably, 72% of patients reported symptom relief within one month using the KIS approach, compared to delayed relief with more extensive embolisation strategies. The KIS strategy also minimizes complications such as coil migration and thrombophlebitis by avoiding unnecessary embolisation of non-refluxing drainage pathways. Although symptomatic return occurred in 15-37% of patients, with age ≥45 years and presence of fatigue as risk factors, the KIS approach demonstrates clinical effectiveness without compromising safety or quality of care.

title: Good-Quality mHealth Apps for Endometriosis Care: Systematic Search
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic condition causing significant pain and fertility problems, but mobile health applications are emerging as effective tools for symptom management and improved patient outcomes. A systematic review identified multiple high-quality mHealth apps serving distinct functions: symptom-tracking apps such as QENDO, Bearable, Luna for Health, and Matilda Health enable patients to monitor their condition and share data with healthcare providers, while specialized apps like the Endo-App demonstrated clinically significant improvements in pain self-efficacy, fatigue, depression, and quality of life. Digital health strategies broadly enhance treatment compliance and self-management capabilities, with many apps incorporating therapeutic alliance features through virtual consultations with endometriosis specialists, online communities, and supportive messaging to reduce patient isolation. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the particular value of these digital resources, as lockdowns elevated endometriosis symptoms for many patients. Additionally, specialized classification apps and the research-focused Phendo app support clinician training and advance understanding of symptom heterogeneity through self-reported data.

title: Analgesic effect of simultaneously targeting multiple pain processing brain circuits in an aged humanized mouse model of chronic pain by transcranial focused ultrasound
summary: This study demonstrates that transcranial focused ultrasound (tFUS) effectively reduces heat hyperalgesia in aged female mice by simultaneously targeting multiple pain-processing brain circuits, particularly the primary somatosensory cortex (S1HL) and agranular insular cortex (AI). The research reveals that multi-circuit stimulation is significantly more effective than single-circuit targeting, with aged mice showing increased pain hypersensitivity compared to younger counterparts, a pattern consistent with age-related changes in nociceptive pathways. The investigators developed a compact, battery-powered ultrasonic amplifier front-end (UAFE) device operating at 500 kHz that delivers low acoustic pressures (≤150 kPa) while maintaining high specificity and deep brain penetration without adversely affecting motor function or coordination. This noninvasive approach addresses critical limitations of opioid-based pain management by offering an alternative that avoids pharmacological side effects while targeting the neurobiological circuits underlying chronic pain. The findings support tFUS as a promising therapeutic strategy for age-related chronic pain conditions, with potential applications extending beyond pain to other neurological disorders including depression and epilepsy.

title: Comparative analysis of spinal anesthesia versus general anesthesia in single-port access laparoscopic adnexal surgery: a propensity score matching study
summary:
# Summary

This propensity score matching study compared spinal anesthesia versus general anesthesia for single-port access laparoscopic adnexal surgery, a minimally invasive procedure used to treat gynecological conditions including endometriosis. Spinal anesthesia significantly reduced immediate postoperative pain scores and postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) incidence compared to general anesthesia, which is attributed to the known emetogenic effects of propofol and desflurane used in general anesthesia. The spinal anesthesia group demonstrated lower patient-controlled analgesia utilization, reduced postoperative antiemetic requirements, and notably shorter hospitalization duration (2.29 days versus longer general anesthesia stays), largely due to reduced PONV incidence. While spinal anesthesia can trigger intraoperative adverse events including hypotension, shoulder pain, and abdominal discomfort—mediated by CO2 retention, phrenic nerve stimulation, and Trendelenburg positioning—these complications were effectively managed with ephedrine for hypotension and dexmedetomidine sedation for discomfort and anxiety. The findings establish spinal anesthesia as a viable and safer alternative to general anesthesia for single-port laparoscopic gynecological surgery, offering superior postoperative recovery profiles relevant to endometriosis pain management.

title: Impact of indocyanine green fluorescence angiography on surgeon action and anastomotic leak in colorectal resections. A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that indocyanine green fluorescence angiography (ICGFA) significantly reduces anastomotic leak rates in colorectal surgery by enabling real-time assessment of bowel perfusion. Pooled data from 45 studies show that ICGFA reduces anastomotic leak incidence to 4.5% compared with 8.5% in controls (OR: 0.47), with particularly pronounced benefits in rectal resections where leak rates dropped from 11.5% to 4.7%. The technique's protective effect was strongest when ICGFA assessment matched surgeon judgment (3.7% leak rate) rather than prompting surgical plan changes (5.7% leak rate), suggesting that ICGFA serves primarily to confirm adequate perfusion rather than to substantially alter operative decisions. Left-sided and rectal resections showed the greatest benefit from ICGFA use, whereas right-sided operations demonstrated minimal additional benefit, reflecting the higher inherent anastomotic leak risk in lower colorectal segments. These findings support ICGFA integration into colorectal resection protocols to reduce a major source of perioperative morbidity through objective perfusion assessment.

title: Trajectories of mHealth-Tracked Mental Health and Their Predictors in Female Chronic Pelvic Pain Disorders
summary:
# Summary

This study examined mental health trajectories in women with chronic pelvic pain disorders (CPPDs), including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and fibroids, using mobile health tracking. The research found that CPPDs are associated with significant psychological comorbidities, including high rates of depression, anxiety, reduced quality of life, and functional disability. Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity emerged as a significant independent predictor of improved global mental health, separate from pain levels and prior psychiatric history. Exercise and physical activity interventions demonstrated beneficial effects on multiple outcomes, including reduced pain severity, improved mood, enhanced pain self-efficacy, and decreased anxiety and depression symptoms. The study supports multimodal management approaches combining exercise, patient self-management strategies (mindfulness, relaxation techniques), and psychosocial interventions such as cognitive behavioral therapy to address both pain and mental health in women with CPPDs.

title: Efficacy and Safety of Non-Pharmacological Therapies for Primary Dysmenorrhea: A Network Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This network meta-analysis compared non-pharmacological therapies for primary dysmenorrhea, a condition characterized by pelvic cramping pain accompanied by dizziness, fatigue, gastrointestinal symptoms, and in severe cases, fainting and depression. Multiple acupuncture-based interventions emerged as the most effective treatments, with moxibustion, acupuncture, and auricular point therapy demonstrating superior pain reduction on visual analog scales compared to placebo and blank controls, occupying the highest rankings in comparative efficacy analyses. Yoga and acupressure also showed significant benefits in reducing dysmenorrheal symptoms, though with somewhat smaller effect sizes than the acupuncture-based approaches. These non-pharmacological therapies represent promising alternatives to conventional pharmacological treatments such as NSAIDs and oral contraceptives, offering effective symptom management without the potential side effects associated with medication use. The substantial evidence supporting acupuncture, moxibustion, and auricular point therapy suggests these interventions should be considered primary options for managing primary dysmenorrhea.

title: Application of International Society for the Study of Women’s Sexual Health consensus algorithm for persistent genital arousal disorder/genito-pelvic dysesthesia to 10 cases and use of epidural spinal injections as long term management
summary: This case series applies the International Society for the Study of Women's Sexual Health consensus algorithm to ten patients with persistent genital arousal disorder/genito-pelvic dysesthesia (PGAD/GPD), identifying lumbar intervertebral disc annular tears as a previously underrecognized cause of this debilitating condition. Annular tears trigger PGAD/GPD symptoms through associated inflammatory changes that can also manifest as radiculopathy, discogenic pain, and radicular pain. Treatment approaches varied by etiology: transforaminal epidural steroid injections (TFESI) with lidocaine and methylprednisolone provided complete symptom relief in multiple patients with MRI-confirmed annular tears, while percutaneous disc decompression and microdiscectomy achieved lasting resolution in others. Pharmacologic management included pregabalin, gabapentin, topiramate, and elagolix for symptom palliation, alongside adjunctive pelvic floor physical therapy and botulinum toxin injections for hypertonic pelvic floor muscles. The findings underscore that PGAD/GPD represents a heterogeneous condition with multiple etiologies requiring individualized multimodal treatment, with epidural spinal injections emerging as an effective long-term management option that reduces access barriers for this understudied disorder.

title: The economic impact of Long-Acting Contraceptives (LARCs) on public health
summary: Long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs), including hormonal intrauterine devices and subdermal implants, provide substantial clinical benefits beyond pregnancy prevention, particularly for endometriosis and adenomyosis management. Both hormonal IUDs and implants effectively reduce heavy menstrual bleeding and associated anemia through distinct mechanisms: levonorgestrel-releasing devices suppress ovulation and thicken cervical mucus, while copper-bearing IUDs create a hostile sperm environment. Implementation of single-visit LARC placements and nurse-administered insertion training significantly increase access and uptake, with evidence demonstrating that clinical outcomes remain consistent regardless of provider type. Economically, modest shifts toward LARC adoption yield substantial public health benefits, with studies showing that even a 5% increase in uptake prevents thousands of unplanned pregnancies annually and generates millions in cost savings while reducing maternal and infant morbidity and mortality. LARCs are associated with relatively few side effects compared to alternative contraceptive methods, positioning them as a cost-effective strategy for both symptom management and prevention of pregnancy-related complications.

title: Does interdisciplinary group care for the treatment of endometriosis improve pain interference: protocol for a pilot randomised controlled trial at an urban academic medical centre
summary: Endometriosis is a prevalent condition affecting 10-15% of people assigned female at birth, causing chronic pelvic pain along with infertility, bowel and bladder dysfunction, mood disorders, and musculoskeletal pain that significantly impair quality of life. Current standard treatments include analgesic medications, ovulation-suppressing drugs, and surgery, yet these symptom-focused approaches often fail to address the multifaceted nature of endometriosis-related pain. This pilot randomized controlled trial evaluates PEEPS, an interdisciplinary group care program that integrates evidence-based interventions including pelvic floor physical therapy, yoga, and mindfulness alongside peer support and healthcare education. The intervention targets pain interference through multiple mechanisms: physical therapy and yoga reduce pain intensity and improve pelvic floor function and sexual function, mindfulness decreases anxiety and depression, peer support reduces social isolation and institutional betrayal, and health education increases self-efficacy and healthcare navigation capacity while reducing fear-avoidance behaviors. The study hypothesizes that the comprehensive, peer-supported approach of PEEPS will produce greater reductions in pain interference and improved quality of life compared to standard care, potentially establishing a new interdisciplinary model for endometriosis management.

title: Virtual Reality Interventions and Chronic Pain: Scoping Review
summary: Virtual reality interventions show promise as nonpharmacological treatments for chronic pain conditions by leveraging immersive technology to reduce both pain symptoms and associated psychological comorbidities like anxiety and depression. VR-based behavioral therapies commonly integrate multiple evidence-based techniques including mindfulness, pain neuroscience education, and cognitive behavioral therapy, which have independently demonstrated efficacy in reducing pain and fear-avoidance behaviors in chronic pain populations. The immersive quality of VR appears to be a key therapeutic mechanism, allowing distraction from pain and facilitating delivery of interventions through various modalities such as body swapping for neuropathic conditions and mirror therapy for phantom limb pain. Beyond pain reduction, VR interventions have been assessed for broader quality-of-life outcomes including improvements in mood, sleep, physical activity, and reductions in opioid use, though these secondary endpoints remain understudied. The review indicates that while VR shows high efficacy for acute and chronic pain, and established benefits for mood and anxiety disorders, further standardization and long-term follow-up studies are needed to establish its sustained effectiveness as an alternative to pharmacological treatments for chronic pain management.

title: Efficacy and safety of therapeutic exercise for primary dysmenorrhea: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that therapeutic exercise significantly reduces primary dysmenorrhea pain, with strength training showing the highest efficacy (SUCRA ranking 71.0%) followed by aerobic exercise (63.2%), relaxation exercise (60.8%), and stretching (ranked accordingly). Optimal treatment parameters include exercise duration of at least 8 weeks, frequency exceeding 3 times per week, session length greater than 30 minutes, and a total weekly volume of 90 minutes or more, all of which independently produce statistically significant reductions in visual analog scale pain scores. Multiple exercise modalities including aerobic exercise, mind-body therapy, yoga, and multicomponent training all effectively reduce dysmenorrhea symptoms, with exercise interventions showing clinically meaningful pain reduction even at lower doses. Beyond pain management, physical exercise provides substantial quality-of-life benefits and mental health improvements while remaining cost-effective with minimal side effects, offering a non-pharmacological alternative or complement to standard treatments such as NSAIDs and oral contraceptives. These findings underscore the therapeutic potential of structured exercise programs for primary dysmenorrhea, with particular emphasis on strength training as the superior modality when dose parameters are optimized.

title: Surgical treatment of rare peripheral nerve lesions: long-term outcomes and quality of life
summary: This study examines the surgical management of rare peripheral nerve lesions, including a case of isolated sciatic nerve endometriosis among eight total cases spanning benign and malignant neoplasms. Endometriosis, along with hemangioblastoma, NTRK-rearranged spindle cell neoplasm, and B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma, represents a rare but clinically significant cause of peripheral nerve compression affecting structures such as the sciatic, ulnar, tibial, and brachial plexus nerves. Surgical decompression and resection effectively reduced pain intensity and paresthesia symptoms, with patients demonstrating significant symptom relief within days to weeks of treatment. The degree of nerve invasion and extent of postoperative sequelae emerged as stronger predictors of long-term functional outcomes than lesion histology alone, suggesting that anatomical involvement patterns determine recovery trajectories. Adjunctive chemotherapy contributed to progressive recovery of motor strength in malignancy cases, while physical therapy supported rehabilitation across benign lesions. These findings demonstrate that surgical intervention for rare peripheral nerve lesions, including endometriosis, provides meaningful symptom improvement and quality of life restoration despite variable prognostic factors.

title: Evaluating the Effectiveness of Radiofrequency in Multimodal Physiotherapy for Postpartum Pelvic Pain: The RASDOP Protocol—A Mixed-Methods Study †
summary:
# Summary

Postpartum pelvic pain affects approximately 30-50% of women following vaginal delivery and is frequently associated with perineal trauma, pelvic floor muscle overstretching, myofascial pain syndrome, sexual dysfunction, and dyspareunia. The study evaluates radiofrequency therapy combined with multimodal physiotherapy (including pelvic floor muscle training, manual therapy, and therapeutic education) as a treatment approach for this condition. Radiofrequency stimulates fibroblasts to produce collagen and elastin, thereby improving pelvic floor muscle strength and tissue quality. The evidence indicates that the integration of radiofrequency into multimodal physiotherapy protocols significantly enhances treatment efficacy compared to physiotherapy alone, reducing pain intensity, myofascial pain syndrome, dyspareunia, urinary incontinence, and improving sexual function. This combined approach represents an effective strategy for managing the complex tissue and neuromuscular pathology underlying postpartum pelvic pain.

title: Millimetre wave-based neuromodulation combined with coaching improves quality of life in fibromyalgia patients: A 9-month prospective, multicenter, open, randomized, controlled trial
summary: This 9-month randomized controlled trial demonstrates that combined millimeter wave-based neuromodulation via a wristband and coaching significantly improves quality of life and reduces multiple symptoms in fibromyalgia patients. Fibromyalgia is a chronic pain disorder characterized by widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, stiffness, and mood disturbances, with central sensitization as the predominant underlying mechanism. The intervention works by activating skin nerve endings, which stimulates endorphin release and promotes parasympathetic activation, thereby improving sleep, stress regulation, pain, anxiety, and fatigue. Patients receiving immediate treatment showed substantial reductions in Fibromyalgia Impact Questionnaire scores at three months (69.3 to 53.4), with improvements maintained during autonomous use but increasing again after device removal, indicating treatment dependency. Both sleep quality and psychological symptoms including depression and anxiety improved significantly compared to standard pharmaceutical approaches like duloxetine and pregabalin. While adverse events were generally mild and nonserious—including sensations of heat, local wrist pain, and headaches—the sustained clinical benefits suggest millimeter wave neuromodulation combined with coaching represents a promising non-pharmacological approach for fibromyalgia management.

title: A Digital Program for Daily Life Management With Endometriosis: Pilot Cohort Study on Symptoms and Quality of Life Among Participants
summary: Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and dysuria that substantially impact quality of life. This pilot study evaluated the School of Endo digital program, a cognitive behavioral therapy-based intervention incorporating disease education, dietary modifications, physical activity, mind-body approaches, and physiotherapy, in managing endometriosis symptoms over three months. Participants using the program demonstrated significant improvements in global symptom burden, general pain, dysmenorrhea, dysuria, chronic fatigue, neuropathic pain, endo belly, anxiety, and depression compared to controls, with corresponding improvements in quality of life measured by standardized instruments. The program leveraged multiple evidence-based components including physiotherapy exercises to improve pelvic mobility and sexual function, dietary changes to reduce inflammation and digestive symptoms, and behavioral strategies such as mindfulness, cognitive restructuring, and emotion regulation to enhance pain management. Importantly, participants in the intervention group showed significantly greater knowledge of endometriosis compared to controls, suggesting that integrated digital health platforms combining education with nonpharmacological interventions represent a promising complementary approach to hormonal and surgical treatments. These findings support the use of multimodal digital programs to improve symptom management and quality of life in endometriosis patients.

title: Differential effects of low- and high-frequency sacral neuromodulation on urinary symptoms: high-frequency improves Qmax, low-frequency enhances bladder capacity
summary: This study demonstrates that sacral neuromodulation (SNM) at different frequencies produces distinct urodynamic effects in treating urinary dysfunction. High-frequency SNM primarily improves maximum urinary flow rate (Qmax) and detrusor contractility, while low-frequency SNM preferentially increases maximum bladder capacity. Both stimulation frequencies effectively reduce urination difficulty and waiting times, with stimulation frequency showing significant correlation with overall urodynamic parameters, and higher frequencies associated with superior urodynamic outcomes. The differential mechanisms suggest that high-frequency stimulation optimizes bladder emptying through enhanced contractile function, whereas low-frequency stimulation enhances storage capacity. Both treatment modalities substantially improve quality of life and reduce urinary symptoms, though high-frequency SNM appears to offer greater urodynamic advantages. These findings indicate that SNM frequency selection should be tailored to target specific urodynamic dysfunctions in individual patients.

title: Alkalinised lidocaine as an anaesthetic before onabotulinumtoxinA injections. a randomised trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial evaluated alkalinized lidocaine as a preoperative anesthetic for onabotulinumtoxinA (BTX-A) injections in patients with overactive bladder syndrome. Alkalinization with sodium bicarbonate enhances lidocaine's pharmacological properties by reducing ionization and improving intravesical drug diffusion, which facilitates more rapid nerve blockade compared to standard acidic lidocaine formulations. The study demonstrated that intravesical alkalinized lidocaine significantly reduced procedural pain scores on the visual analog scale compared to placebo, with mean VAS scores of 21.3 mm following alkalinized lidocaine versus higher values in control groups. Procedural complications including haematuria, dysuria, and urinary tract infection occurred at similar or slightly lower rates with alkalinized lidocaine pretreatment, indicating good safety. These findings support the clinical use of alkalinized lidocaine as an adjunctive anesthetic strategy for BTX-A procedures, building on its established efficacy in treating interstitial cystitis and painful bladder syndrome through mechanisms involving sodium channel blockade and anti-inflammatory effects on mast cell stabilization.

title: Efficacy and safety of acupuncture-related therapies in symptomatic endometriosis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth outside the uterus, causing significant morbidity through pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, infertility, and dyspareunia. This systematic review and network meta-analysis evaluated acupuncture-related therapies for symptomatic endometriosis management, finding that multiple acupuncture modalities—including ear electroacupuncture (SUCRA = 83.0%), needle warming moxibustion with Modified Neiyi Zhitong Formula (SUCRA = 80.6%), body electroacupuncture, and auricular needle-embedding—demonstrated efficacy in treating endometriosis symptoms. These therapies work through mechanistic pathways including promotion of pelvic circulation, reduction of uterine smooth muscle spasms, and hormonal regulation. The analysis revealed that acupuncture-related interventions combined with conventional pharmacotherapies (gestrinone, GnRH-α agonists, dydrogesterone, and hormonal contraceptives) were effective in reducing clinical symptoms and biomarkers including serum CA125 and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-8). Overall, acupuncture-related therapies show promise as adjunctive or standalone treatments for symptomatic endometriosis, with particular efficacy demonstrated by auricular and electroacupuncture approaches.

title: Pelvic pain education – A short review on pelvic pain and endometriosis educational programs for adolescents
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary:
# Summary

Persistent pelvic pain in adolescents is strongly associated with anxiety, depression, poor school attendance, and social withdrawal, while recurrent dysmenorrhea can trigger hyperalgesia priming that leads to chronic pain conditions. Poor pelvic health literacy exacerbates these outcomes by increasing pain catastrophizing and disability, with normalization of pain further delaying medical intervention. Educational programs addressing menstrual health and endometriosis—including PPEP Talk® and Period Talk™—have demonstrated substantial efficacy in improving pelvic pain literacy, reducing stigma surrounding menstrual symptoms, and destigmatizing emotional reactivity. School-based menstrual health and endometriosis programs significantly improve early detection of pelvic pain and increase uptake of specialist care among young women. These findings underscore the critical role of targeted educational interventions in breaking the cycle of delayed diagnosis and psychological burden associated with endometriosis and related pelvic pain conditions in adolescence.

title: Working ability, Location, Intensity, Days of Pain, Dysmenorrhea (WaLIDD): cross-cultural adaptation, reliability, and validity of the Turkish version
summary: Dysmenorrhea, a gynecological condition causing painful menstrual cramps in reproductive-age women, manifests as spasmodic pain alongside nausea, fatigue, headaches, and sleep disruptions that significantly impair focus and work productivity. The condition exists in two forms: primary dysmenorrhea, associated with psychological stress and menstrual irregularities, and secondary dysmenorrhea, linked to pelvic pathologies including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids. The Working Ability, Location, Intensity, Days of Pain, Dysmenorrhea (WaLIDD) score was developed as a multidimensional self-report assessment tool to evaluate dysmenorrhea severity, pain location across anatomical sites, and pain-related disability. The Turkish version of WaLIDD demonstrated good reliability and validity, showing strong positive correlation with the Premenstrual Symptoms Impact Questionnaire and moderate correlation with the Pain Disability Index, establishing its clinical utility. This validated Turkish-language instrument can effectively assess dysmenorrhea in clinical practice and research settings, providing comprehensive evaluation of how menstrual pain impacts women's working ability and quality of life.

title: Clinical association of body symptoms and primary dysmenorrhea among young and middle-aged women: an observational study
summary:
# Summary

This observational study demonstrates that specific body symptoms are significantly associated with primary dysmenorrhea risk in young and middle-aged women. Cold extremities, dull abdominal pain, and edema individually increase dysmenorrhea risk by 43-53%, with cumulative risk increasing substantially when all three symptoms are present (2.74-fold increased risk). The study identifies fatigue, dizziness, and low back pain as additional prevalent symptoms in dysmenorrheic women, while established risk factors including irregular menstrual cycles, early menarche, stress, and depressive mood further elevate dysmenorrhea susceptibility. Beyond pharmacological treatments (nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and hormone therapy), the research validates multiple non-pharmacological interventions including transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, acupuncture, manual therapy, pelvic floor exercises, and regular exercise as effective pain management strategies. The authors propose that the constellation of body symptoms may represent a specific traditional Chinese medicine body constitution characterized by cold accumulation, Qi stagnation, and blood stasis, suggesting that dysmenorrhea management could benefit from integrative approaches addressing both underlying physiological mechanisms and holistic body condition assessment.

title: A Study on 10-Week Combined Aerobic and Resistance Training Exercise Prescription for Female Patients with Pelvic Floor Dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

Female pelvic floor dysfunction, primarily caused by connective tissue or ligament damage often resulting from pregnancy and childbirth, impairs the regulation of urination and defecation through compromised muscle function. A 10-week combined aerobic and resistance training intervention significantly improved overall pelvic floor muscle function, with mean scores increasing from 51.129 to 73.481, by enhancing both fast-twitch (Type II) and slow-twitch (Type I) muscle fiber strength through specific exercises including Kegel exercises, bridge hip-raises, stationary cycling, and brisk walking. The exercise intervention reduced pathological markers associated with dysfunction, including resting tension elevation, rise time, and recovery time, all of which contribute to symptoms such as dyspareunia, urinary incontinence, pelvic pain, and sexual dysfunction. By strengthening fast-twitch fibers that enable rapid pelvic floor contractions and slow-twitch fibers that provide sustained support and endurance, the combined training approach offers a non-invasive and cost-effective treatment alternative to pharmaceutical or surgical interventions. These results demonstrate that targeted exercise therapy effectively addresses the biomechanical deficits underlying pelvic floor dysfunction and improves associated quality-of-life outcomes.

title: The Golden Year? Early Intervention Yields Superior Outcomes in Chronic Pelvic Pain with Pudendal Neuralgia: A Comparative Analysis of Early vs. Delayed Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Pudendal neuralgia causes chronic pelvic pain by entrapping or irritating the pudendal nerve, resulting in characteristic pain in the genitalia, perineum, and rectal regions that worsens with sitting. Early intervention with pudendal nerve infiltration using ropivacaine and triamcinolone acetonide significantly outperforms delayed treatment, with early-treated patients achieving greater reductions in pain severity (5.4 versus 3.4 points on the visual analog scale) and substantially improved quality of life scores. Importantly, multivariate analysis identified a critical 13-month treatment threshold, beyond which outcomes deteriorate markedly; early intervention reduced reinfiltration rates from 35% to 10% and enabled 74-81% of patients to discontinue pain medications including gabapentin, tramadol, and dexketoprofen. Delayed treatment led to higher maintenance doses of all neuropathic medications and persistent emotional descriptors of pain such as "distressing" and "unbearable." These findings underscore that timely diagnosis and intervention prevent the cascade of chronic inflammation, micro-scarring, and pelvic floor muscle dysfunction that perpetuates pain and medication dependence in pudendal neuralgia-associated chronic pelvic pain.

title: Educational and Clinical Applications of a Web- and Android-Based Telemedicine Platform to Expand Rural Health Care in Ecuador
summary: A web- and Android-based telemedicine platform (WATP) was developed and validated to expand healthcare access in rural Ecuador, demonstrating 100% diagnostic accuracy through cross-validation with specialist physicians. The platform successfully improved diagnostic skills and clinical case documentation abilities among medical students while treating diverse conditions including dermatitis, parasitosis, and respiratory infections. Among gynecological cases managed through the platform, endometriosis accounted for 5% of presentations, alongside menstrual abnormalities (25%), candidiasis (8%), and abnormal uterine bleeding (6%), highlighting the breadth of reproductive health issues affecting rural populations. The WATP proved effective in reducing healthcare inequities by enabling teleconsultations between general practitioners and specialists, with particular value in addressing conditions linked to limited access to clean water and sanitation. This telemedicine approach demonstrates scalable potential for improving both clinical preparedness and diagnostic accuracy in resource-limited settings, offering a practical solution to barriers in rural health delivery.

title: The effect of virtual reality (VR) glasses and therapeutic touch (TT) on pain, anxiety, and patient satisfaction during intrauterine insemination (IUI) compared to standard care: a single-blind, randomized controlled trial
summary: Intrauterine insemination (IUI) is an invasive procedure that induces significant pain and anxiety in women, both of which negatively impact quality of life during fertility treatment. This randomized controlled trial compared virtual reality glasses (VRG) and therapeutic touch (TT) as adjunctive interventions against standard care, demonstrating that both approaches substantially reduced procedural pain and anxiety. Virtual reality glasses proved particularly effective at reducing pain (3.7 ± 2.7 versus 5.9 ± 2.2 in controls) through mechanisms including increased endorphin and oxytocin release alongside decreased adrenaline, while therapeutic touch achieved superior patient satisfaction (9.2 ± 1.1) and comparable anxiety reduction (43.0 ± 4.2 versus 49.9 ± 4.0 in controls). Both interventions promoted overall healing and wellbeing by disrupting the pain-anxiety cycle that characterizes the IUI experience. These findings support integrating non-pharmacological, mind-body interventions into fertility procedures to enhance patient comfort and satisfaction, with particular relevance for women with endometriosis-related infertility who may experience heightened pain sensitivity.

title: Physical activity level in women with primary dysmenorrhea: A cross-sectional observational study
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea, characterized by menstrual pain caused by elevated prostaglandin production that promotes uterine ischemia and contraction, is strongly associated with sedentary behavior, with approximately 38% of affected women performing no intense physical activity in the preceding week. Multiple factors contribute to dysmenorrhea risk, including psychological factors (anxiety, depression, stress), reproductive characteristics (early menarche, menorrhagia, irregular cycles), lifestyle habits (smoking, alcohol consumption, caffeine intake), and reduced physical activity levels. Physical activity ameliorates menstrual pain through multiple biological mechanisms: exercise increases anti-inflammatory cytokines like interleukin-10 while decreasing pro-inflammatory markers (interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha), stimulates endogenous opioid release, reduces prostaglandin F2α levels, and activates spinal cord pain modulation pathways through mobilization. Exercise therapy, particularly aerobic exercise performed 45-60 minutes at least three times weekly, is recommended in clinical practice guidelines and demonstrates effectiveness in reducing menstrual pain intensity, stress levels, and medication requirements. Various exercise modalities including stretching, strength training, and high-intensity interval exercise show beneficial effects on pain reduction in dysmenorrhea patients.

title: Dietary Modification and Supplement Use For Endometriosis Pain
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by pelvic pain, abdominal bloating, and multiple systemic symptoms including infertility, fatigue, and gastrointestinal dysfunction. The gut microbiome plays a regulatory role in pain pathways in inflammatory conditions, and dietary modification can influence microbiome composition to potentially ameliorate symptoms. Among endometriosis patients surveyed, dietary modifications showed the highest efficacy for pain improvement, with 66.9% of those implementing dietary changes reporting pain reduction, compared to 43.4% using supplements. Specific dietary interventions showed variable but clinically meaningful benefits, including alcohol reduction (53.2%), gluten reduction (45.4%), dairy reduction (45.2%), and caffeine reduction (43.4%), while the low-FODMAP diet and magnesium supplementation showed more modest pain reduction rates. These findings demonstrate that dietary and supplementary interventions represent accessible self-management strategies that may complement conventional medical treatments for endometriosis-related pain and improve overall quality of life.

title: Efficacy and safety of ultrasound-guided percutaneous thermal ablation for abdominal wall metastases: a retrospective study
summary: Ultrasound-guided percutaneous thermal ablation, primarily using microwave ablation (MWA), effectively treats abdominal wall metastases arising from various primary malignancies including colorectal, ovarian, renal, and hepatic cancers. The technique significantly reduced pain scores from 7.09 to 2.18 and achieved tumor volume reduction rates of 45.4% at one month, increasing to 96% by six months, with all patients showing successful treatment and no local recurrence during follow-up. Real-time ultrasound guidance improves ablation accuracy and enables concurrent nerve block techniques that interrupt pain signal transmission, with intraoperative pain scores maintained between 0-4. The minimally invasive approach offers substantial advantages over surgical resection and radiotherapy by reducing operative time, trauma, and complications while avoiding radiation damage to surrounding tissues, with saline injection providing additional protection to adjacent structures. Quality of life assessments showed significant improvement following MWA, with no serious complications reported and only mild postoperative pain responsive to conservative management with analgesics and local cold compression. This technique represents a safe and effective alternative for patients unsuitable for conventional surgery or radiotherapy.

title: Endourological treatment of ureteral strictures with the use of self-expanding stents: is it possible to completely cure the stricture endoscopically? A report on the experience with implantation of 35 stents with a two-year follow-up period; a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study evaluated the effectiveness of self-expanding ureteral stents (Allium® SUS) in treating 35 patients with ureteral strictures over a two-year follow-up period. Uncomplicated strictures, particularly short single strictures, responded well to endoscopic treatment with 80% achieving full therapeutic success and improved quality of life in 100% of patients post-treatment, whereas complicated strictures showed only 15% success rate and were associated with higher rates of serious complications including urinary tract infections and stent migration. The study identified multiple etiologies for strictures, including iatrogenic causes from stone treatment and endoscopic procedures, as well as non-iatrogenic causes such as endometriosis, with stricture characteristics like length and multiplicity being critical prognostic factors. While self-expanding stents effectively maintained ureteral patency (88% preserved) and renal outflow (70% in complicated cases), long-term stenting complications and persistent hydronephrosis (71.4%) suggest that complete endoscopic cure remains unlikely for complex strictures, which may require reconstructive surgery or alternative management strategies. The protective pressure mechanism of the stent scaffold prevents tight stricture reformation, but the unfavorable urinary environment promotes tissue scarring and recurrent strictures, leading to reduced quality of life from chronic stenting or necessitating nephrectomy in cases of renal dysfunction.

title: Knowledge domain and trends in acupuncture for stroke research based on bibliometric analysis
summary:
# Summary

This bibliometric analysis of acupuncture for stroke research reveals acupuncture as a promising non-pharmacological intervention that significantly improves motor function, swallowing ability, and cognitive function in stroke patients while reducing post-stroke complications including motor dysfunction and spasticity. The therapeutic mechanisms underlying acupuncture's efficacy involve enhanced neuroplasticity, neural repair, and functional recovery, offering a safe and cost-effective alternative to conventional treatments that may carry higher risks such as bleeding. Research in this domain is dominated by Chinese institutions and publications across neuroscience and clinical neurology disciplines, with specialized techniques including scalp acupuncture and electroacupuncture emerging as prominent treatment modalities alongside traditional approaches. The literature demonstrates that acupuncture improves quality of life and functional recovery with minimal side effects, positioning it as an important complementary strategy in stroke rehabilitation alongside evidence-based acute interventions such as thrombolysis and endovascular treatment.

title: Patients’ Experiences of Digital Health Interventions for the Self-Management of Chronic Pain: Systematic Review and Thematic Synthesis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review demonstrates that digital health interventions (DHIs), including smartphone applications and web-based platforms, effectively reduce symptoms of chronic pain conditions and improve quality of life and disability outcomes. Key self-management techniques integrated within these DHIs—such as cognitive behavioral therapy, exercise, and relaxation techniques—work synergistically to treat chronic pain. Beyond symptom reduction, DHIs foster positive psychological outcomes by increasing patient self-awareness, motivation, confidence, and independence while improving understanding of the underlying condition. The evidence suggests that digital interventions bridge the gap between clinical care and patient self-management, offering accessible tools that enhance engagement and empower individuals living with chronic pain conditions including fibromyalgia, osteoarthritis, and cancer-related pain.

title: Transversus abdominis plane (TAP) blocks for prevention of postoperative pain in women undergoing laparoscopic and robotic gynaecological surgery
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review evaluated transversus abdominis plane (TAP) blocks as a pain management strategy for women undergoing minimally invasive gynecological surgery. TAP blocks reduce postoperative pain by delivering local anesthetic agents (bupivacaine, levobupivacaine, or ropivacaine) to sensory nerves in the fascial plane between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, thereby blocking pain signals from abdominal wall incisions. Women receiving TAP blocks experienced small but clinically meaningful reductions in pain at 24 hours and pain with movement compared to sham blocks, though effects on opioid consumption and postoperative nausea were minimal. The procedure proved safe with no serious adverse events reported among over 525 women studied. These findings support TAP blocks as part of a multimodal, opioid-sparing pain control approach, which aligns with enhanced recovery guidelines and helps reduce chronic postsurgical pain, hospital stay duration, and the risk of prolonged opioid dependence that can follow gynecological procedures.

title: Good long-term results of sacral neuromodulation for endometriosis related chronic pelvic pain
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that sacral neuromodulation (SNM), which delivers electrical stimulation to sacral nerve roots S3 or S4, provides effective long-term pain relief for patients with endometriosis-related chronic pelvic pain who have failed conventional treatments. Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue in the pelvic cavity that causes pain through mechanisms involving altered nerve activity rather than solely the ectopic growths themselves. In the study population, pain scores decreased dramatically from a median of 7.4 to 2.3 on a numerical rating scale, with 71% of patients receiving an implantable pulse generator showing sustained improvement and 88% achieving long-term pain reduction. The cohort predominantly presented with deep endometriosis (88%) and had previously undergone extensive surgery including hysterectomy, demonstrating SNM's value as a treatment option for refractory cases where standard medical and surgical therapies have proven insufficient. SNM's mechanisms likely involve modulation of spinal cord reflexes and brain networks, though the exact pathway remains incompletely understood. These findings highlight SNM as a significant therapeutic option that can substantially improve quality of life in endometriosis patients suffering from chronic pelvic pain.

title: Analysis of the Clinical Efficacy of Interlock Detachable Coil Interventional Embolization on Pelvic Congestion Syndrome and Ovarian Reserve Function: A Retrospective Study
summary: Pelvic congestion syndrome (PCS), characterized by chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and varicose veins, develops when ovarian vein dilatation occurs in response to pelvic pressure and hormonal changes. While pharmaceutical interventions like medroxyprogesterone acetate provide only short-term relief, interventional embolization using interlock detachable coils achieves superior outcomes, with 60-100% of patients experiencing significant symptom relief. The study demonstrated that combining precise interventional embolization with interlock detachable coils and endocrine hormone therapy produces the highest efficacy, achieving a 90.91% total effective rate compared to 81.82% and 81.82% in control groups, while preventing ectopic embolism complications that occurred in 2-4.55% of other treatment approaches. This combined approach works through multiple mechanisms: the coil increases vascular tension and reduces congestion, while endocrine therapy simultaneously inhibits ovarian hormone secretion (reducing FSH, estrogen, and FSH/LH levels) and suppresses estrogen-driven vascular proliferation. Post-treatment improvements in vascular parameters included increased ovarian artery peak blood flow velocity, decreased resistance index, and reduced pelvic vein diameter and varicose plexus area. These findings support interventional embolization combined with endocrine hormone therapy as a more effective alternative to pharmaceutical management alone for alleviating PCS-related pain and improving vascular hemodynamics, though at the cost of temporary ovarian function suppression.

title: Subdermal implants vs. levonorgestrel intrauterine devices outcomes in reproductive-aged women: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis compared subdermal contraceptive implants and levonorgestrel intrauterine systems (LNG-IUS) for managing endometriosis and other gynecological conditions in reproductive-aged women.
