Both methods are recommended for endometriosis treatment, with etonogestrel (ENG) implants and LNG-IUS systems effectively reducing endometriosis pain and improving menstrual symptoms through mechanisms including ovulation suppression, cervical mucus thickening, and endometrial atrophy. However, subdermal implants demonstrated significantly higher rates of adverse effects, including a 2.42-fold increased risk of user dissatisfaction, substantially elevated incidence of acne (OR 2.21) and weight gain (OR 4.63), and higher device removal rates due to side effects. In contrast, the LNG-IUS showed a more favorable side effect profile with lower discontinuation rates, though both methods can cause menstrual irregularities, mood changes, and reduced libido. Notably, subdermal implants showed reduced ovarian cyst detection in women without endometriosis or adenomyosis, suggesting differential clinical outcomes depending on underlying gynecological pathology.

title: Risk Factors of Primary Dysmenorrhea in Female Adolescent Basketball Players Related to Dietary, Hormonal, and Immuno-Metabolic Factors and Disordered Eating Attitudes
summary:
# Summary

This study identified multiple hormonal, metabolic, and dietary factors associated with primary dysmenorrhea in adolescent female athletes. Elevated prolactin and cortisol levels emerged as significant risk factors, with prolactin increasing dysmenorrhea risk 1.75-fold, suggesting a role for stress-related hormonal dysregulation in menstrual pain. The underlying pathophysiology involves increased intrauterine prostaglandin F2α and E2 synthesis through the arachidonic acid cascade, which enhance myometrial contractions and uterine vasoconstriction leading to ischemia and pain. Notably, adolescents with dysmenorrhea demonstrated disordered eating attitudes, lower overall energy and macronutrient intake, and diminished physical activity levels, while interventions including high-intensity interval training and probiotic supplementation showed promise in symptom reduction. The study also revealed that dysmenorrhea athletes had lower BMI, earlier menarche, and alterations in androgen metabolism, indicating that dysmenorrhea in this population involves complex interactions between endocrine dysfunction, nutritional insufficiency, and behavioral factors rather than a single causal mechanism.

title: High-Intensity Interval Training Enhances the Positive Effect of Pentoxifylline on Lipid Profile and Inflammatory Markers in an Endometriosis Animal Model
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is associated with cardiovascular disease risk through multiple interconnected mechanisms, including chronic inflammation, dyslipidemia, and elevated inflammatory markers such as high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP). This study demonstrated that combining high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with pentoxifylline (PTX) synergistically enhanced therapeutic effects in an endometriosis animal model, achieving greater reductions in endometriosis lesion volume compared to pentoxifylline alone. The combined treatment significantly improved cardiovascular risk factors by reducing hs-CRP, total cholesterol, and triglycerides while pentoxifylline alone reduced total cholesterol. Both exercise training and pentoxifylline independently modulate lipid profiles and inflammatory markers through distinct mechanisms—exercise upregulates high-density lipoprotein and lipoprotein lipase, while pentoxifylline suppresses inflammatory cytokines and improves vascular function through enhanced eNOS expression. The findings suggest that HIIT plus pentoxifylline represents an effective multi-modal approach for managing endometriosis-associated cardiovascular complications by simultaneously addressing lesion burden and systemic metabolic dysfunction.

title: Effects of a low-FODMAP diet on patients with endometriosis, a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study demonstrates that a low-FODMAP diet significantly improves symptoms and quality of life in endometriosis patients, particularly through reducing visceral hypersensitivity—a shared pathogenic mechanism underlying both endometriosis and irritable bowel syndrome. Endometriosis patients frequently experience gastrointestinal complications including chronic pelvic pain, constipation, bloating, and bowel symptoms comparable to IBS, which affects approximately three times more endometriosis patients than controls. Among participants who completed the low-FODMAP diet, 84% reported decreased bowel symptoms, 65% experienced reduced chronic pelvic pain, and 53% reported less bloating, with significant improvements in constipation scores (7.0 to 5.0, p=0.023). The diet improved multiple quality-of-life domains measured by the EHP-30 questionnaire, including pain control, emotional well-being, social support, self-image, work life, and sexual intercourse. The mechanism involves elimination of poorly absorbed carbohydrates to reduce intestinal water volume and colonic gas production, thereby alleviating visceral hypersensitivity. These findings suggest that dietary self-management empowers endometriosis patients to actively control symptoms and substantially enhance their overall quality of life.

title: Effect of comfort-oriented intervention combined with medical sponge positioning cushion on treatment compliance and psychological status of patients undergoing side-lying enema operation: A retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

Retention enema procedures cause significant physical discomfort and psychological distress including anxiety and depression due to their intimate nature, while inadequate patient understanding further decreases treatment compliance. This retrospective study demonstrates that comfort-oriented nursing interventions combined with medical sponge positioning cushions substantially improve treatment outcomes by addressing both physical and psychological barriers. The comfort group achieved a 93.33% compliance rate and 90.00% satisfaction rate, with notably higher proportions maintaining adequate drug retention time (≥2 hours) compared to conventional care. The intervention reduced anxiety (HAMA scores) and depression (HAMD scores) more effectively than standard care through multiple mechanisms including relaxation training, mindfulness meditation, environmental optimization with warm colors and soft lighting, and detailed procedural explanations that enhanced patient sense of control. Comfort-oriented care promoted active coping strategies while reducing avoidance and submission coping behaviors, fostering trust between patients and healthcare providers that led to greater active participation in treatment. These findings indicate that integrating comfort-focused nursing approaches with proper positioning support represents an effective strategy for enhancing both treatment efficacy and psychological well-being in patients undergoing retention enema procedures.

title: Non-pharmacological interventions involving traditional Chinese medicine for assisted reproductive technology: A group consensus
summary: This consensus article establishes clinical recommendations for integrating traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) non-pharmacological interventions throughout assisted reproductive technology (ART) treatment cycles to improve fertility outcomes and patient quality of life. Multiple TCM modalities—including manual acupuncture, electroacupuncture, transcutaneous electrical acupoint stimulation (TEAS), moxibustion, and auricular acupressure—demonstrate high-confidence evidence for improving ART success rates and pregnancy outcomes. These interventions target specific physiological processes at different cycle stages: promoting follicle growth during early stimulation, improving endometrial receptivity before embryo transfer, facilitating ovulation induction and pituitary downregulation, and reducing postoperative pain after oocyte retrieval. Beyond reproductive endpoints, TCM interventions also address psychological comorbidities associated with ART, including anxiety, depression, and emotional disorders through acupoint stimulation and five-elements music therapy. The consensus provides comprehensive guidance for implementing eight distinct non-pharmacological TCM approaches across seven ART cycle stages and three major complications, positioning these adjunctive therapies as evidence-supported options for enhancing both fertility treatment efficacy and patient well-being during assisted reproduction.

title: Pain management training for people with persistent pain and their informal carers (JOINT SUPPORT): multicentre randomised controlled feasibility trial with embedded qualitative study in English musculoskeletal services
summary:
# Summary

Persistent musculoskeletal pain, including conditions such as low back pain, fibromyalgia, and osteoarthritis, significantly impairs symptom management and quality of life in patients while imposing substantial physical and emotional burdens on informal carers. The JOINT SUPPORT intervention, a carer-inclusive pain management training program delivered through group-based sessions, telephone follow-up, and workbooks by physiotherapists and occupational therapists, demonstrated promising improvements across multiple outcomes including pain, fatigue, depression, and carer burden at three-month follow-up. The intervention components—encompassing education on pain understanding, pacing, graded activity, fear avoidance reduction, goal-setting, and medication management—were acceptable to both patient-carer dyads and health professionals without reported adverse events. While qualitative findings highlighted the value of shared experiences in fostering group cohesion and validation among participants, practical barriers such as inability to attend hospital appointments and work constraints limited trial participation. These results suggest that carer-inclusive pain management training shows feasibility and promise as an enhancement to usual care for persistent musculoskeletal pain management in English musculoskeletal services.

title: Factors influencing abortion duration, bleeding volume, pain scores, and anxiety levels during medical abortion: a cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study identified multiple factors influencing medical abortion outcomes, including pain, anxiety, duration, and bleeding. Key findings revealed that younger age and a history of dysmenorrhea predicted greater pain and anxiety during medical abortion, likely due to underlying endometrial inflammation, angiogenesis, and heightened uterine smooth muscle sensitivity associated with dysmenorrhea. Conversely, prior obstetric experience through vaginal delivery or cesarean section, as well as previous surgical abortion, significantly reduced pain and anxiety, suggesting that familiarity with reproductive procedures increases pain tolerance and psychological preparedness. Physical activity during medical abortion presented a paradoxical relationship: while increased walking and step rates shortened abortion duration by promoting drug absorption and tissue elimination, they simultaneously elevated pain scores through exercise-induced uterine spasms. The study highlights that mifepristone and misoprostol trigger the uterine contractions responsible for abortion, with common side effects including bleeding, severe cramping, headache, and nausea, and emphasizes that medical counseling and painkillers effectively mitigate pain and psychological distress during the procedure.

title: Co-developing a digital mindfulness- and acceptance-based intervention for endometriosis management and care: a qualitative feasibility study
summary:
# Summary: Digital Mindfulness- and Acceptance-Based Intervention for Endometriosis Management

Endometriosis is a chronic condition characterized by pelvic pain, dyspareunia, infertility, and fatigue that significantly impairs quality of life and increases risk of psychological distress including anxiety, depression, and stress. This qualitative feasibility study evaluated MY-ENDO, a digitalized mindfulness- and acceptance-based self-management intervention co-developed with patients to address both the physical and psychological burden of endometriosis. The intervention combines disease education with practical exercises including mindfulness practices and yoga, which participants reported reduced pelvic pain and muscle tension while improving sleep, reducing rumination, and promoting relaxation. Participants also reported that MY-ENDO improved endometriosis literacy, enhanced their sense of control over the condition, reduced social isolation, and shifted their mindset from feeling powerless to adopting acceptance-based coping strategies that enabled better disease management. The digital format improved accessibility and reduced stigma by allowing patients to access therapeutic content privately at any time. Patient co-development of the intervention and tailored focus on endometriosis enhanced acceptability and engagement, ultimately supporting improved quality of life beyond traditional medical treatments.

title: Surgical and oncological outcome after cytoreductive surgery and hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy for peritoneal mesothelioma
summary:
# Summary

Peritoneal mesothelioma comprises three histological subtypes with distinct clinical characteristics and treatment responses. Diffuse malignant peritoneal mesothelioma (DMPM) is associated with asbestos exposure and displays aggressive invasive growth with high mortality, while multicystic peritoneal mesothelioma (MCPM) and well-differentiated papillary peritoneal mesothelioma (WDPM) predominantly affect young women and are associated with abdominal inflammation and endometriosis rather than asbestos exposure. The standard treatment approach combines cytoreductive surgery with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) using cisplatin and doxorubicin, which reduces ascites production and pain while improving quality of life. For unresectable disease, immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, ipilimumab, pembrolizumab) and targeted therapies (atezolizumab combined with bevacizumab) demonstrate clinical benefit, with emerging evidence supporting pressurized intraperitoneal aerosol chemotherapy (PIPAC) achieving over 50% histological tumor regression and median overall survival of 26 months. Multiple prognostic factors including histological subtype, Ki67 status, lymph node involvement, extent of disease, and completeness of cytoreduction predict survival outcomes in DMPM patients.

title: Effect of transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation with 2% lignocaine in dental extraction
summary:
# Summary

This article demonstrates that transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) is a non-invasive therapeutic modality effective for managing pain across multiple conditions, including endometriosis, arthritis, fibromyalgia, and diabetic neuropathy. TENS works through multiple biological mechanisms: it induces endogenous endorphins that bind to opiate receptors to block pain signal transmission, stimulates production of dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin to enhance analgesia, and operates via gate control theory to inhibit pain transmission. In the specific context of dental extraction, TENS combined with 2% lignocaine significantly reduced injection-related anxiety and discomfort while improving hemostasis and wound healing through enhanced blood flow to treated areas. The analgesic effects of TENS are directly correlated with increased serotonin levels, and the sustained endogenous opioid release provides prolonged patient comfort extending beyond electrode removal. These findings establish TENS as an effective, non-invasive alternative for pain management that operates through well-characterized neurochemical pathways applicable to chronic pain conditions like endometriosis.

title: Management practices of primary dysmenorrhea among female high school students in Nekemte town, East Wallaga Zone, Western Oromia, Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea is caused by elevated prostaglandin levels that peak before menstruation begins, leading to menstrual cramps that significantly impact students' academic performance, school attendance, and daily activities. Both pharmacological and nonpharmacological interventions effectively reduce menstrual pain, with NSAIDs such as ibuprofen and diclofenac being the most commonly used medications, though ibuprofen demonstrated superior pain reduction efficacy. Nonpharmacological approaches including physical exercise, hot baths, massage, and herbal remedies like ginger tea provide additional symptom relief and are utilized by the majority of affected students. Primary dysmenorrhea frequently presents with associated symptoms including lower back pain, headaches, anxiety, and restlessness, with risk factors including irregular menstrual cycles and positive family history. The condition's substantial burden on work productivity and quality of life underscores the importance of accessible pain management strategies tailored to adolescent populations in resource-limited settings.

title: Exploration of Key Brain Regions Involved in Acupuncture and Moxibustion Analgesia: An Imaging-Based Study
summary: This imaging-based study elucidates the neurobiological mechanisms underlying acupuncture and moxibustion analgesia by mapping their differential effects on brain regions. Acupuncture predominantly activates sensory and motor cortices including the anterior cingulate cortex, insula, prefrontal cortex, and primary somatosensory cortex across frontal, parietal, and temporal lobes, while moxibustion preferentially engages limbic system structures, the thalamus, and regions involved in emotional regulation. Both modalities reduce pathological pain and treat various chronic pain conditions—including musculoskeletal and neuropathic pain—through activation of endogenous analgesic pathways that promote release of endorphins, serotonin, and endogenous opioid peptides while simultaneously inhibiting endogenous pain-causing substances. The study demonstrates that acupuncture and moxibustion regulate pain perception, inflammatory responses, and nervous system function through broad cortical and subcortical activation patterns involving structures from the brainstem and periaqueductal gray matter to the cerebellum and basal nuclei. These findings provide neuroimaging evidence supporting the clinical efficacy of these traditional approaches for managing chronic pain conditions associated with disability, anxiety, depression, and sleep disorders.

title: Vitamin D for primary dysmenorrhea and endometriosis-related pain – A systematic review of registered RCTs
summary: This systematic review of randomized controlled trials examined vitamin D's therapeutic potential for primary dysmenorrhea and endometriosis-related pain. Primary dysmenorrhea pathophysiology involves elevated prostaglandin production, which increases uterine contractions and restricts blood flow, leading to ischemia and pain; endometriosis develops through multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, immune dysfunction, genetic factors, and hormonal imbalances that result in uncontrolled endometrial tissue growth and chronic inflammation. Vitamin D supplementation demonstrated medium-to-high effectiveness in reducing pain severity, pain duration, and functional impairment in primary dysmenorrhea patients, with pooled data from nine trials showing significant pain reduction compared to controls. However, evidence for vitamin D's efficacy in endometriosis-related pain was substantially weaker, suggesting condition-specific differences in treatment responsiveness. Vitamin D's anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties, combined with its roles in hormonal regulation and immune surveillance, provide plausible biological mechanisms for pain symptom relief, though additional research is needed to clarify vitamin D's effects on prostaglandin regulation and to establish stronger evidence in endometriosis populations. Current standard treatments for these conditions include NSAIDs for primary dysmenorrhea and hormonal therapies or surgical intervention for endometriosis.

title: Impact of Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) guidelines implementation in deep infiltrating endometriosis surgery. A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) is associated with severe pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia, significantly impairing quality of life. This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocols effectively reduce postoperative length of hospital stay by approximately 2.88 days in DIE surgical patients without increasing readmission rates or complication rates at any severity level. ERAS protocols achieve these improvements through multimodal approaches including prehabilitation programs that alleviate preoperative distress and anxiety, and multimodal analgesia strategies tailored to DIE patients' lower postoperative pain thresholds and higher analgesic requirements. The implementation of ERAS protocols is associated with better overall postoperative outcomes while simultaneously reducing both direct and indirect healthcare costs associated with endometriosis surgery. These findings support the adoption of ERAS guidelines as a safe and effective standard for managing surgical care in DIE patients.

title: Impact of Relaxation and Music Intervention on Psychological and Gastrointestinal Health in Military Recruits: A Prospective Study
summary: This study demonstrates the bidirectional relationship between psychological distress and gastrointestinal dysfunction, mediated by the brain-gut axis and gut microbiota. Anxiety and depression are closely associated with gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal pain, dyspepsia, regurgitation, and constipation. Progressive muscular relaxation training combined with music intervention effectively reduced psychological symptoms, with depression disappearing in 56% of affected participants and anxiety resolving in 84% of those with baseline anxiety. The combined intervention also significantly improved gastrointestinal symptoms, reducing abdominal pain, dyspepsia, regurgitation, and constipation scores. These findings suggest that addressing the psychological components of gastrointestinal dysfunction through relaxation and music therapy provides a non-pharmacological approach to improving both mental health and digestive symptomatology. The results support the therapeutic potential of mind-body interventions targeting the brain-gut axis in populations experiencing stress-related psychological and gastrointestinal comorbidities.

title: Acute effects of virtual reality-based relaxation and exergaming on primary dysmenorrhea symptoms
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea is characterized by severe abdomino-pelvic pain during menstruation, often accompanied by bloating, headache, nausea, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. This study compared three interventions for managing dysmenorrhea symptoms: immersive virtual reality (VR) relaxation, nonimmersive VR exergaming, and traditional Jacobson's relaxation. All three interventions significantly reduced abdomino-pelvic pain intensity and menstrual symptom severity, with reductions in pain scores ranging from 0.78–0.85 and symptom severity from 0.73–0.88. However, Jacobson's relaxation proved most effective overall, with participants reporting superior symptom relief and greater perceived effectiveness compared to both VR-based approaches. While immersive VR relaxation offered promise through enhanced distraction effects and exergaming provided motivation through engaging entertainment, practical limitations including cybersickness in immersive VR and exercise-induced discomfort in exergaming reduced their real-world utility. The findings suggest that VR-based therapies show potential as alternatives for dysmenorrhea management, though traditional relaxation techniques currently remain the preferred non-pharmacological intervention.

title: Evaluation of health-related quality of life of female students suffering from primary dysmenorrhea: findings of a cross-sectional study from Pakistan
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea is a prevalent menstrual condition characterized by spasmodic lower abdominal cramps accompanied by systemic symptoms including headache, vomiting, nervousness, mood swings, and breast tenderness. The underlying pathophysiology involves increased prostaglandin production and progesterone dysregulation, which enhance myometrial contractility, leading to uterine ischemia and decreased pain thresholds. This cross-sectional study of Pakistani female students demonstrated that primary dysmenorrhea significantly impairs health-related quality of life by reducing academic performance, work performance, mobility, self-care capacity, and usual activities while elevating anxiety and depression symptoms. Several factors including family history, menstrual cycle characteristics (regularity, bleeding duration, cycle length), symptom duration, and seasonal variation correlated with reduced quality-of-life scores. Treatment approaches employed by participants included pharmacological interventions (NSAIDs, acetaminophen, hormonal contraceptives) and lifestyle modifications (heat application, exercise, massage, meditation), with heat application being the most widely adopted strategy among students. These findings emphasize that primary dysmenorrhea warrants medical attention as an important public health concern affecting female students' overall functioning and psychosocial wellbeing.

title: Electroacupuncture for recovery of gastrointestinal function after laparoscopic hysterectomy surgery: a prospective, randomized, controlled trial
summary: This prospective randomized controlled trial demonstrates that electroacupuncture significantly accelerates gastrointestinal recovery following laparoscopic hysterectomy, a common surgical intervention for endometriosis management. Electroacupuncture improved gastrointestinal peristalsis and reduced the incidence of postoperative ileus, with patients in the intervention group experiencing first flatus approximately 5.8 hours earlier (36.4 versus 42.2 hours) and first defecation approximately 5.3 hours earlier (46.0 versus 51.3 hours) compared to controls. Beyond gastrointestinal outcomes, electroacupuncture also reduced postoperative pain and nausea scores, addressing multiple dimensions of postoperative morbidity. These findings suggest electroacupuncture represents a feasible non-pharmacological approach to enhance postoperative recovery and quality of life in patients undergoing surgical treatment for endometriosis, potentially reducing hospitalization duration and complications associated with prolonged ileus.

title: Perception of university students about the use of painkillers, other remedies and lifestyle modifications for primary dysmenorrhea; a cross-sectional study at KEMU
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea, characterized by excessive cramping pain caused by increased uterine prostaglandin levels following progesterone decline after ovulation, significantly impacts university students' daily functioning. The pathophysiology involves elevated prostaglandins triggering uterine contractions that restrict blood flow and generate anaerobic metabolites, which first-line pharmacological treatments like NSAIDs and oral contraceptives effectively target by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis or suppressing ovulation, respectively. Among surveyed students, 46% used painkillers (predominantly paracetamol at 65%), while complementary and alternative therapies—particularly bed rest (68%), hot compresses (53.6%), and natural supplements like green tea (29.5%)—were more frequently adopted, with utilization increasing significantly with dysmenorrhea severity. Beyond conventional medications, herbal remedies (ginger, chamomile, fennel) and supplements (Vitamin B1, magnesium, fish oil) show modest evidence for pain relief, while non-pharmacological approaches including heat application, exercise, acupuncture, and massage provide additional options. Student choice of complementary therapies was primarily motivated by desire to reduce analgesic dependence (58%), perceived safety (38.3%), and availability, though 84% acknowledged concerns about long-term NSAID use causing gastrointestinal and renal complications.

title: Traditional Chinese Medicine Herbs for Breast Cancer Prevention and Survival: A narrative review of epidemiological studies from Taiwan
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review of epidemiological studies from Taiwan demonstrates that traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) herbal products are associated with significant protective effects against breast cancer and improved survival outcomes. Multiple studies consistently show that TCM herbal use reduces breast cancer risk and that adjunctive TCM therapy alongside conventional medicine reduces both mortality rates and all-cause mortality compared to conventional medicine alone. At the mechanistic level, preclinical research indicates that Chinese herbs possess anti-cancer properties through tumor growth inhibition, induction of apoptosis, suppression of angiogenesis, and enhancement of immune function. Specific herbal formulations—including Si Wu Tang (containing Shu Di Huang, Dang Gui, Bai Shao, and Chuang Xiong), the Di Huang Wan Series, and San-Huang-Xie-Xin-Tang—show particularly strong associations with reduced breast cancer risk and mortality. Beyond cancer prevention, TCM herbs ameliorate treatment-related side effects including fatigue, nausea, and vomiting, while Angelica sinensis Radix demonstrates a dose-dependent protective effect and alleviates menopausal symptoms such as hot flashes and mood disturbances. These findings suggest that TCM may serve as a valuable adjunctive therapy for breast cancer prevention and management in conjunction with conventional treatments.

title: Safety and efficacy of cryoablation of soft-tissue tumours: a systematic review
summary: This systematic review evaluates percutaneous cryoablation as a treatment for soft-tissue tumors including desmoid tumors, vascular malformations, and abdominal wall endometriosis. Across multiple studies, cryoablation demonstrated substantial efficacy in reducing pain associated with these conditions, with average pain reductions of 79% for desmoid tumors, 72% for vascular malformations, and 82% for abdominal wall endometriosis. The procedure achieved high technical success rates, particularly for vascular malformations at 100%. Notably, cryoablation exhibited a favorable safety profile, with only minor adverse events occurring in approximately 20% of patients and major adverse events remaining rare. These findings suggest that percutaneous cryoablation is both an effective and safe therapeutic option for managing pain and lesion volume in soft-tissue conditions, including endometriosis affecting the abdominal wall.

title: Translation and Validation of the Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP) Scale in Indonesian Version
summary: This study validated the Indonesian translation of the Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP) scale, a standardized instrument for assessing premenstrual syndrome (PMS) and primary dysmenorrhea severity. The validation demonstrated high reliability (Cronbach's alpha of 0.90) and construct validity comparable to international versions from countries including Japan, Brazil, China, and Germany, with individual scale factors ranging from 0.72 to 0.86 in internal consistency. PMS encompasses diverse affective, behavioral, and physical symptoms—including abdominal pain, headaches, breast soreness, mood swings, and concentration difficulties—that significantly impact women's quality of life, work productivity, and interpersonal relationships. The pathophysiology of PMS involves hormonal factors (progesterone and estrogen dysregulation) and neurotransmitter abnormalities (serotonin), while psychosocial stressors can exacerbate symptoms. Effective management strategies include lifestyle modifications (exercise, nutritious diet, stress management), cognitive-behavioral therapy, hormonal contraceptives, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, with emerging evidence supporting nursing interventions such as relaxation techniques and psychosocial support. The validated Indonesian DRSP provides clinicians with a reliable screening tool for identifying menstrual symptom severity in Indonesian-speaking populations.

title: Clinical Trial: Effect of a 28‐Day Low FODMAP Diet on Gastrointestinal Symptoms Associated With Endometriosis (EndoFOD)—A Randomised, Controlled Crossover Feeding Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition that commonly causes gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal pain, bloating, diarrhea, and dyschezia, which significantly impair quality of life and are often misdiagnosed as irritable bowel syndrome. This randomized controlled crossover trial demonstrated that a 28-day low FODMAP diet substantially reduced gastrointestinal symptoms in endometriosis patients, with 60% responding to the dietary intervention compared to 26% on a control diet, likely through reductions in osmotic load and colonic transit time. Beyond gastrointestinal benefits, the low FODMAP diet improved stool consistency, reduced abdominal pain and bloating, and enhanced both gastrointestinal-specific and endometriosis-related quality of life measures, with improvements appearing by week two. The diet also showed secondary benefits on psychological symptoms, reducing stress, depression, and anxiety in patients with abnormal baseline scores, and participants reported improved physical function and reduced impact of pain on daily activities. These findings provide the first high-quality evidence supporting dietary FODMAP restriction as an effective non-pharmacological treatment strategy for managing the substantial gastrointestinal burden of endometriosis.

title: Acupuncture for Anxiety, Depression, and Sleep in Veterans with Combat-Related Posttraumatic Stress Disorder: A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial evaluated acupuncture's efficacy for treating anxiety, depression, and insomnia in veterans with combat-related posttraumatic stress disorder. Verum (true) acupuncture demonstrated large effect sizes in reducing anxiety and depression symptoms, with moderate effects on sleep quality, while sham acupuncture also produced significant improvements with somewhat smaller effect magnitudes, indicating a substantial placebo component to the treatment response. Both treatment modalities showed superior retention and satisfaction rates compared to standard approaches, with minimal adverse events and low withdrawal rates, establishing acupuncture's safety profile when administered by competent practitioners. The findings suggest that acupuncture may function as a safe, effective, and patient-acceptable treatment option for the constellation of anxiety, depression, and insomnia symptoms commonly comorbid with PTSD, potentially offering clinicians an alternative or complementary approach to standard pharmacological or psychotherapeutic interventions. The strong sham response highlights the importance of expectation, therapeutic context, and clinician-patient interactions in mediating acupuncture's therapeutic benefits, though verum treatment consistently produced superior outcomes across all measured symptom domains.

title: Chronic pelvic pain and botulinum toxin
journal: Toxicon : official journal of the International Society on Toxinology
summary: Botulinum toxin is emerging as a promising treatment for chronic pelvic pain, a major source of suffering and disability affecting both men and women. The toxin works through multiple mechanisms, including inhibition of acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction, which reduces muscle contraction and spasm, and suppression of pain-related neurotransmitters such as substance P, calcitonin gene-related peptide, and glutamate. Clinical evidence demonstrates that botulinum toxin effectively treats various pelvic pain conditions including vaginismus, vestibulitis, vulvar pain, and chronic prostatitis, with effects beginning within 1–2 weeks and peaking at four weeks post-injection. Beyond pain relief, botulinum toxin treatment improves sexual function, reduces intravaginal pressure, and enhances quality of life, with particular benefit for non-menstrual pain and pain during intercourse. However, the therapy is associated with significant adverse effects including urinary and fecal incontinence, urinary retention, constipation, and vaginal dryness. The article contextualizes botulinum toxin within the broader management of endometriosis-related chronic pelvic pain, where standard treatments include surgical lesion removal and hormonal suppression of the menstrual cycle.

title: Dorsal Root Ganglion Stimulation in Patients With Refractory Abdominal Pain: A Case Series
summary: Dorsal root ganglion (DRG) stimulation is a neuromodulatory therapy that effectively treats refractory chronic abdominal pain by targeting sensory neurons in the DRG to modulate nociceptive signals from peripheral organs. This case series documented three patients with chronic abdominal pain from endometriosis, pancreatitis, and pancreatic cancer who achieved substantial pain reductions of 80-100% following DRG stimulation, along with significant improvements in daily functioning and reduced opioid dependency. DRG stimulation offers distinct advantages over alternative interventions such as spinal cord stimulation, celiac plexus blocks, and intrathecal drug delivery, providing more precise targeting and more durable pain relief without requiring frequent repeat procedures. Chronic abdominal pain associated with these conditions is often compounded by psychosocial factors including anxiety and depression, which can impact treatment response and overall outcomes. The integration of psychological support alongside DRG neuromodulation therapy may further enhance treatment efficacy and optimize patient outcomes in this challenging patient population.

title: Association between MIND diet its components with endometriosis risk: secondary results from a case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study demonstrates a substantial protective association between adherence to the MIND diet and endometriosis risk, with each unit increase in MIND diet score corresponding to a 47% lower odds of disease and high adherence associated with an 83% risk reduction. The MIND diet's protective effects are mediated through its anti-inflammatory properties and strategic composition of nutrient-rich foods including green leafy vegetables, other vegetables, nuts, legumes, berries, fish, whole grains, and olive oil, all of which individually showed reduced endometriosis odds when consumed at highest levels. Conversely, high intake of pro-inflammatory foods such as red meat and butter-margarine products was associated with substantially elevated endometriosis risk, likely through mechanisms involving increased circulating estrogen levels that stimulate ectopic endometrial tissue proliferation. The study identified established endometriosis risk factors including early menarche, shortened menstrual cycle duration, and nulliparity, while also demonstrating that dietary factors like reduced fiber and increased dietary fat elevate serum estrogen concentrations, potentially enhancing disease susceptibility. These findings suggest that dietary modification through MIND diet adherence represents a viable dietary intervention strategy for endometriosis symptom management and risk reduction, potentially operating through modulation of estrogen metabolism and systemic inflammation.

title: Clinical Delivery of Whole Systems Traditional Chinese Medicine and Impacts Upon Patient Reported Outcomes During IVF
journal: Global Advances in Integrative Medicine and Health
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how whole systems traditional Chinese medicine (WS-TCM), including acupuncture and complementary techniques such as ear seeds, electro-acupuncture, and cupping, affects patient outcomes in women undergoing IVF. The research documented that acupuncture is associated with higher rates of clinical pregnancy and live births, while also effectively reducing anxiety, depression, and postoperative pain. Beyond reproductive outcomes, WS-TCM substantially improved patient-reported quality of life by reducing pain, stress, and anxiety—addressing the significant psychosocial burden of infertility, with nearly 27% of patients reporting anxiety disorder diagnoses. Given the established link between psychological stress and impaired fertility, the study supports WS-TCM as a useful integrative treatment strategy that simultaneously targets both physiological reproductive function and the emotional distress commonly accompanying infertility care and endometriosis-related pain.

title: Management of metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD)—An expert consensus statement from Indian diabetologists' perspective
summary:
# Summary

This expert consensus statement addresses the management of metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) in the Indian population, emphasizing the central roles of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and insulin resistance in disease pathogenesis. The document identifies multiple risk factors including hypothyroidism, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and lifestyle factors such as physical inactivity and high dietary intake of saturated fats and fructose, while highlighting protective interventions including weight loss, Mediterranean diet adoption, and regular aerobic and resistance exercise. Pharmacological management is reviewed across several drug classes, with Saroglitazar and Resmetirom approved in India for MASLD treatment, while GLP-1 receptor agonists, SGLT2 inhibitors, and metformin represent additional therapeutic options for improving hepatic steatosis and fibrosis. Diagnostic approaches incorporate non-invasive elastography techniques (VCTE and ARFI imaging) and fibrosis prediction scores (FIB-4, NAFLD Fibrosis Score, APRI), enabling early detection and risk stratification in diabetic patients at heightened MASLD risk. The consensus underscores that thyroid hormones regulate hepatic lipogenesis and fatty acid oxidation while cardiovascular risk management with statins remains essential, reflecting MASLD's systemic metabolic implications.

title: Pudendal Nerve Irritation as Unique Symptom of Pelvic Congestion Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic congestion syndrome (PCS) is an underdiagnosed cause of chronic pelvic pain affecting 10–30% of women of reproductive age, characterized by venous insufficiency and dilation of ovarian and pelvic veins that lead to local tissue congestion and venous stasis. This case report highlights a rare neurological manifestation of PCS in which dilated ovarian, parauterine, and paravaginal veins caused direct compression of the pudendal and femoral nerves, resulting in localized right iliac fossa pain that worsened during menstruation and prolonged standing. Advanced imaging with magnetic neurography and venography proved crucial for identifying the anatomical relationship between venous engorgement extending into presacral and paravertebral regions and perineural irritation at the L5 nerve root level. Bilateral ovarian vein embolization using Ruby coils combined with adjunct sclerotherapy successfully decompressed the perineural venous plexus and achieved complete symptom resolution, with sustained improvement at one-year follow-up and subsequent successful pregnancy. This case underscores the importance of recognizing neurological complications in PCS through advanced neuroimaging and the effectiveness of minimally invasive endovascular treatment, emphasizing that early recognition and a multidisciplinary approach involving vascular surgeons, neuroradiologists, and neurologists are essential to prevent chronic nerve injury and optimize patient outcomes.

title: Integrating Superior Hypogastric Plexus Block with Conventional Therapies: Advancements in Chronic Pelvic Pain Management
summary: Superior hypogastric plexus block (SHPB) has emerged as a promising adjunctive intervention for managing chronic pelvic pain, including pain associated with endometriosis, by disrupting pain signal transmission from the pelvic viscera using local anesthetics such as bupivacaine and lidocaine. Clinical evidence demonstrates that SHPB treatment, enhanced by advanced imaging modalities including fluoroscopy and ultrasound, achieves substantial pain reduction across multiple pelvic pain conditions—achieving 70% pain reduction in cancer-related pelvic pain and significant relief in five of six endometriosis patients studied. Integration of SHPB with conventional therapies such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, opioids, antidepressants, physical therapy, and cognitive-behavioral therapy optimizes therapeutic outcomes while reducing opioid consumption and improving overall quality of life. Chronic pelvic pain, affecting 15–20% of women globally, is frequently associated with comorbid anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbances that exacerbate symptoms, necessitating multimodal treatment approaches. The selection of SHPB as a treatment option depends on factors including the underlying etiology and severity of the pain condition, with evidence suggesting comparable efficacy to alternative nerve block procedures such as impar ganglion block.

title: Adjunctive Efficacy of Superior Hypogastric Plexus Block in Chronic Pelvic Pain: A Comprehensive Review
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pelvic pain, frequently associated with endometriosis and other conditions such as interstitial cystitis and pelvic inflammatory disease, arises from chronic inflammation, neuropathic mechanisms, and central sensitization. The superior hypogastric plexus, located at the L5-S1 vertebral level, transmits nociceptive signals from pelvic organs including the uterus, bladder, and rectum via sympathetic nerve fibers. Superior hypogastric plexus block (SHPB) is an effective interventional technique that interrupts these pain pathways, providing significant relief of chronic pelvic pain and associated symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dysuria while improving overall quality of life. Patient factors predicting favorable SHPB response include younger age, shorter pain duration, lower baseline pain intensity, and lower levels of anxiety and depression, which frequently accompany chronic pelvic pain. SHPB demonstrates clinical efficacy comparable to alternative interventions such as ganglion impar block and sacral nerve block, and can reduce postoperative opioid consumption when used preemptively. This review establishes SHPB as a valuable adjunctive treatment option for endometriosis-related and other chronic pelvic pain conditions, offering a minimally invasive alternative to traditional surgical and pharmacological approaches.

title: Use of Digital and Telemedicine Tools for Postoperative Pain Management at Home: A Scoping Review of Health Professionals’ Roles and Clinical Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This scoping review demonstrates that digital tools and telemedicine interventions significantly improve postoperative pain management in home-based settings across multiple technology platforms. Mobile applications, web-based systems, wireless sensors, remote messaging platforms, and virtual reality technologies consistently reduce postoperative pain and opioid consumption while decreasing unscheduled emergency visits and hospital readmissions. Beyond pain control, these digital interventions enhance functional recovery, improve sleep quality, boost patient satisfaction, and strengthen therapeutic adherence through real-time communication and active symptom monitoring. Implementation success depends critically on healthcare professional involvement and adequate digital literacy among patients, as barriers including limited internet connectivity and low technology adoption in elderly populations remain key challenges to widespread clinical adoption.

title: The impact of mind-body internet and mobile-based interventions on fatigue in adults living with chronic physical conditions: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that mind-body internet and mobile-based interventions (IMIs) significantly reduce fatigue in adults with chronic physical conditions, achieving a moderate-to-large effect size (SMD = -0.74). Fatigue is a prevalent and debilitating symptom across multiple chronic conditions—including cancer, multiple sclerosis, fibromyalgia, and neurological disorders—that substantially impacts physical, emotional, and psychological quality of life. The interventions most commonly employed cognitive behavioral therapy either alone or combined with complementary techniques such as yoga, mindfulness, meditation, tai chi, and progressive muscle relaxation, with CBT-based interventions showing slightly larger effects (SMD = -0.89) than non-CBT approaches. Notably, younger participants experienced greater fatigue reduction from mind-body IMIs, and both self-guided and personnel-supported delivery formats proved effective, with particularly robust effects observed in the neurological disease subgroup (SMD = -1.19). These findings establish mind-body interventions as evidence-based, non-pharmacological options for managing fatigue as a transdiagnostic symptom across diverse chronic conditions.

title: Mobile applications available in Saudi Arabia for the management of Primary Dysmenorrhea: A quality review and content analysis
summary: Primary dysmenorrhea, characterized by menstrual cramps and suprapubic pain without underlying pelvic pathology, affects women predominantly below age 25 and is managed through both pharmaceutical and non-pharmaceutical approaches. NSAIDs such as ibuprofen and hormonal contraceptives effectively treat the condition, though they carry side effects including stomach ulcers and weight gain. Exercise-based interventions, including both aerobic activities like jogging and walking and stretching-based approaches such as yoga, significantly reduce menstrual pain, while complementary physical therapy methods including lavender oil massage, progressive relaxation, and Kegel exercises also improve symptoms. Mobile menstrual tracking applications available in Saudi Arabia increasingly incorporate comprehensive features for dysmenorrhea management, including mood tracking, mindfulness exercises, and exercise recommendations based on the FITT principle, with machine learning and artificial intelligence optimizing period predictions and symptom analysis. These applications further support users with conditions like endometriosis and PCOS, and increasingly integrate telemedicine capabilities for virtual consultation with healthcare specialists. This systematic review of 16 Saudi Arabian menstrual tracking apps found that 87.5% require subscription, indicating the growing commercialization of digital tools for menstrual health management.

title: Integration of a Lifestyle Modification Intervention for Women with Overweight and Obesity in a Gynecologic Oncology Practice
summary: Obesity substantially increases the risk of endometrial cancer, with affected women experiencing 4- to 10-fold greater cancer incidence and facing complications including poor surgical outcomes and increased non-cancer mortality. A remotely delivered, group-based lifestyle modification intervention proved feasible and effective for gynecologic cancer patients with overweight and obesity, achieving mean weight loss of 4.19 kg with 40% of participants losing at least 5% of initial body weight. Beyond weight reduction, the intervention improved multiple quality-of-life measures including fatigue, physical functioning, and overall health outcomes. Remote delivery eliminated critical access barriers such as travel time constraints and rural-urban inequalities, while the group-based format enhanced retention compared to individual programs. These findings demonstrate that integrating structured lifestyle modifications into gynecologic oncology practice represents a practical, accessible strategy to reduce obesity-related cancer risk and improve health outcomes in cancer survivors.

title: A Scoping Review of Interdisciplinary Care Programs for Women With Persistent Pelvic Pain
summary:
# Summary

Persistent pelvic pain represents a complex, multifactorial condition strongly associated with endometriosis, adenomyosis, vulvodynia, and painful bladder syndrome, alongside significant comorbidities including irritable bowel syndrome, fibromyalgia, migraines, anxiety, and depression. This scoping review of interdisciplinary care programs identifies comprehensive treatment approaches spanning physiotherapy (manual therapy, pelvic floor rehabilitation, exercise management), psychological interventions (cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based approaches, acceptance and commitment therapy), biomedical treatments (endometriosis reduction surgery, peripheral nerve blocks, pharmacotherapy), and patient education on pain science and pelvic floor anatomy. Sexual and relationship therapy, alongside pain coping strategy development, address the profound impacts of pelvic pain on work capacity, intimate relationships, and sexual function. The evidence supports multimodal, interdisciplinary treatment models incorporating assessment, education, physiotherapy, and psychological management as core program elements, with dietary interventions such as low FODMAP diets offering additional symptom management for comorbid conditions like irritable bowel syndrome.

title: Research Methodology in Acupuncture for Managing Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome: A Scoping Review
summary: This scoping review examines acupuncture research methodologies for managing interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS), a chronic condition characterized by pelvic pain, bladder pressure, and associated symptoms including nocturia, sleep disorders, and sexual dysfunction that disproportionately affects women. Acupuncture reduces IC/BPS symptoms through multiple proposed mechanisms: needle stimulation modulates endorphin and enkephalin levels, induces local adenosine release to inhibit pain signals, deactivates pain-processing brain regions including the limbic system and default mode network, and triggers vascular and immune responses leading to inflammatory mediator release. The reviewed studies predominantly employed electroacupuncture, classical acupuncture, and moxibustion approaches, with consistent use of specific acupoints (BL23, SP6, BL35, and SP9) in treatment protocols. Primary outcome measures across studies focused on pain assessment (73.33%), quality of life and symptom burden (46.67%), urinary frequency and bladder capacity (33-40%), and nocturia, urgency, and frequency (20%). Beyond IC/BPS, acupuncture demonstrates similar efficacy for related chronic pelvic pain conditions including endometriosis, overactive bladder, and prostatitis, with an excellent safety profile showing minimal adverse events despite millions of treatment sessions. These findings support acupuncture as a evidence-based complementary therapy for IC/BPS management, though the review underscores the need for standardized research methodologies to strengthen the evidence base.

title: Herbal decoctions for dysmenorrhea under universal health coverage pilot project: Evidence from a nationwide claims database in the Republic of Korea
summary: This nationwide claims database study demonstrates that customized herbal decoctions significantly reduce dysmenorrhea symptom severity in both primary and secondary cases, with median severity scores declining substantially over treatment. The pathophysiology of primary dysmenorrhea involves elevated prostaglandin F2α and E2 levels that trigger uterine hypercontraction, leading to ischemia and hypoxia—mechanisms that herbal treatments appear to counteract, offering an alternative to conventional NSAIDs and hormonal contraceptives. Secondary dysmenorrhea patients, particularly those with endometriosis-related pain, presented with more severe baseline symptoms but still benefited from herbal intervention. The study identified four traditional Korean medicine patterns associated with dysmenorrhea, with Qi stagnation and blood stasis being most prevalent, suggesting pattern-specific herbal formulations enhance treatment efficacy. While adverse events were rare (0.58% of patients), predominantly mild gastrointestinal symptoms, the integration of herbal decoctions into universal health coverage significantly increased treatment accessibility and utilization among dysmenorrhea patients. These findings support herbal medicine as an effective, accessible treatment option for managing menstrual pain and reducing associated productivity losses, though treatment effectiveness modestly decreased with advancing age.

title: Efficacy and safety of drug combinations for chronic pelvic pain: a systematic review
summary: This systematic review evaluates the efficacy of combination drug therapies compared to monotherapy for treating chronic pelvic pain (CPP), a prevalent and multifaceted condition affecting predominantly women and causing significant emotional, physical, and socioeconomic burden through increased healthcare utilization. The review examined nine studies involving 1,299 participants testing various pharmacological interventions including antibiotics (ciprofloxacin), alpha-blockers (tamsulosin), intravesical agents (pentosan polysulfate, hyaluronic acid, chondroitin), antihistamines (hydroxyzine), hemostatic agents (troxerutin, carbazochrome), and hormone modulators (linzagolix). Despite the availability of numerous treatment options, the key finding is that combination therapy did not demonstrate significantly superior pain reduction compared to monotherapy, with monotherapy alone proving inadequate for universal symptom relief. Notable exceptions include the combination of troxerutin and carbazochrome, which effectively reduced anal discomfort and defecation-related pain, and linzagolix with add-back therapy, which achieved 72.9% response rates for dysmenorrhea and 47.3% for nonmenstrual pelvic pain. Additionally, certain combination approaches targeting endometriosis-associated CPP, such as N-acetylcysteine with oral contraceptives, showed promise, and intravesical pentosan polysulfate combined with oral therapy improved health-related quality of life. The findings suggest that while combination therapy offers potential benefits in specific clinical contexts, the optimization of pelvic pain management remains complex and likely requires individualized treatment approaches.

title: Effectiveness and Safety of Korean Medicine in Treating Female Infertility: A Prospective Multicenter Observational Study
summary: This prospective multicenter study evaluated Korean medicine (KM) interventions for female infertility, demonstrating that acupuncture and herbal medicine—particularly the Jogyeongjongok-tang decoction—were the most frequently employed treatments, with additional modalities including moxibustion, cupping, and pharmacopuncture used in combination protocols. Among the cohort, unexplained infertility (59.4%) was the predominant diagnosis, while kidney deficiency emerged as the most common pattern identification according to traditional Korean medicine diagnostics, followed by blood deficiency and liver depression types. Korean medicine treatment resulted in clinical pregnancy rates of 20.8% overall, with higher rates of 24.2% in the combined KM-conventional medicine group, alongside significant improvements in ongoing pregnancy and live birth rates, with thirteen successful deliveries recorded. Both acupuncture and herbal medicine demonstrated statistically significant reductions in dysmenorrhea and premenstrual syndrome severity, suggesting benefits beyond fertility enhancement. The herbal formulations incorporated key ingredients including Angelica sinensis, Paeoniae Radix, Poria Sclerotium, and Citri Unshius Pericarpium, with specific formulas like Guibi-tang and Yuklinzu prescribed frequently across participants. Safety was well-established, with no serious adverse events reported and only minor adverse effects noted in individual cases, supporting the use of Korean medicine as an adjunctive or primary treatment modality for female infertility.

title: Enhancing Military Women's Health and Readiness Through Targeted Research Initiatives
summary:
# Summary

This research initiative addresses interconnected health challenges affecting military women, with particular focus on chronic pelvic pain and post-traumatic stress disorder. The studies establish that hip and pelvic injuries, combined with sexual trauma exposure, are significant contributors to chronic pelvic pain in female service members, while physical therapy treatments demonstrate effectiveness in managing this condition. Additionally, research reveals a strong association between trauma exposure and alterations in both gut microbiome composition and neuronal activity, suggesting mechanistic links between psychological and physiological health outcomes. A promising therapeutic approach involves developing synbiotic formulations containing prebiotics and probiotics that may reduce PTSD symptoms through microbiome-targeted interventions. The research program aims to generate foundational evidence supporting microbiome-based therapeutics as novel treatment strategies for PTSD in military populations. These integrated findings underscore the importance of multidisciplinary approaches addressing both musculoskeletal and microbiota-mediated pathways to improve health and operational readiness in female service members.

title: Uncomfortable Paresthesia and Dysesthesia Following Tonic Spinal Cord Stimulator Implantation
summary: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) effectively reduces chronic pain in conditions such as complex regional pain syndrome and persistent spinal pain syndrome by activating touch and vibration fibers that interrupt ascending pain signals at the spinal cord level, consistent with gate control theory. While tonic SCS stimulation produces significant pain relief—reducing mean pain scores from 8.05 to 3.6 over 24 months—it causes uncomfortable paresthesia and dysesthesia in a notable proportion of patients, leading to device explantation and considerable morbidity. Current tobacco use emerged as a highly significant risk factor, increasing the likelihood of paresthesia development and complications 7.2-fold; chronic nicotine exposure dysregulates multiple neurotransmitter systems (opioid, cholinergic, serotonergic, noradrenergic, and dopaminergic) and smoking-derived oxidative stress compounds like acrolein upregulate pain receptors and induce hyperalgesia. High-frequency and burst stimulation techniques substantially reduce paresthesia incidence compared to traditional tonic stimulation, offering improved tolerability profiles. These findings underscore the importance of stimulation modality selection and smoking cessation counseling in optimizing SCS outcomes and minimizing neurological complications requiring revision surgery or device removal.

title: Multimodal Approaches in the Management of Temporomandibular Disorders: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Temporomandibular disorders (TMDs) are the most common cause of non-dental orofacial pain and involve complex interactions between the nervous system, muscular system, and cervical spine, with associated symptoms including headaches, restricted joint mobility, muscle pain, and joint inflammation. Multimodal treatment approaches combining physiotherapy, pharmacological interventions, and psychological support have demonstrated superior efficacy in reducing TMD symptoms compared to single-modality treatments. Specific physiotherapeutic techniques—including manual therapy (Maitland mobilization, rhythmic mobilization), therapeutic exercises, dry needling, myofascial release, and post-isometric relaxation—effectively reduce pain intensity and improve jaw function and mandibular opening. Emerging evidence supports integrating manual therapy targeting both the upper cervical spine and TMJ, along with motor control training for neck muscles, reflecting the neuroanatomical convergence of trigeminal and cervical nociceptive inputs. Biopsychosocial factors including stress, anxiety, and depression significantly contribute to TMD pathogenesis, with hormonal factors like elevated estrogen influencing pain modulation; accordingly, comprehensive management should incorporate psychological interventions such as counseling, yoga-based programs, and pain science education. The evidence demonstrates that combined manual and physical therapy integrated with patient education and psychological support provides the most effective management strategy for TMDs.

title: Comparative efficacy of pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions on pain intensity of primary dysmenorrhoea: protocol for systematic review and network meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhoea is a highly prevalent menstrual pain condition characterized by cyclic pelvic pain often accompanied by nausea, back pain, fatigue, and psychological symptoms including anxiety and depression, with significant impacts on quality of life and both physical and mental health. The underlying pathophysiology involves excessive prostaglandin synthesis and release, which triggers uterine hypercontraction, tissue ischemia, hypoxia, and inflammation. Pharmacological treatment options include non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and acetaminophen, which work by inhibiting prostaglandin production, alongside hormonal contraceptives that suppress ovulation. Complementary to pharmaceutical approaches, diverse non-pharmacological interventions have demonstrated efficacy, including physical activities (aerobic exercise, resistance training, stretching), thermotherapy, acupuncture-based treatments, transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, behavioral interventions (biofeedback, relaxation, hypnotherapy), dietary supplements (vitamins, ginger, fennel, zinc), and manual therapies. This protocol aims to conduct a systematic review and network meta-analysis to comparatively assess the relative effectiveness of these pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions on pain intensity reduction and quality of life improvement in primary dysmenorrhoea.

title: Feasibility trial of a self-help digital intervention for functional cognitive disorder
summary: Functional cognitive disorder (FCD) is a condition characterized by memory dysfunction and cognitive complaints that arise from abnormal attention patterns, high memory expectations, and poor metacognition, leading to significant distress, disability, and work/social impairment. FCD frequently co-occurs with depression, anxiety, sleep problems, fibromyalgia, and chronic fatigue syndrome, with patients experiencing symptoms such as mind-blanking, dissociative episodes, and sleep disturbances. Mementum, a novel digital self-help intervention based on cognitive behavioral therapy principles including cognitive restructuring, education about attention mechanisms, and stress-reduction techniques, demonstrated significant improvements in illness perceptions, depressive symptoms, quality of life, and cognitive symptoms in a feasibility trial with 62% of participants reporting cognitive improvement. The intervention produced notable reductions in brief illness perception scores and depressive severity while promoting patient empowerment and confidence in diagnosis, though improvements in objective memory function as measured by standardized questionnaires were not statistically significant. Overall satisfaction with Mementum was high at 62%, with borderline significant improvements in work and social functioning observed across participants. These findings suggest that digital self-help interventions targeting cognitive and illness perceptions may offer accessible treatment for FCD, though future research should address individual variability in response and optimize personalization for improved outcomes.

title: Effectiveness of digital healthcare in managing menstrual symptoms: a systematic review
summary:
# Digital Healthcare for Menstrual Symptom Management: Key Findings

Dysmenorrhea and premenstrual syndrome represent significant menstrual health challenges with distinct underlying mechanisms. Dysmenorrhea is driven by elevated uterine prostaglandins causing enhanced uterine contractions and pain, while premenstrual syndrome involves cyclical hormonal fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone that regulate serotonin levels, producing diverse physical symptoms (bloating, breast tenderness, headaches) and psychological symptoms (depression, anxiety, irritability). Digital healthcare interventions, including smartphone applications and web-based programs, effectively reduce both dysmenorrhea and premenstrual syndrome severity. Specific evidence-based digital approaches include menstrual cycle tracking with chatbot counseling, video-based yoga and Pilates exercises, self-acupressure programs, cognitive-behavioral therapy, and stress prevention training. These digital solutions enhance reproductive health literacy and promote self-management through anonymous, accessible platforms that increase user satisfaction. Overall, digital health technologies represent cost-efficient, effective alternatives to traditional pharmacological treatments, offering women diverse non-pharmacological options to manage menstrual symptoms while avoiding medication side effects.

title: A 12-Week Strict Low FODMAP Diet Reduces the Severity Levels of Fatigue, Depression, Anxiety, and Inattention in Patients with Irritable Bowel Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This 12-week randomized controlled trial demonstrates that a strict low FODMAP diet substantially improves both gastrointestinal and psychological symptoms in irritable bowel syndrome patients by reducing fermentation of poorly absorbed short-chain carbohydrates in the small intestine. Participants experienced significant reductions in fatigue, anxiety, and depression, alongside improvements in attentional control measured by computerized performance testing—notably the first study to document cognitive benefits from dietary intervention in IBS. The improvements in mental health outcomes suggest that dietary management modulates the gut-brain axis disturbances underlying IBS-associated mood and cognitive dysfunction. These findings establish dietary management as a crucial therapeutic approach that addresses both physical gastrointestinal symptoms and comorbid psychological burden in IBS patients, with particular efficacy in IBS-D and IBS-M subtypes.

title: Interaction of blood-entry components, network pharmacology and transcriptomics to elucidate the mechanism of Wentong plaster in treating primary dysmenorrhea
summary:
# Summary

Wentong plaster (WTT), a traditional Chinese herbal formulation comprising Corydalis Rhizoma, Chuanxiong Rhizoma, Salviae Miltiorrhizae Radix Et Rhizoma, and other botanical components, treats primary dysmenorrhea through multiple integrated mechanisms. The treatment significantly upregulates nitric oxide (NO) levels, which promotes blood vessel dilation and smooth muscle relaxation, while simultaneously downregulating pain-associated prostaglandins (PGF2α and PGE2) and hormonal factors (estrogen and progesterone) that typically become elevated in dysmenorrhea. Additionally, WTT suppresses the IL-17 inflammatory signaling pathway by decreasing Cxcl6 and IL-17 expression while upregulating the protective factor Lcn2, thereby reducing inflammation and uterine pathology. Clinical evidence demonstrates that WTT significantly alleviates menstrual pain by addressing the underlying pathophysiology of dysmenorrhea, including excessive uterine contractions and the inflammatory cascade associated with this condition.

title: An Examination into the Safety and Efficacy of Khapregesic®, a Khaya Senegalensis Preparation, on Women Experiencing Menstrual Pain and Menstrual Distress: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial demonstrated that Khapregesic®, a Khaya senegalensis preparation, significantly reduces menstrual pain and menstrual distress in women compared to placebo, with the treatment group achieving a 45.8% reduction in pain ratings and a 30.6% reduction in overall menstrual distress scores. The therapeutic effects likely stem from multiple mechanisms: the bark contains limonoids with anti-inflammatory properties, exhibits antinociceptive activity, and stimulates opioid receptors while inhibiting cyclooxygenase enzymes that produce pain-mediating prostaglandins E2 and F2α. Beyond pain relief, Khaya senegalensis improved emotional wellbeing and psychological symptoms (35.5% reduction), reduced physical symptoms, decreased rescue medication use, and improved sleep quality. The antioxidant and potential GABAergic effects of the preparation may contribute to its broader benefits in alleviating the multifaceted burden of menstrual distress, which encompasses physical symptoms such as abdominal pain, bloating, and fatigue alongside mood disturbances. These findings support Khaya senegalensis as a safe and effective alternative or complementary option to conventional pharmacological treatments for dysmenorrhea and associated menstrual symptoms.

title: Effectiveness of Multisymptom Support for Better Relief and Alleviation of Common Effects in Perimenopause (EMBRACE PERIMENOPAUSE)
summary: The EMBRACE PERIMENOPAUSE trial evaluated a multisymptom nutraceutical formulation (Arth Perimenopause Multisymptom Support capsules) containing ashwagandha, vitex, black cohosh, and other herbal extracts for treating perimenopause-related symptoms in 30 women. Perimenopause causes a constellation of vasomotor (hot flashes, night sweating), psychological (mood changes, anxiety, irritability), and physical symptoms (joint pain, fatigue, vaginal dryness) that significantly impair quality of life. By day 60 of treatment, the formulation achieved remarkably high symptom resolution rates, including 93% for hot flashes, 91% for anxiety, 90% for depressive mood, and 89% for irritability, with an overall 92.97% reduction in Menopause Rating Scale scores. The botanical ingredients appear to work through modulation of neuroendocrine pathways, as ashwagandha supplementation specifically increased serum estradiol while reducing follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone levels. Beyond symptom relief, treatment significantly improved quality of life and patient satisfaction across both vasomotor and somatic domains, including resolution of breast tenderness, sleep disturbances, and vaginal dryness. These findings suggest that nutraceutical supplementation represents a promising, well-tolerated alternative to hormone replacement therapy for managing the multifaceted symptoms of perimenopause.

title: Management of non-traumatic abdominal pain in the emergency department: a multicentre, stepped-wedge, cluster-randomised trial
summary:
# Summary

This multicentre, stepped-wedge cluster-randomised trial demonstrates that a structured Abdominal Pain Unit (APU) process significantly improves emergency department management of non-traumatic abdominal pain. The APU protocol—comprising systematic clinical examination, laboratory evaluation, pain assessment with targeted management, and sonography when indicated—reduced mean pain scores from 4.3 to 3.6 and notably decreased 30-day mortality from 2.3% to 0.8%. Non-traumatic abdominal pain is a complex clinical presentation associated with life-threatening conditions including sepsis, mesenteric infarction, and ileus, as well as numerous chronic underlying diseases such as endometriosis, gallstones, and previous abdominal surgery. Implementation of the APU process improved patient satisfaction by 1.54 points on the satisfaction scale without increasing emergency department length of stay or raising unnecessary diagnostic procedures. These findings establish the APU protocol as a safe, effective, and efficient intervention that substantially reduces mortality while enhancing pain control and patient outcomes in emergency settings.

title: First use of cord blood platelet-rich plasma in the treatment of vulvar lichen sclerosus: a preliminary study towards a randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Vulvar lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by dysregulated Th-1 and T-reg responses that lead to tissue damage, fibroblast activation, and a constellation of symptoms including pruritus, burning, dysuria, and sexual dysfunction. While topical corticosteroids represent first-line treatment, approximately 30% of patients achieve incomplete symptom resolution and corticosteroids fail to restore vulvar trophism and elasticity critical for sexual function. This preliminary study demonstrates that cord blood platelet-rich plasma (CB-PRP) offers superior therapeutic potential compared to peripheral blood-derived PRP due to its elevated concentrations of anti-inflammatory molecules and growth factors, including platelet-derived growth factor, FGF2, and VEGF, which promote tissue repair and macrophage polarization toward an anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. CB-PRP treatment significantly improved vulvar burning, sexual arousal, sexual satisfaction, skin elasticity, and nourishment in all treated patients, with a 55.5% reduction in maintenance corticosteroid requirements. These findings support CB-PRP as a promising regenerative therapy for vulvar lichen sclerosus that addresses both symptom relief and underlying tissue pathology, warranting further investigation in randomized controlled trials.

title: Future of Stroke Prevention
summary:
# Summary: Future of Stroke Prevention

The 2024 Primary Prevention of Stroke Guideline emphasizes multiple evidence-based approaches to reduce stroke risk across diverse populations. Dietary interventions, particularly the Mediterranean diet, demonstrate substantial protective effects with approximately 35% risk reduction, while GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide and dulaglutide represent emerging pharmacological strategies conferring 24-27% stroke risk reduction in clinical trials. Intensive blood pressure control targeting systolic values below 130 mm Hg provides robust protection, with each 5 mm Hg reduction associated with approximately 13% lower stroke risk. The guideline introduces sex-specific risk assessment, identifying endometriosis, premature ovarian failure, and early-onset menopause as stroke risk factors in women, while recommending careful evaluation of estrogen therapy given its associated increased stroke risk.
