Episiotomy dehiscence represents a significant postpartum complication that can develop following vaginal delivery, with risk factors including elevated maternal BMI exceeding 35 kg/m², instrumental delivery, and fetal macrosomia. Dehiscence leads to poor-quality granular tissue formation and chronic sequelae including dyspareunia, genitourinary dysfunction, and psychological pathology. This case series evaluated a novel physiotherapy protocol combining radiofrequency and magnetotherapy for treating episiotomy dehiscence, demonstrating that all patients achieved 50% pain relief and complete recovery within one week. The mechanisms underlying treatment success involve radiofrequency-induced increases in tissue temperature, skin perfusion, and oxyhemoglobin levels promoting tissue regeneration, while magnetotherapy enhances wound healing and reduces associated symptoms like edema and hematoma. Clinical outcomes showed rapid pain reduction on visual analog scales (from 8–9 to 4–6 after initial treatment) with resolution of edema and hematoma within 7 days. This integrated physiotherapy approach offers a promising non-surgical alternative to traditional suture repair methods, which are associated with increased infection risk and prolonged postpartum pain.

title: Diaphragmatic breathing for referred pain after total laparoscopic hysterectomy: a randomized clinical trial
summary: This randomized clinical trial demonstrates that diaphragmatic breathing exercises effectively reduce referred cervicoscapular pain following total laparoscopic hysterectomy, with pain commonly manifesting in the shoulder, neck, head, and upper limbs. The diaphragmatic breathing group showed significantly faster recovery in pain intensity, neck disability index (NDI), and headache impact test (HIT-6) scores compared to the neck exercise control group, suggesting a mechanism beyond simple neuromodulation. The underlying pathophysiology involves CO₂ insufflation during laparoscopic surgery irritating the phrenic nerve, and diaphragmatic breathing appears to work through multiple mechanisms: facilitating CO₂ dispersion from the peritoneum, modulating phrenic nociceptive afferents, and activating pain gate control pathways via cervical tissue stimulation. Both intervention groups improved over time across all pain measures, though the breathing group demonstrated superior and earlier functional recovery with no reported adverse effects. These findings support diaphragmatic breathing as a safe, non-pharmacological adjunct to multimodal pain management strategies in the postoperative period, potentially reducing reliance on analgesic medications including opioids.

title: Effectiveness of Multidisciplinary Treatment Compared to Single Discipline Treatment of Female Chronic Pelvic Pain: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analyses
summary:
# Summary: Multidisciplinary Treatment of Female Chronic Pelvic Pain

Chronic pelvic pain, which encompasses endometriosis, primary dysmenorrhea, vulvodynia, and bladder pain syndrome, is a complex condition with widespread biopsychosocial consequences including sexual dysfunction (68% of patients), bowel and bladder dysfunction, irritable bowel syndrome (35%), and mental health distress (57%). Meta-analyses demonstrate that multidisciplinary treatment combining medical intervention with physiotherapy and/or nutrition reduces post-treatment pain scores more effectively than single-discipline approaches and produces greater improvements in sexual function and dyspareunia. Notably, multidisciplinary treatment also reduces anxiety and depression symptoms and decreases analgesia use in the majority of participants, though quality of life improvements were not statistically different between treatment groups. The evidence supports a biopsychosocial approach to chronic pelvic pain management, wherein coordinated medical and physiotherapy interventions yield superior pain reduction and functional outcomes compared to medical treatment alone. Minor adverse events, including transient vulvar numbness from electrical stimulation and vaginal spotting, were documented but did not substantially limit treatment feasibility.

title: Discovery of an AKT1-targeting compound from a traditional herbal formula for alcoholic liver disease via integrative computational and experimental approaches
summary: This study identified oleanolic acid, a bioactive compound from the traditional Dampness-Heat Regulating Formula, as a therapeutic agent for alcoholic liver disease through integrated computational and experimental validation. The herbal formula, composed of nine medicinal herbs including Pogostemon cablin, Myristica fragrans, and Lonicera japonica, demonstrates protective effects against alcohol-induced hepatic damage via AKT1 pathway modulation. Oleanolic acid directly binds to AKT1, a key hub target implicated in alcoholic liver disease pathogenesis, and functional studies confirmed its capacity to alleviate ethanol-induced hepatocyte damage in both cellular and zebrafish models. The formula's therapeutic efficacy derives from synergistic anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of its herbal components, particularly the combination of Lonicera japonica and Taraxaci herba, which counteract the oxidative stress and cellular damage central to disease progression. This research establishes a mechanistic foundation for integrating traditional Chinese medicine with modern drug discovery approaches, offering a complementary strategy to existing treatments such as cortisol therapy and interleukin-based interventions that carry significant side effect burdens.

title: Effects of photobiomodulation therapy combined with static magnetic field on pain and function in patients with lateral epicondylitis: a multicentre, randomised, placebo-controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Photobiomodulation therapy combined with static magnetic field (PBMT-sMF) significantly reduces pain in lateral epicondylitis through anti-inflammatory mechanisms, particularly by downregulating the pro-inflammatory cytokine TNF-α and related mediators including IL-1β and PGE2. TNF-α plays a central role in pain pathogenesis by sensitizing nociceptors and activating downstream inflammatory cascades associated with tissue degeneration in this tendinopathic condition. The therapeutic effects of PBMT-sMF stem from dual mechanisms: direct inhibition of neural activity and modulation of inflammatory biomarkers that contribute to pain relief. While PBMT-sMF demonstrates clinically meaningful pain reduction with significantly higher responder rates compared to placebo, it does not substantially improve functional outcomes such as grip strength or forearm disability as measured by standard clinical assessments. These findings suggest that PBMT-sMF is an effective analgesic intervention for lateral epicondylitis but may require complementary approaches to achieve broader functional restoration.

title: Digital health technologies to transform women’s health innovation and inclusive research
summary: Digital health technologies (DHTs) offer transformative potential for addressing longstanding inequities in women's health research and clinical care by improving accessibility through telehealth platforms and mobile health applications. Mobile health apps enhance contraceptive use, reduce unintended pregnancies, and facilitate treatment of sexually transmitted infections, while fertility tracking applications have demonstrated improved time to pregnancy, particularly among underserved populations with limited education. Wearable sensors and connected devices enable personalized healthcare approaches, and both mHealth apps and wearables improve quality of life outcomes. However, significant barriers impede equitable DHT implementation, including cost, connectivity limitations, digital skills gaps, and data privacy concerns that disproportionately affect women. Socio-political factors, such as restrictions on abortion access, further reduce technology adoption rates. Future advancement requires federated learning approaches and collaborative research models that prioritize inclusive design and address the digital divide to ensure DHTs meaningfully benefit all women, including those with chronic conditions like endometriosis-associated pain.

title: Leadership and culture change to advance innovation in women’s health
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and other women's health conditions remain under-researched and underfunded, a disparity that stems partly from insufficient female representation in research and innovation leadership. Women researchers, particularly from low and middle-income countries, play a crucial role in shifting research agendas toward conditions that disproportionately affect women and in identifying overlooked health needs during early-stage innovation design. Inclusive health innovation requires embedding sex and gender considerations into research training and development, establishing diverse leadership teams, and implementing gender-responsive regulatory frameworks that mandate such considerations. Notable examples demonstrate this impact, including women-led innovations like Kasha for stigma reduction and Chava for reproductive health education, as well as initiatives like CognitiveCare that address maternal health gaps in underserved populations. The evidence indicates that women's leadership at early stages of innovation significantly influences problem identification and solution design, enabling health technologies to address gaps that traditional male-dominated development approaches have historically overlooked. Increasing women's representation throughout the entire innovation ecosystem is essential for developing more effective and inclusive health solutions for women's conditions like endometriosis.

title: Symptoms and quality of life assessment after coil and foam embolization in patients with venous-origin chronic pelvic pain (VO-CPP) – a subgroup of pelvic venous disorders (PeVD)
summary: Venous-origin chronic pelvic pain (VO-CPP) results from venous insufficiency in the pelvic region, where refluxing ovarian and uterine veins cause pain and associated symptoms affecting quality of life. Endovascular coil and foam embolization, targeting the left ovarian vein (95% of patients), left uterine vein (75%), and right ovarian vein (55%), provides significant symptom relief with 68–100% pain reduction on the Visual Analog Scale. Treatment produced pronounced reductions in daytime pelvic pain, standing pain, and postcoital pain, with more modest improvements in nighttime pain and sleep disturbances. Both physical and mental health components of quality of life improved significantly at mid-term and long-term follow-ups, independent of ovarian vein diameter measurements. Embolization also reduced associated symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, backache, hip pain, leg pain, and nausea. Patient satisfaction was high, with 81% satisfied with the procedure, 86% willing to repeat it, and 89% willing to recommend it to others.

title: Handheld Point-of-Care Ultrasonography for Gynecology
summary: This study evaluates the feasibility and clinical utility of portable point-of-care ultrasonography (POCUS) for gynecologic imaging, with particular emphasis on its performance under extreme environmental conditions including altered gravity. Portable POCUS demonstrated high diagnostic capability for multiple gynecologic conditions, including detection of ovarian cysts, pelvic free fluid, hemorrhagic cysts, tubo-ovarian abscesses, leiomyomas, and ovarian torsion, while also improving fetal heart tone detection and identification of abnormal pregnancies in early pregnancy assessment. Testing in altered gravity environments revealed that microgravity induces fluid shifts throughout the body and enables rapid uterine and bladder visualization, while hypergravity phases (approximately 2 G) cause heavier upper-extremity movement and probe positioning challenges, along with symptoms including nausea and lower extremity fluid pooling. The study found that minimizing head movement during gravitational transitions reduces associated symptoms of vertigo, nausea, and dizziness by stabilizing the vestibular system, though altered gravity significantly affects spatial orientation and hand-eye coordination. The authors propose that future integration of artificial intelligence-guided image capturing and augmented reality technologies could further improve emergency management capabilities. These findings establish portable POCUS as a versatile diagnostic tool for gynecologic emergencies while identifying specific physiologic challenges and adaptive strategies necessary for ultrasonography in non-terrestrial environments.

title: Nociplastic pain: an introduction
journal: The Journal of the Canadian Chiropractic Association
summary:
# Summary

Unable to provide a summary based on the extracted relationships. The article "Nociplastic pain: an introduction" was processed, but no structured relationship triples were identified in the source material. To generate a meaningful summary of this article's key findings on nociplastic pain mechanisms and clinical relevance, relationship data linking biological entities, pathways, and clinical outcomes would be needed. If relationship extraction was unsuccessful, please verify the article text or provide additional structured data to enable summarization.

title: Clinical Evidence of Acupoint Stimulation for Primary Dysmenorrhea: A Systematic Review and Updated Meta-Analysis [Letter]
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 22 randomized controlled trials demonstrates that acupoint stimulation effectively reduces pain associated with primary dysmenorrhea, a chronic and recurrent condition affecting many women. The analysis reveals that electroacupuncture treatment using dense-sparse wave patterns produces particularly strong immediate analgesic effects for dysmenorrhea symptoms. The findings align with established pharmaceutical approaches, as both progesterone and estrogen hormones are known to alleviate endometriosis-associated pain through distinct mechanisms. These results provide robust clinical evidence supporting acupoint stimulation as a viable therapeutic option for managing menstrual pain, though the study emphasizes the need for long-term follow-up observations given the chronic nature of primary dysmenorrhea. The integration of rigorous statistical analysis across multiple trials strengthens the evidence base for acupuncture-based interventions in dysmenorrhea management.

title: Long Term Effects of Acupuncture for Primary Dysmenorrhea: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that acupuncture is effective for managing primary dysmenorrhea, reducing pain intensity significantly compared to no intervention and outperforming NSAIDs as a standalone treatment. Acupuncture also enhances the analgesic effects of NSAIDs when used in combination, providing a clinically relevant advantage given that prolonged NSAID use is associated with gastrointestinal mucosal injury and neuropsychiatric adverse effects, while hormonal contraceptives carry risks of irregular bleeding and venous thromboembolism. Primary dysmenorrhea, characterized by spasmodic lower abdominal pain accompanied by nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting, causes substantial morbidity including work absenteeism and economic burden. The key strength of acupuncture lies in its safety profile and durable symptom relief, making it a valuable alternative for patients with gynecological pain disorders. However, the analysis notes that acupuncture showed no statistical superiority over sham acupuncture at three months post-treatment, suggesting that further investigation into sustained clinical benefits is warranted.

title: Implementing a Fermentable Oligosaccharides, Disaccharides, Monosaccharides, and Polyols Diet in Asia: Addressing Cultural, Clinical and Practical Challenges
summary: The low FODMAP diet is an evidence-based therapeutic approach that effectively reduces irritable bowel syndrome symptoms including abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits by limiting fermentable carbohydrates that undergo bacterial fermentation and cause intestinal distension. While the diet has shown efficacy in treating IBS and may benefit other conditions such as endometriosis and chronic diarrhea, implementing it in Asian populations presents significant cultural challenges, as traditional dishes in India, Japan, and Korea frequently contain high-FODMAP ingredients like onions, garlic, wheat products, lentils, and dairy. The study identified specific high-FODMAP foods common in Asian cuisine—including lentil-based dishes (88% high FODMAP), wheat products like semolina and vermicelli, and cauliflower—and demonstrated practical modifications using low-FODMAP substitutes such as garlic-infused oil, rice flour, cucumber, and spring onion greens. Since IBS symptoms significantly impair quality of life and are associated with notable mental health comorbidities, successful dietary interventions require culturally adapted recipes and delivery by trained dietitians. The research emphasizes that effective FODMAP diet implementation across Asia demands careful recipe modification that maintains cultural food traditions while reducing fermentable oligosaccharide content.

title: Urologic Manifestations of Nonrelaxing Pelvic Floor Dysfunction: Insights on Clinical Workup and Management
summary:
# Summary

Non-relaxing pelvic floor dysfunction (NR-PFD) is an underdiagnosed condition caused by chronic pelvic floor muscle contraction that results from maladaptive learned behaviors, often originating in childhood, and is perpetuated by chronic pain-related factors including anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbance. This dysfunction causes a spectrum of urologic symptoms including storage dysfunction (urinary frequency, urgency, nocturia) and voiding dysfunction (hesitancy, straining, weak stream, urinary retention), along with pelvic pain, defecatory dysfunction, and sexual dysfunction such as dyspareunia. NR-PFD is frequently associated with conditions including endometriosis, interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, and irritable bowel syndrome, with endometriosis excision itself potentially causing postoperative pelvic floor hypertonicity and urinary retention. Diagnosis relies on video urodynamics, surface electromyography, and focused physical examination, with imaging modalities such as ultrasound and MRI providing additional morphologic assessment. Pelvic floor physical therapy is the first-line treatment with strong evidence of efficacy, while adjunctive options including biofeedback, trigger point injections, botulinum toxin, and sacral neuromodulation can be employed in refractory cases.

title: Diagnostic delay in rare diseases in the Campania region: addressing ageing, gender disparities, and the “postcode lottery effect” to reduce the patient odyssey
journal: The European Journal of Public Health
summary: This study examines diagnostic delays across rare diseases in the Campania region of Italy, identifying significant gender and geographic disparities. Female patients, particularly those with endometriosis and other gender-specific conditions, experience substantially longer diagnostic delays compared to males, alongside increased delays in older patients and those residing outside Naples, demonstrating a marked "postcode lottery effect." Immunological disorders, connective tissue diseases, digestive system diseases, and complex congenital malformations show particularly high risks of diagnostic delay (odds ratios ranging from 2.08 to 4.53), while hematological, metabolic, endocrine, and respiratory diseases demonstrate lower delay risks. The research highlights that comorbidities in older patients and female underrepresentation in clinical trials contribute to diagnostic delays, though newborn screening programs and increased awareness among pediatricians help mitigate delays in pediatric populations. These findings underscore systemic inequities in rare disease diagnosis and support targeted interventions to reduce diagnostic odysseys, particularly for women and patients in underserved geographic regions.

title: Smartphone-Based Muscle Relaxation for Migraine in the Emergency Department
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial evaluated smartphone-based progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) as a self-management intervention for migraine patients discharged from the emergency department. The PMR intervention, delivered through the RELAXaHEAD app alongside an electronic headache diary, significantly reduced migraine-related disability as measured by the Migraine Disability Assessment Scale (MIDAS), with 82.4% of PMR users achieving clinically meaningful improvement compared to 45.7% in the control group. Notably, PMR efficacy correlated with usage frequency, with high-frequency users demonstrating superior outcomes in MIDAS scores and disability reduction. While the intervention did not significantly reduce monthly headache days or overall migraine-specific quality of life scores between groups, the improvements in MIDAS scores persisted regardless of baseline anxiety or depression status, suggesting PMR's effectiveness as an adjunctive self-management tool. These findings support smartphone-based PMR as a grade A, evidence-based prevention strategy that addresses the substantial clinical burden of migraine recurrence following emergency department discharge, with nearly half of patients typically revisiting the ED within three months.

title: Chinese herbal medicine for primary dysmenorrhoea
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: This Cochrane systematic review demonstrates that Chinese herbal medicine is an effective treatment for primary dysmenorrhoea, reducing menstrual pain and associated symptoms. Across multiple randomized controlled trials, Chinese herbal medicine showed significant improvements in pain relief with a relative risk of 1.99 compared to control treatments, while self-designed herbal formulae demonstrated even greater efficacy with a relative risk of 2.06. Chinese herbal medicine also outperformed alternative therapies such as acupuncture for pain relief. While nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and contraceptive pills remain established treatments for dysmenorrhoea, this evidence suggests Chinese herbal medicine offers a promising therapeutic option for women experiencing painful menstrual cramps associated with primary dysmenorrhoea, potentially addressing cases where conventional treatments are less tolerated or desired by patients.

title: Evaluation of the need for the integration of a nurse specialist (EndoNurse) into the interdisciplinary care of patients with endometriosis: Cross-sectional study
journal: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes significant pain symptoms and substantially impairs quality of life and social functioning, with patients experiencing a wide spectrum of manifestations including dysmenorrhea (77.1%), flatulence and back pain (74.9%), and dyspareunia (63.7%). Current management involves multiple therapeutic approaches, with surgery and analgesics being most common (73.1% and 65.6% respectively), alongside hormonal treatments and lifestyle modifications. This cross-sectional study demonstrates that integrating a specialized endometriosis nurse (EndoNurse) into multidisciplinary care teams significantly improves patient satisfaction, treatment compliance, and clinical outcomes while reducing healthcare costs and emergency room utilization. Patients prioritize EndoNurse involvement in pain management, nutrition counseling, sports and physiotherapy guidance, coping strategies, and therapy education, with poor physician communication, treatment side effects, and insufficient patient information being major drivers of non-compliance. The findings support that continuous specialized nursing care combined with comprehensive patient education addressing disease understanding, sexuality, nutrition, and psychological well-being is critical for optimizing endometriosis management and reducing the burden of this chronic disease.

title: Transcutaneous auricular neurostimulation to reduce heavy menstrual bleeding in women with and without von Willebrand disease
summary:
# Summary

Transcutaneous auricular neurostimulation (tAN) reduces heavy menstrual bleeding by approximately 57% and shortens menstruation duration by 19% in women with and without von Willebrand disease, while simultaneously reducing menstrual pain, cramping, and fatigue and improving overall quality of life. The mechanism involves tAN activating both the vagal and trigeminal nerve networks, which modulate platelet function through multiple pathways: enhanced adenosine diphosphate-mediated alpha granule release, increased activated glycoprotein IIb/IIIa surface expression, and improved clot strength. At the systemic level, vagal stimulation induces acetylcholine release in the spleen, which primes platelets by elevating intracellular calcium and increasing P-selectin expression, thereby enhancing platelet responsiveness to pro-coagulant signals. Additionally, tAN shifts monocytes toward an anti-inflammatory phenotype and releases central nervous system endorphins, providing antinociceptive effects that contribute to pain reduction and improved physical and emotional functioning. This non-pharmacological intervention demonstrates substantially greater efficacy than existing medical treatments while being well-tolerated, making it a promising alternative for managing heavy menstrual bleeding across diverse etiologies.

title: Pain Neuroscience Education in Children and Adolescents with Chronic Pain: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Pediatric chronic pain affects children and adolescents across multiple pain types (headache, back pain, abdominal pain, musculoskeletal pain) and involves complex pathophysiology including central sensitization, whereby inhibitory pain pathways fail and pain perception becomes amplified. This systematic review demonstrates that pain neuroscience education (PNE) consistently improves pain-related knowledge, self-efficacy, functionality, and quality of life in children and adolescents with chronic pain, while producing moderate reductions in anxiety, catastrophizing, and kinesiophobia. Direct effects on pain intensity are modest, though combining PNE with exercise and booster sessions yields more favorable outcomes, and digital or gamified formats enhance engagement. Integration of PNE into biopsychosocial, multidisciplinary treatment programs—particularly alongside exercise and family involvement—appears to optimize pain management outcomes in this population.

title: Erythema ab igne—A Potential Cutaneous Marker of Chronic Heat Use in Patients with Endometriosis: A Narrative Literature Review and a Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines erythema ab igne (EAI), a benign skin condition caused by chronic exposure to low-level infrared heat, as a potential cutaneous marker of prolonged heating device use in endometriosis patients. Women with endometriosis frequently experience dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain driven by prostaglandins, leukotrienes, and vasopressin, leading them to rely heavily on heating pads and heat therapy for symptomatic relief—interventions that improve pelvic blood flow and reduce uterine contractility. However, prolonged or inappropriate heat application can trigger EAI through heat shock protein activation of NRAS and BRAF kinases, which promote keratinocyte proliferation while suppressing apoptotic pathways. Although typically benign, chronic EAI carries a risk of malignant transformation into squamous cell carcinoma, Merkel cell carcinoma, or basal cell carcinoma. The authors emphasize the need for patient education on safe heat therapy use and highlight how EAI illustrates the broader impact of chronic pain in endometriosis, linking cutaneous manifestations to neuroplastic alterations and psychiatric comorbidities including depression and anxiety. Topical treatments such as hydroquinone, retinoids, and in dysplastic cases 5-fluorouracil or imiquimod can improve skin appearance and prevent progression.

title: Management of symptoms of suspected adenomyosis uteri using herbal medicine modified Bojungikgi-tang: a case report with ultrasound monitoring
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis causes chronic pelvic pain, severe dysmenorrhea, and excessive menstrual bleeding (hypermenorrhea), which can lead to secondary complications including anemia, fatigue, and depression that significantly impair quality of life. While standard treatments such as oral contraceptives, progestins, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, and surgical interventions remain primary options, this case report demonstrates that herbal medicine modified Bojungikgi-tang (BZYQT) effectively reduced menstrual pain from a numeric rating scale of 7–8 to 1–2 and normalized hemoglobin levels following conventional hormonal therapy failure. The herbal formulation, containing key components such as Astragali Radix, Rehmanniae Radix Recens, and animal-derived gelatin products, works through multiple mechanisms including suppression of abnormal endometrial proliferation, promotion of tissue regeneration, enhancement of hematopoiesis, and modulation of estrogen receptor activity. Over six months of treatment, the patient experienced progressive improvement in menstrual cycle regularity (normalized to 28–29 days), resolution of dizziness, improved digestive function, and reduced menstrual bleeding, suggesting that herbal medicine may offer a viable complementary therapeutic option for adenomyosis management when conventional approaches prove insufficient.

title: Acupuncture combined with Chinese herbal medicine versus Chinese herbal medicine alone to improve clinical efficacy in treating endometriosis-associated pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis-associated pain, dysmenorrhea, and pelvic pain significantly impact women's quality of life, and traditional hormonal therapies are limited by adverse effects and high recurrence rates. This meta-analysis demonstrates that acupuncture combined with Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) substantially outperforms CHM monotherapy in treating endometriosis-related pain, with significantly higher clinical efficacy rates (OR = 3.75) and reduced visual analog scale scores. The combination therapy works through complementary mechanisms: acupuncture provides rapid local pain relief by inhibiting inflammatory responses and regulating the neuro-endocrine-immune network, while CHM prolongs therapeutic effects and prevents symptom recurrence by inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β) and regulating serum CA-125 and PGE2 levels. The synergistic "global regulation plus local treatment" approach not only relieves pain but also improves posterior fornix tender nodules and CHM syndrome scale scores while enhancing overall quality of life. The evidence indicates that acupuncture combined with CHM represents a valuable non-hormonal therapeutic option for managing endometriosis-associated pain with improved clinical outcomes and reduced disease progression.

title: Endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain management: Between a rock (denial) and a hard place (overdiagnosis)
summary:
# Summary

While endometriosis is strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain, the relationship is inconsistent and multifactorial, with ectopic endometrium not always responsible for symptoms. A lesion-centered diagnostic approach can be misleading, particularly in patients with noncyclic pain or superficial peritoneal endometriosis, leading to unsatisfactory treatment outcomes. Cyclical pain symptoms such as dysmenorrhea, catamenial dyschezia, and dysuria respond well to menstruation suppression, while mid-cycle pain improves with ovulation inhibition; progestogens and GnRH agonists/antagonists (including triptorelin and linzagolix) effectively reduce noncyclic chronic pelvic pain. Response to GnRH agonist therapy is associated with superficial peritoneal endometriosis, while non-responders may have central sensitization requiring multispecialty pain management. The article cautions against diagnostic overreach through noninvasive testing, salivary tests, and self-testing marketed directly to consumers, which substantially increase misdiagnosis and overdiagnosis risks.

title: Effect of multidimensional nursing on low anterior resection syndrome after sphincter preservation surgery for low rectal cancer
summary: Low anterior resection syndrome (LARS) is a common complication affecting over 80% of patients following sphincter-preserving surgery for low rectal cancer, characterized by frequent defecation, urgency, fecal incontinence, and incomplete defecation that significantly impair quality of life. The syndrome results from multiple pathophysiological mechanisms including rectal nerve damage, anal sphincter dysfunction, reduced rectal volume, altered rectal compliance, and abnormal pelvic floor muscle function. While conventional interventions such as pelvic floor muscle training, biofeedback, dietary adjustments, and Kegel exercises provide symptom management, a Nursing Outcomes Classification (NOC)-based multidimensional nursing approach integrating individualized patient education, behavioral therapy, psychological support, and structured follow-up significantly improves outcomes. In clinical application, patients with mild LARS received pelvic floor rehabilitation and stoma irrigation, while those with severe LARS additionally received biofeedback training and nutritional support with glutamine and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. The multidimensional nursing intervention demonstrated superior effectiveness compared to standard care, resulting in higher patient self-efficacy, self-decision-making, positive attitude, stress reduction, nursing satisfaction, and markedly improved quality of life scores on the EORTC QLQ-C30 scale. This comprehensive approach addresses both the physical symptoms and psychological burden of LARS, establishing health beliefs that promote sustained self-management behaviors and enhance overall patient outcomes.

title: Myopraktikk (NO): A Narrative Review and Conceptual Hypothesis on Intrafasciomembranal Fluid Pressure, Biotensegrity, and Immediate Remote Myofascial Responses
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review proposes intrafasciomembranal fluid pressure (IFMFP) as a unifying mechanism underlying myofascial pain syndromes and related conditions. Elevated IFMFP results from muscle overuse, impaired venous and lymphatic drainage, and emotional stress, leading to a self-perpetuating pathophysiological cascade: increased pressure causes local hypoxia and anaerobic metabolism, generating lactate and pyruvate that create local acidosis. Acidosis induces hyaluronan densification within fasciomembranes, reducing tissue permeability and further elevating pressure while activating nociceptors to produce pain, trigger points, muscle weakness, and fatigue. The authors propose that Myopraktikk, a manual treatment technique based on biotensegrity principles, effectively treats myofascial pain and related conditions by reducing trigger points, muscle stiffness, and fascial restrictions through mechanical intervention. This framework integrates biomechanical, physiological, and psychosocial factors to explain previously disconnected phenomena including fibromyalgia, delayed-onset muscle soreness, and chronic exertional compartment syndrome. The conceptual hypothesis positions fascial fluid dynamics as central to understanding and treating widespread musculoskeletal pain conditions.

title: Abstracts from the International Pelvic Pain Society (IPPS) Annual Scientific Meeting on Pelvic Pain 2024 in Conjunction with the 6th World Congress on Abdomino-Pelvic Pain
summary:
# Summary

This collection of abstracts from the 2024 International Pelvic Pain Society conference presents multiple therapeutic approaches for endometriosis-related pain and associated pelvic pain conditions. Intracavitary TECAR therapy emerges as a promising treatment that stimulates collagen production and angiogenesis while reducing pain and improving sexual function in endometriosis patients, with 80% of treated women reporting significant pain improvements. Complementary topical treatments including promestriene and oxytocin gel address genitourinary syndrome of menopause and vaginal atrophy through distinct mechanisms—promestriene significantly improved vaginal maturation index and sexual function, while oxytocin gel improved vaginal mucosa health and reduced intravaginal pH. The research reveals that chronic pelvic pain in endometriosis is characterized by specific neuroimmune changes, including reduced stromal nerve thickness and elevated dendritic cell density, establishing a biological basis for pain perpetuation. Additionally, pain widespreadness during menstruation shows strong associations with multiple comorbid symptoms including sensory hypersensitivity, somatization, and sleep disturbance, while patients with chronic pelvic pain face significant socioeconomic barriers to care that exacerbate their condition.

title: Digital Health Technologies in the Treatment of Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndromes: A Systematic Review of Randomized Clinical Trials
summary: Chronic pelvic pain syndromes, including endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, and bladder pain syndrome, involve complex mechanisms including central sensitization, autonomic nervous system dysfunction, and hyperalgesia, resulting in significant functional disability, sexual dysfunction, and reduced quality of life, particularly in women. Traditional and emerging therapeutic approaches such as pelvic floor muscle training, manual therapy, acupuncture, and cognitive interventions including mindfulness and pain education have demonstrated efficacy in treating chronic pelvic pain. This systematic review of randomized clinical trials evaluated digital health technologies as novel treatment modalities, finding that virtual reality, internet-delivered cognitive-behavioral interventions, mobile applications, and telehealth-delivered exercises showed promise in reducing pain intensity and associated symptoms in endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, and bladder pain syndrome patients. Virtual reality interventions demonstrated particular effectiveness for endometriosis and dysmenorrhea, though some studies reported minor adverse effects including motion sickness, headache, and nausea. The evidence indicates that digital health technologies offer meaningful pain reduction across multiple chronic pelvic pain conditions, though the magnitude of improvement varied across studies and both experimental and control groups sometimes showed pain reduction from baseline. Overall, digital health technologies represent a valuable adjunctive treatment option that may improve accessibility and outcomes for patients with chronic pelvic pain syndromes.

title: The efficacy of Fire dragon cupping combined with Shaofu Zhuyu decoction in treating primary dysmenorrhea with cold coagulation and blood stasis
summary: This study demonstrates that Fire Dragon Cupping combined with Shaofu Zhuyu Decoction effectively treats primary dysmenorrhea associated with cold coagulation and blood stasis by targeting vascular dysfunction in the uterus. The combination therapy significantly reduced pain scores, Traditional Chinese Medicine syndrome scores, and visual analogue scale ratings compared to decoction alone, while improving uterine blood perfusion as measured by decreased resistance and pulsatility indices. The therapeutic mechanism involves modulating vasoactive mediators: the treatment reduced endothelin-1 (a potent vasoconstrictor expressed in uterine tissue) and increased nitric oxide (a vasodilator), thereby reducing myometrial spasm and improving local blood circulation. Shaofu Zhuyu Decoction's herbal components—including Chuanxiong rhizoma, Corydalis rhizoma, Angelicae sinensis radix, and warming agents like Cinnamomi cortex—promote blood circulation and remove blood stasis, while Fire Dragon Cupping enhances these effects through meridian dredging and massage-induced circulation improvement. The study suggests that dysmenorrhea pathophysiology involves impaired endothelial function and altered inflammatory cytokine expression, which this integrated Traditional Chinese Medicine approach successfully addresses through combined physical and pharmacological intervention.

title: Ultrasound‐Guided External Trigger Point Injections for Female Patients With Myofascial Pelvic Pain: A Report of Two Cases
summary: High-tone pelvic floor disorder, a common cause of myofascial pelvic pain syndrome, develops when muscle injury creates trigger points—localized areas of tenderness within hypertonic muscles. This case series demonstrates that ultrasound-guided external trigger point injections using lidocaine 1% effectively treat myofascial pelvic pain, with both patients experiencing significant relief lasting several months, suggesting superior efficacy compared to internal transvaginal approaches. The authors document a multimodal treatment hierarchy for high-tone pelvic floor disorder, with first-line pelvic floor physical therapy supplemented by pharmacologic agents including low-dose naltrexone, tizanidine, and vaginal baclofen/diazepam suppositories, progressing to botulinum toxin injections and sacral neuromodulation if needed. Ultrasound guidance enhances trigger point injection accuracy and outcomes, allowing precise visualization of target sites previously thought impossible to image, thereby improving treatment precision and patient results. The cases illustrate that pelvic pain often has multiple etiologies, including endometriosis, vaginismus, vulvodynia, and Crohn's disease, necessitating comprehensive multimodal approaches. Ultrasound-guided external trigger point injections represent an effective intermediate intervention that can be integrated into comprehensive treatment programs for patients with pelvic pain refractory to conservative management.

title: Experiences of Sexual Health and Intimate Relationships in Women With Lipedema: A Qualitative Study
summary: Lipedema is a chronic disease predominantly affecting women, characterized by abnormal subcutaneous fat accumulation that causes pain, heaviness, swelling, and easy bruising, with symptoms often emerging during puberty due to female hormones and genetic predisposition. Beyond physical symptoms, lipedema significantly impairs quality of life through body dissatisfaction, shame, and stigmatization that profoundly affect women's sexual health and intimate relationships. Women with lipedema experience multifaceted barriers to sexual intimacy, including bodily pain that restricts sexual activity, physical heaviness that creates discomfort during intimate situations, and compression garment use that inhibits sexual spontaneity. Conservative treatments such as compression therapy and lymphatic drainage provide symptom management, while liposuction can reduce pain and swelling, though many women struggle with the psychological burden of body image concerns regardless of treatment. The research highlights that sexual health challenges in lipedema extend beyond physical limitations to encompass psychological and relational dimensions, with affected women caught between desire for intimacy and avoidance strategies shaped by shame and body dissatisfaction. Addressing the comprehensive impact of lipedema requires integrated approaches that target both physical symptoms and the psychological well-being essential to intimate relationships.

title: Efficacy and safety of traditional Chinese medicine for endometriosis-related pain: a meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) effectively reduces endometriosis-associated pain by targeting inflammatory pathways. CHM significantly decreased visual analogue scale (VAS) scores for both dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia, with particularly strong effects on dyspareunia pain reduction. The pain relief appears mechanistically linked to CHM's ability to downregulate key pro-inflammatory cytokines, specifically reducing serum levels of tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) and interleukin-6 (IL-6), both of which are associated with endometriosis-related pain. These inflammatory markers were measured as secondary outcomes to evaluate CHM's therapeutic impact on pain symptoms. Overall, the analysis supports CHM as an effective treatment option for endometriosis-associated pain with an acceptable safety profile, suggesting that anti-inflammatory mechanisms may underlie its clinical benefits.

title: Online randomised e-hypnotherapy for chronic pelvic pain study (REST): comparing e-hypnotherapy with relaxation and waitlist to improve pain, cost-effectiveness and biopsychosocial outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pelvic pain, including endometriosis-related pain, affects 15–34% of the global population and is strongly associated with poor health-related quality of life, depression, anxiety, and sexual dysfunction. The bidirectional relationship between pain and psychological distress creates particularly poor treatment outcomes when both conditions coexist. Hypnotherapy, delivered through online prerecorded sessions (e-hypnotherapy), shows promise as a cost-effective psychological intervention, with evidence indicating it reduces chronic pelvic pain severity by approximately 30% and concurrently improves anxiety, depression, and overall quality of life. The REST trial evaluates whether e-hypnotherapy outperforms relaxation techniques and waitlist control in improving pain and biopsychosocial outcomes for chronic pelvic pain patients. This research addresses a significant treatment gap, as psychological interventions remain underutilized despite recommendations for their inclusion in multidisciplinary chronic pelvic pain management, particularly given the accessibility advantages of online delivery.

title: AI-Assisted Knee Infrared Imaging Based Acupuncture for Treating Knee Osteoarthritis: A Randomized Controlled Study Protocol
summary:
# Summary

Knee osteoarthritis is a chronic degenerative disease characterized by pain, stiffness, and functional impairment that significantly impacts patient quality of life. Acupuncture, a traditional Chinese medicine approach, has demonstrated efficacy in reducing osteoarthritis-related pain and improving joint function through multiple mechanisms including endogenous opioid release, inflammatory pathway modulation, and enhanced local blood circulation. This randomized controlled trial proposes an innovative AI-assisted acupuncture approach that uses infrared thermography to identify heat-sensitive knee areas and generate personalized treatment protocols, hypothesizing that such individualization will enhance treatment outcomes compared to standard acupuncture. The study will measure primary outcomes through infrared imaging-detected skin temperature changes, which correlate with clinical symptoms and inflammatory markers including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α. While acupuncture carries known adverse effects including dizziness, bleeding, hematoma formation, and potential local infection risk, the research aims to establish whether AI-guided acupuncture provides superior symptom relief and functional improvement, with celecoxib available as rescue medication for ethical considerations.

title: Electroacupuncture for Refractory Chronic Pelvic Pain: A Case Report with 24-Month Follow-Up
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents the successful treatment of refractory chronic pelvic pain using electroacupuncture in a 29-year-old woman who had failed multiple conventional therapies. The patient's condition was multifactorial, involving adenomyosis, central sensitization, and neuropathic pain components, which contributed to her chronic pelvic pain alongside associated symptoms of dyspareunia, anxiety, depression, and bowel dysfunction. Previous treatments including leuprolide acetate, pregabalin, superior hypogastric plexus neurolysis, and traditional acupuncture provided limited relief. After 40 sessions of electroacupuncture, the patient experienced dramatic pain reduction (from worst pain score of 10 to 2) with corresponding improvements in quality of life (from 0.331 to 0.942), sustained over the 24-month follow-up period. Electroacupuncture's analgesic mechanisms appear to involve regulation of neurotransmitters, inflammatory pathways, neural plasticity, and activation of descending inhibitory pathways, while also improving emotional disorders. This case demonstrates that electroacupuncture may offer a valuable therapeutic option for patients with refractory chronic pelvic pain who have exhausted conventional treatment modalities.

title: Efficacy and safety of acupuncture combined with pelvic floor muscle training in the treatment of stress urinary incontinence in women: A systematic review meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic meta-analysis demonstrates that acupuncture combined with pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) significantly improves outcomes in stress urinary incontinence (SUI), a condition closely associated with pregnancy, childbirth, menopause, and obesity. The combined treatment was superior to PFMT alone, reducing urine leakage by a weighted mean difference of 2.58 grams on a 1-hour pad test and substantially improving quality-of-life measures including Incontinence Questionnaire-Short Form scores and the Incontinence Impact Questionnaire-7. Mechanistically, pelvic floor muscle training strengthens pelvic floor structure and function necessary for urethral sphincter closure, while electroacupuncture at specific acupoints (Shenshu and Huiyang) enhances leak point pressure and bladder capacity by modulating collagen deposition and reducing matrix metalloproteinase expression in urethral tissues. Additional evidence indicates acupuncture reduces neuronal activation in brain centers controlling micturition and decreases daytime incontinence episodes by over 60 percent, with acupuncture demonstrating broader efficacy in treating various gynecological conditions including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome. These findings support the combined approach as an effective and safe therapeutic option for managing SUI and improving patient quality of life.

title: Effects of comprehensive nursing care on infection prevention and recovery outcomes in women undergoing gynecological surgery
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that comprehensive perioperative nursing care significantly reduces infection rates and improves recovery outcomes in gynecological surgery patients. Patients receiving comprehensive nursing intervention experienced substantially lower surgical site infection rates (4.2% versus 16.7%) and urinary tract infections (8.3% versus 20.8%), along with reduced postoperative inflammatory markers including C-reactive protein, white blood cell count, and procalcitonin, requiring shorter courses of prophylactic antibiotics. The nursing care protocol encompasses preoperative psychological interventions and personalized assessments to reduce anxiety, intraoperative measures to prevent complications such as hypothermia and nerve injury, and postoperative strategies including early mobilization, pain management, and infection prevention that collectively reduce recovery time and enhance quality of life. Additional benefits include prevention of thrombosis through ankle exercises and pneumatic compression devices, optimization of bowel function through early mobilization and dietary management, and long-term support through continuity of care and remote follow-up systems that reduce recurrence risk and improve compliance with recovery protocols. These findings underscore the critical role of systematic, multimodal nursing interventions in mitigating perioperative complications and enhancing both immediate postoperative outcomes and long-term health management for women undergoing gynecological procedures.

title: The Use of Platelet-Rich Plasma in Wildlife Veterinary Medicine
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Platelet-Rich Plasma Applications in Wildlife Veterinary Medicine

Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) and related blood-derived preparations have emerged as versatile therapeutic agents in wildlife veterinary medicine, functioning through the delivery of multiple growth factors including platelet-derived growth factor, transforming growth factor-β, vascular endothelial growth factor, and insulin-like growth factor. These formulations promote tissue repair and regeneration across diverse applications by accelerating wound healing, reducing excessive inflammation, and facilitating bone and tendon repair in wildlife species. PRP demonstrates particular efficacy in managing chronic wounds, orthopaedic injuries, and complex lesions, with documented success in treating conditions ranging from cervical wounds in geese to perineal wounds in felines, consistently improving wound closure rates while reducing infection risk and necrosis. Specialized variants such as thrombocyte-leukocyte-rich plasma (TLRP) and platelet-rich fibrin (PRF) have shown enhanced effectiveness for specific applications, including shell lesion healing and corneal injury repair, with multimodal protocols combining PRP with complementary therapies such as low-level laser therapy yielding superior outcomes by reducing scarring and promoting organized tissue regeneration. The therapeutic benefits of PRP extend to systemic conditions including intra-articular chondroprotection, intervertebral disc regeneration, and chronic mucosal disorders, making blood-derived regenerative therapies a valuable treatment modality in wildlife veterinary practice.

title: Interventional Radiology: Towards Personalized Medicine
summary: Interventional radiology (IR) represents a core implementation of precision medicine, enabling real-time diagnosis and treatment of disease with millimetric accuracy and minimal invasiveness. The field exemplifies personalized medicine through diverse therapeutic applications, including cryoablation for abdominal wall endometriosis, genicular artery embolization for osteoarthritis pain relief, irreversible electroporation for prostate cancer, and splenic artery embolization for splenic injury. Notably, genicular artery embolization demonstrates IR's capacity to provide durable pain reduction and functional improvement in patients with osteoarthritis, while cryoablation offers a targeted approach to endometriosis management. The future of IR-based personalized medicine will be substantially enhanced by integration of artificial intelligence, machine learning, and radiomics-based predictive modeling, which will enable interventional radiologists to anticipate treatment responses and individualize therapeutic strategies. These advances position IR as a central pillar rather than an optional adjunct in precision medicine, capable of tailoring interventions to patient-specific disease characteristics and anatomical presentations.

title: Acupuncture in Uterine Fibroids Management: Literature Update on Frequently Used Acupoints and the Underlying Mechanism—A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary: Acupuncture in Uterine Fibroids Management

Uterine fibroids are hormone-dependent tumors that develop after menarche and cause significant clinical complications including abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, infertility, and pregnancy complications. Risk factors include genetic abnormalities, race, obesity, and nulliparity, while estradiol and progesterone play central roles in fibroid development through the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis. Acupuncture addresses fibroids through multiple mechanisms: needle insertion activates local receptors and brain integration centers, stimulating endogenous opioids (beta-endorphins, enkephalins, dynorphins) and promoting adenosine-mediated anti-inflammatory responses that provide pain relief and reduce dysmenorrhea more effectively than NSAIDs. Key acupoints including Guanyuan (CV4), Sanyinjiao (SP6), and Zusanli (ST36) improve uterine microcirculation, regulate the HPO axis, and modulate sex hormone levels including estradiol and progesterone. Acupuncture targets traditional Chinese medicine concepts of blood stasis by promoting blood circulation and regulating Qi, with yin meridians and Conception Vessel acupoints most frequently applied in clinical practice, offering a non-invasive alternative to pharmacological and surgical interventions.

title: Perspectives on Intravenous Iron Therapy Logistics and Adherence: Results from a Patient Survey
summary:
# Summary

Iron deficiency anemia, commonly caused by heavy menstrual bleeding, endometriosis, inflammatory bowel disease, and chronic blood loss, produces debilitating symptoms including fatigue, weakness, dizziness, cold intolerance, palpitations, and dyspnea that significantly impair quality of life and activities of daily living. Intravenous iron therapy effectively treats iron deficiency anemia and improves these symptoms in over 60% of patients, with newer-generation formulations such as ferric carboxymaltose and ferumoxytol requiring fewer doses and shorter infusion times compared to older agents like iron sucrose. However, multiple infusions per month substantially reduce patient adherence and quality of life by forcing individuals to schedule their lives around therapy appointments, with nearly 65% reporting adverse impacts on productivity and ability to attend important events. Single-dose intravenous iron regimens are strongly preferred by patients and associated with improved adherence, particularly among socioeconomically disadvantaged populations who face barriers from unforeseen conflicts, scheduling difficulties, and transportation limitations. Payor step therapy policies and treatment burden remain significant obstacles to accessing newer-generation products that could substantially improve patient convenience and therapeutic compliance.

title: Ethanol sclerotherapy for management of endometriomas: an overview of systematic reviews
summary: Ethanol sclerotherapy has emerged as a promising minimally invasive alternative to surgical cystectomy for treating endometriomas in reproductive-age women, offering a technical success rate exceeding 95% with favorable safety profiles. Unlike endometrioma cystectomy, which significantly reduces ovarian reserve and serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels through an irreversible mechanism, ethanol sclerotherapy preserves ovarian function and results in no loss of ovarian reserve while maintaining comparable pregnancy rates to surgery. The procedure demonstrates high efficacy in pain relief, with approximately 86% of patients experiencing pain resolution or marked improvement, alongside a moderate recurrence rate of approximately 14% and low complication rates (11% minor, 1.7% major). Technical optimization through longer ethanol exposure duration (>10 minutes) reduces recurrence risk approximately fivefold compared to shorter exposures, though prolonged exposure increases minor complications, necessitating a clinical balance. Despite these favorable outcomes, the evidence base remains low to very low quality overall, highlighting the need for larger, well-designed studies to fully establish sclerotherapy's superiority over traditional surgical approaches in managing endometriosis-associated pain and infertility.

title: Randomized controlled study on the use of virtual reality for pain relief in oocyte retrieval under transvaginal ultrasound guidance using paracervical block and conscious sedation
summary: This randomized controlled trial investigated virtual reality as a non-pharmacological intervention for pain management during transvaginal oocyte retrieval combined with paracervical block and conscious sedation. Despite prior evidence suggesting virtual reality reduces anxiety and pain in various medical procedures, this study found no significant difference in maximal pain levels at vaginal puncture or oocyte retrieval between women using virtual reality and control participants. The research confirms that oocyte retrieval causes pain through puncture of the vaginal wall and ovarian capsule, and that procedural anxiety can amplify pain perception. While concurrent use of multiple pain relief modalities (such as paracervical block combined with conscious sedation) produces better analgesia than single-method approaches, the addition of virtual reality did not provide additional pain reduction in this specific context. The study suggests that procedure duration significantly correlates with worst pain scores, indicating this factor may be more influential than distraction techniques in this patient population. These findings highlight the importance of multimodal analgesia strategies while questioning the universal efficacy of virtual reality across different procedural contexts in reproductive medicine.

title: The Effectiveness of Traction Massage Combined with Herbal Compress for Relieving Menstrual Cramps: A Randomized Controlled Trial
journal: International Journal of Therapeutic Massage & Bodywork
summary:
# Summary

Dysmenorrhea, or menstrual cramps, affects up to 90% of menstruating individuals and involves a complex biological cascade initiated by prostaglandins released during menstruation. Elevated prostaglandin levels, particularly PGF2α and PGE2 produced by cyclooxygenase, drive excessive uterine contractions and vasoconstriction, reducing blood flow to uterine tissues and triggering oxygen deficiency that activates pain receptors. Additional inflammatory mediators including leukotrienes produced by 5-lipoxygenase further contribute to smooth muscle contraction and ischemia. This randomized controlled trial demonstrated that traction massage combined with herbal compress effectively treats dysmenorrhea and its associated symptoms, including fatigue, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, headache, dizziness, irritability, and lower back pain. The combined intervention improves circulation, reduces inflammation, and relieves muscle tension through muscle stretching and stimulation of blood flow. These findings provide evidence supporting Thai massage therapy combined with herbal compresses as an effective non-pharmacological approach for managing menstrual pain and improving overall quality of life in individuals with dysmenorrhea.

title: Readmissions and Emergency Room Visits Following Outpatient and Inpatient Herniorrhaphies
summary:
# Summary

This study analyzes readmissions and emergency department visits following hernia repair procedures, identifying that approximately one in twelve patients experience unplanned healthcare encounters within 30 days postoperatively. The primary reasons for emergency visits vary by timing: urinary retention and pain control dominate early visits (days 0-7), while gastrointestinal complaints and surgical site infections become increasingly prevalent with delayed return of bowel function contributing substantially to both emergency visits and readmissions. Notably, significant disparities exist in postoperative outcomes, with Medicaid insurance, African American race, inpatient surgery status, and higher comorbidity burden emerging as the strongest independent predictors of unplanned encounters. The study demonstrates that evidence-based interventions including Enhanced Recovery After Surgery protocols, multimodal analgesia, regional anesthesia, peripherally-acting opioid antagonists (alvimopan and methylnaltrexone) for ileus, and modern anesthetic reversal agents can mitigate common postoperative complications. These findings underscore the critical importance of targeted perioperative strategies encompassing patient education, bowel regimen optimization, infection surveillance, and improved follow-up access in reducing preventable readmissions and emergency visits following hernia repair.

title: Online Cognitive-Behavioral Programs for Women Living With Endometriosis: Protocol for a Scoping Review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory and estrogen-dependent condition affecting approximately 10% of women worldwide, characterized by abnormal tissue growth outside the uterus that produces multidimensional burdens including pelvic pain, fatigue, anxiety, depression, and infertility. The underlying mechanism involves endometriosis-associated inflammation triggering central sensitization and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation, which lower pain thresholds and are further exacerbated by stress and anxiety. Women with symptomatic endometriosis experience particularly severe impacts on mental health, emotional distress, and social isolation, with pain often manifesting as neuropathic or nociplastic in nature. Cognitive-behavioral therapies have demonstrated efficacy in reducing pain, depression, and stress while normalizing cortisol levels, with specific techniques including psychoeducation, goal-setting, problem-solving, coping strategies, relaxation training, and mindfulness approaches. Online cognitive-behavioral interventions and digital exercise programs show promise as accessible treatments for improving quality of life and reducing symptoms in women with endometriosis. This scoping review protocol aims to evaluate the evidence for online cognitive-behavioral programs as scalable therapeutic options for managing the complex pain and psychological burden associated with endometriosis.

title: Potential analgesic function of the clitoris in pregnant women: A feasibility study
summary: This feasibility study investigated whether external vibrating device stimulation of the clitoris provides analgesic benefits in pregnant women. The clitoris, a complex organ intimately connected to surrounding muscles, fasciae, and nerves, appears to mediate pain relief through multiple mechanisms involving increased pain detection thresholds, perineal muscle relaxation, and activation of endogenous opioid and other neurotransmitter systems. When pregnant women applied mechanical vibration to the clitoral suspensory ligament area, they reported pain relief in 86% of 304 painful episodes, including benefits for labor pain, postpartum discomfort, and breastfeeding-related pain. The therapeutic mechanism likely involves interaction between pleasure and pain neural pathways, blood flow enhancement, and nervous system responses induced by the vibration. These findings suggest that clitoral stimulation represents a novel, non-pharmacological approach for managing pregnancy-related and perineal pain, though larger studies are needed to confirm efficacy and establish optimal protocols.

title: Impact of Physical Rehabilitation on Endometriosis and Adenomyosis-Related Symptoms: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and adenomyosis are chronic gynecological conditions strongly associated with multiple pain symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and dysuria, as well as infertility, psychological alterations, and reduced quality of life. Pelvic floor dysfunctions and central sensitization contribute to symptom chronification in these patients. This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that physical rehabilitation offers substantial therapeutic benefits through multiple modalities: therapeutic exercise programs (including Hatha Yoga and progressive muscle relaxation) reduce pain intensity and improve pressure pain thresholds, anxiety, and depressive symptoms, while electrophysical agents (TENS, NMES, HILT, tDCS, and CRMRF) effectively reduce pain and enhance quality of life. Manual therapy approaches such as pelvic floor physiotherapy, manual therapy, and Swedish massage produce sustained improvements in pain intensity at 3-6 months follow-up, and multimodal exercise programs additionally improve musculoskeletal function, cardiorespiratory fitness, and reduce fatigue. These findings establish physical rehabilitation as a valuable evidence-based complementary strategy for managing endometriosis and adenomyosis-related symptoms through diverse mechanisms targeting both pain signaling and functional outcomes.

title: Peripheral nerve stimulation for the treatment of anterior cutaneous nerve entrapment syndrome: A case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

Anterior cutaneous nerve entrapment syndrome (ACNES) causes chronic abdominal wall pain and burning sensations through compression of sensory nerve branches from the thoracic intercostal nerves, often precipitated by abdominal trauma or surgical procedures. Clinical diagnosis is facilitated by positive Carnett's sign and response to rectus sheath blocks, which serve as both diagnostic and therapeutic interventions. While conservative management employs medications such as muscle relaxers, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatories, tricyclic antidepressants, and gabapentinoids, peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) has emerged as a promising minimally invasive treatment for refractory cases, modulating pain signaling through orthodromic stimulation of non-nociceptive Aβ nerve fibers. The case presented demonstrates substantial clinical efficacy, with an 80% reduction in pain following temporary PNS implantation. PNS represents an advantageous alternative to surgical neurectomy for managing chronic neuropathic pain conditions including ACNES, with proven applications across multiple pain syndromes such as occipital neuralgia, chronic pelvic pain, and post-amputation pain.

title: Platelet-Rich Plasma in Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Platelet-Rich Plasma for Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome

Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection represents a promising therapeutic approach for interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS), a condition characterized by chronic pelvic pain, urinary urgency, and nocturia stemming from urothelial dysfunction, neurogenic inflammation, and fibrosis. Meta-analytic evidence demonstrates that PRP injection significantly reduces pain scores, urinary frequency, and nocturia while improving functional bladder capacity, voided volume, and maximum flow rate. The therapeutic mechanisms involve multiple growth factors: vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and epidermal growth factor (EGF) stimulate restoration of damaged urothelial umbrella cells, while hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) counteracts myofibroblast activation that drives fibrosis in the detrusor muscle. Additionally, PRP modulates neurogenic inflammation by suppressing mast cell activation through interleukin-1 receptor antagonist (IL-1RA) signaling, thereby reducing neuronal hypersensitivity characteristic of IC/BPS. PRP's balanced regulation of matrix metalloproteinases and their inhibitors restores extracellular matrix homeostasis, while its fibrin matrix provides structural support during tissue repair, offering clinical advantages over botulinum toxin alternatives that may reduce detrusor contractility.

title: Acupuncture-induced pain relief and anti-inflammatory effects in chronic pelvic inflammatory disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that acupuncture and related therapies provide significant pain relief and anti-inflammatory benefits for chronic pelvic inflammatory disease (CPID), a condition characterized by persistent pelvic pain and impaired reproductive function. Acupuncture substantially reduced pain intensity and simultaneously suppressed key inflammatory markers including C-reactive protein, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor alpha, indicating a dual mechanism combining analgesic and immunomodulatory effects. Beyond pain management, acupuncture improved clinically relevant outcomes including enhanced fallopian tube patency, pelvic mass regression, and increased pregnancy rates while reducing disease recurrence. Combined electroacupuncture and herbal medicine showed particularly promising results in alleviating pelvic pain, reducing systemic inflammation, and improving reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest acupuncture represents an effective non-pharmacological intervention for CPID that addresses both the symptomatic burden and underlying inflammatory pathology while offering potential fertility benefits.

title: Clinical Effectiveness and Cost‐Effectiveness of Misoprostol in Comparison to Dilatation and Curettage for Treatment of First‐Trimester Missed Abortion (Gestational Age ≤ 14 Weeks) in Iran
summary:
# Summary

This Iranian cost-effectiveness study compared hospital-based misoprostol administration, clinic-based misoprostol, and surgical dilatation and curettage (D&C) for treating first-trimester missed abortion at gestational ages ≤14 weeks. Hospital-based misoprostol dominated both alternative strategies, achieving higher complete abortion rates, superior quality of life outcomes, and substantially lower costs (saving approximately €1,576.80 per patient compared to D&C) while maintaining acceptable clinical outcomes. Although medical abortion with misoprostol was associated with higher rates of mild to moderate complications including bleeding, pain, nausea, and fever compared to surgical approaches, clinic-based misoprostol with self-administration at home yielded the poorest clinical outcomes and highest indirect costs due to productivity loss. The findings demonstrate that hospital-supervised misoprostol administration represents the most cost-effective and clinically optimal approach for first-trimester pregnancy termination in Iran, offering patients higher satisfaction and acceptance while avoiding the need for anesthesia required by surgical methods.

title: Ethanol sclerotherapy for endometriomas: a fertility-preserving alternative
summary: Endometriomas, ovarian manifestations of endometriosis affecting up to 55% of women with the disease, pose significant clinical challenges by causing pain, infertility, and compromised ovarian reserve. While laparoscopic cystectomy has been the standard surgical treatment, it substantially reduces ovarian reserve by 40–60% as measured by anti-Müllerian hormone levels and carries recurrence rates approaching 50% after five years. Ethanol sclerotherapy has emerged as a promising minimally invasive alternative that treats endometriomas through protein denaturation, epithelial cell dehydration, and induction of coagulative necrosis within the cyst wall. This procedure effectively reduces endometriosis-associated pain, including dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain, while preserving ovarian reserve and anti-Müllerian hormone levels, with retention of ethanol for more than ten minutes significantly reducing recurrence rates. During assisted reproduction, ethanol sclerotherapy yields similar or higher oocyte numbers and improved clinical pregnancy rates compared to cystectomy, while maintaining greater ovarian responsiveness. The procedure carries low complication rates, with minor adverse events occurring in approximately 10% of cases and major complications in fewer than 2%, positioning it as a fertility-preserving, cost-effective alternative for managing ovarian endometriomas.

title: Primary dysmenorrhea and quality of life among university nursing students in Vietnam
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea affects over 78% of nursing students in this Vietnamese cohort and significantly impairs quality of life across all measured domains. The condition is characterized by cramping lower abdominal pain accompanied by nausea, vomiting, dizziness, fatigue, headaches, and sleep disturbances. Genetic predisposition plays a substantial role, with family history of dysmenorrhea conferring a 3.29-fold increased risk, while modifiable risk factors including irregular menstrual cycles, heavy menstrual flow, and frequent caffeine consumption also show strong associations with dysmenorrhea development. Caffeine may exacerbate pain through vasoconstrictive effects leading to uterine ischemia, with caffeine consumers demonstrating a 2.51-fold higher risk of menstrual pain. Pain severity directly correlates with diminished quality of life and reduced academic performance, though non-pharmacological interventions such as warm compresses, rest, and light physical activity provide symptomatic relief. These findings highlight the substantial individual and public health burden of primary dysmenorrhea among young women and identify modifiable lifestyle factors that could be targeted for prevention and management strategies.

title: Lack of Scientific Evidence for the Use of Gestrinone in the Treatment of Lipedema: A Systematic Review
summary: Lipedema is a chronic progressive disorder characterized by abnormal subcutaneous adipose tissue accumulation, associated with pain, pressure sensitivity, and easy bruising. While initially proposed as a potential treatment given its established efficacy in endometriosis, gestrinone lacks scientific evidence for lipedema management due to the distinct pathophysiology of the two conditions. Lipedema involves complex hormonal, genetic, and inflammatory mechanisms, including potential progesterone resistance that contributes to inflammation, abnormal fat deposition, and fibrosis, whereas gestrinone's androgenic and antiprogestogenic properties carry significant adverse effects including cardiovascular disorders, liver changes, and virilization. Current evidence-based treatments for lipedema focus on conservative approaches including compression therapy, complex decongestive physical therapy, low-impact exercise, nutritional guidance, and psychological support, with liposuction with lymphatic preservation reserved for selected cases. Symptoms frequently exacerbate during hormonal fluctuations at puberty, pregnancy, and menopause, suggesting female hormonal involvement in pathophysiology. The systematic review demonstrates that gestrinone is inappropriate for lipedema and that comprehensive, multidisciplinary conservative management remains the standard of care.

title: Light at the end of the tunnel: design, implementation and outcomes of a pelvic pain management programme
summary: Chronic pelvic pain, commonly associated with endometriosis, adenomyosis, and other gynecological conditions, significantly impairs quality of life and causes work disability. A multidisciplinary Pelvic Pain Management Programme incorporating physiotherapy, behavioral interventions, and group-based activities effectively reduces pain intensity while simultaneously improving pain self-efficacy, kinesiophobia, pain catastrophizing, depression, and patient activation. The program achieves sustained biopsychosocial improvements by empowering patients to develop self-management strategies and shift their mindset toward pain acceptance, with enhanced pain self-efficacy correlating with reduced functional impairment and affective distress. Increased patient activation leads to broader healthcare benefits including improved self-management behaviors, better quality of life, and reduced hospital admissions and costs. Group participation additionally reduced participant isolation and fostered personal relationships and sense of agency.
