These findings demonstrate that structured, multidisciplinary pain management programs are feasible and effective interventions for individuals with chronic pelvic pain refractory to conventional treatment.

title: Clinical Study on the Combined Therapy of Sinew Acupuncture and Low‐Frequency Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation for Hemiplegic Shoulder Pain
summary: This clinical study demonstrates that combined sinew acupuncture and low-frequency (1 Hz) repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) provides superior therapeutic benefit for hemiplegic shoulder pain compared to either treatment alone. Sinew acupuncture works through a piezoelectric effect that reduces myofascial tension and facilitates collagen fiber realignment, while 1 Hz rTMS suppresses neuronal excitability in the overactive contralateral motor cortex, promotes neuroplasticity, and reduces inflammation and neuronal apoptosis. The combined therapy group achieved the most substantial improvements, with pain reduction from 5.6 to 1.6 on the visual analog scale, upper limb motor function (Fugl-Meyer Assessment) increasing from 34.1 to 49.4, and activities of daily living (Modified Barthel Index) improving from 49.9 to 71.1. Both individual treatments significantly improved outcomes when used independently, but their synergistic combination addresses complementary pathophysiological mechanisms: local fascial dysfunction through acupuncture-induced bioelectricity and regional cortical imbalance through rTMS-mediated interhemispheric rebalancing. These findings suggest that combined mechanical and neurophysiological interventions may offer an effective approach to managing post-stroke shoulder pain and restoring functional capacity in hemiplegic patients.

title: Long-lasting Effect of Penetrating Acupuncture among Responders: Double-blind RCT of Acupuncture for Vulvodynia
summary: This double-blind randomized controlled trial examined penetrating acupuncture as a treatment for vulvodynia, a chronic condition characterized by vulvar pain that causes dyspareunia, sexual dysfunction, and severe psychological burden including suicidal ideation. Penetrating acupuncture significantly reduced vulvar pain intensity, improved dyspareunia and sexual function, and enhanced overall quality of life and mental health in study responders compared to placebo controls. Notably, among responders, penetrating acupuncture produced longer-lasting pain reduction than skin-touch placebo acupuncture, with placebo responders showing 2.7 times higher risk of returning to baseline pain levels during the 12-week post-treatment follow-up period. The study measured pain outcomes using validated instruments including the Tampon Test for provoked pain, Average Pain Intensity scales, and the Female Sexual Function Index, with improvements averaging 0.52 standard deviations across outcomes. These findings suggest that acupuncture's therapeutic effects in vulvodynia extend beyond placebo mechanisms, offering a potentially valuable non-pharmacological option for patients who frequently resort to alcohol and analgesics to manage their severe pain.

title: Evaluation of endometriosis patients' expectations of a therapeutic patient education program and comparison with practitioners' perception
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic condition affecting approximately 10% of women of childbearing age, significantly impairs quality of life through multiple symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, bowel disorders, and impacts on sexual and social relationships, while diagnostic delays of 7-10 years exacerbate patient suffering and mental health effects. Current management relies primarily on hormone therapy and surgery with only moderate patient satisfaction rates. Therapeutic patient education (TPE) has demonstrated effectiveness in managing other chronic diseases by improving symptom management, quality of life, and patient self-care skills, yet guidelines for its application to endometriosis are lacking. The study found that the majority of endometriosis patients (87.8%) would prefer TPE immediately following diagnosis to prioritize symptom management and quality-of-life improvements, indicating strong patient demand for this educational intervention. Additionally, supportive family interactions were associated with improved treatment adherence and disease management, while controlling or critical family behaviors reduced these outcomes. These findings suggest TPE could address a significant gap in endometriosis management and warrant development of tailored educational programs to enhance patient outcomes and quality of life.

title: Peripheral Nerve Stimulation to the Transverse Abdominis Plane as Therapy for Refractory Abdominal Pain: A Case Series of Two Patients
summary:
# Summary

This case series demonstrates that transverse abdominis plane (TAP) peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) effectively treats chronic refractory abdominal pain through modulation of peripheral nerve activity. TAP PNS works by stimulating large-diameter Aβ fibers, which activates inhibitory dorsal horn interneurons that block pain signal transmission from smaller nociceptive fibers, while simultaneously reducing central sensitization and hyperalgesia. The anterior rami of the T6-L1 spinal nerves, which give rise to subcostal, intercostal, ilioinguinal, and iliohypogastric nerves within the transverse abdominis plane, provide innervation to the entire anterolateral abdominal wall. In both cases presented—one involving bilateral ilioinguinal neuralgia secondary to hysterectomy for endometriosis and fibroids, and another involving postherpetic neuropathic abdominal wall pain—patients achieved greater than 90% pain reduction with TAP PNS after failing multiple pharmacological interventions including opioids, gabapentin, and pregabalin. These findings support TAP PNS as a safe and effective neuromodulatory option for managing refractory abdominal pain while significantly reducing opioid requirements and improving functional outcomes.

title: The effect of curcumin capsule on the severity and duration of primary dysmenorrhea among students: A triple-blind randomized controlled trial in the West of Iran
journal: European Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology and Reproductive Biology: X
summary:
# Summary

This triple-blind randomized controlled trial demonstrates that curcumin capsules effectively reduce primary dysmenorrhea severity, duration, and associated symptoms including pain, nausea, vomiting, lethargy, headaches, and fainting in female students. Curcumin exerts its therapeutic effects through multiple mechanistic pathways: it inhibits cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) enzyme activity, thereby suppressing prostaglandin synthesis, which normally drives excessive uterine contractions and tissue ischemia during menstruation. Additionally, curcumin exhibits phytoestrogen-like properties that enable it to bind estrogen receptors and modulate hormonal balance, further reducing uterine contractility and restoring uterine blood flow while decreasing neural pain sensitivity. The study's results support curcumin as an effective, low-risk complementary treatment for dysmenorrhea that operates through mechanisms similar to conventional NSAIDs but with the added benefit of hormonal modulation through its phytoestrogenic activity.

title: Efficacy of antioxidant supplementation in alleviating endometriosis-related pain: insights from a systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs
summary: Oxidative stress emerges as a key pathophysiological mechanism in endometriosis, contributing to inflammation, tissue damage, and pain perception that characterizes the condition. A meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials examining antioxidant supplementation reveals that these agents reduce oxidative stress and may alleviate endometriosis-related pain, particularly dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain, though with low-to-very low certainty of evidence. Melatonin demonstrates the most consistent pain-reducing potential, with doses of 5-10 mg daily showing significant reductions in dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain, whereas higher doses of 20 mg showed no significant benefit. Combination therapies, particularly vitamin C plus vitamin E, and garlic powder also showed consistent benefits across multiple pain types including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and dyspareunia. However, results remain heterogeneous across studies, with some interventions such as higher-dose vitamin D and resveratrol showing no significant pain relief compared to placebo. Overall, while antioxidant supplementation offers a promising therapeutic approach to managing endometriosis-related pain through oxidative stress mitigation, higher-quality evidence is needed to establish optimal dosing regimens and identify which patients may benefit most from specific antioxidant interventions.

title: Impact of dietary patterns on pain and quality of life in ovarian and deep endometriosis: observational study for nutritional interventions
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including chronic pain, fertility struggles, fatigue, and digestive problems, with inflammation playing a central role in disease pathology. This observational study demonstrates that dietary patterns significantly impact pain management and quality of life in women with ovarian and deep endometriosis. Women with endometriomas exhibit poor dietary habits characterized by elevated consumption of red meat, butter, total fat, monounsaturated fatty acids, and cholesterol, combined with reduced adherence to Mediterranean diet principles and lower dairy intake, all of which negatively correlate with pain control, menstrual function, and sexual function. Conversely, healthy dietary interventions—including increased omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid intake, reduced trans-unsaturated fat consumption, and elimination of gluten, dairy, caffeine, and alcohol—demonstrate significant symptom improvement, with 43-53% of women reporting reduced pain and enhanced quality of life metrics including emotional well-being, vitality, and sexual function. Mediterranean diet adherence emerged as particularly beneficial for health-related quality of life. These findings support nutritional interventions as a non-pharmacological strategy to ameliorate the burden of endometriosis-related symptoms.

title: Matrix Radiofrequency Combined with Myofascial Manipulation in the Treatment of Myofascial Pelvic Pain: A Retrospective Study
summary:
# Article Summary

Myofascial pelvic pain, a significant chronic pelvic pain subtype associated with endometriosis and other pelvic conditions, arises from myofascial trigger points in the pelvic floor muscles that generate local inflammation, mechanical hyperalgesia, and tactile allodynia. This retrospective study demonstrates that matrix radiofrequency combined with myofascial manipulation effectively treats myofascial pelvic pain through complementary mechanisms: myofascial manipulation reduces muscle tension, improves blood circulation, and restores pelvic floor muscle control and coordination, while radiofrequency therapy blocks pain signal transmission, reduces central sensitization, and decreases local inflammatory responses. Following treatment, patients showed significantly reduced pain scores (visual analog scale) and improved pelvic floor muscle function, including enhancements in fast-twitch contraction, tonic contraction, endurance contraction, and reduced resting electromyographic amplitude. The combined approach proved significantly more effective than myofascial manipulation alone, resulting in superior improvements in both pain symptoms and pelvic floor muscle function. These findings suggest that matrix radiofrequency combined with myofascial manipulation represents an effective multimodal physical treatment strategy for managing myofascial pelvic pain and improving quality of life in patients with chronic pelvic pain conditions.

title: Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Iuliu HaţieganuUniversity of Medicine and Pharmacy
summary: Acupuncture demonstrates therapeutic potential for managing psychological symptoms associated with endometriosis-related pain through vagal nerve stimulation. The treatment effectively reduces anxiety and depression by engaging the vagus nerve, which plays a critical role in maintaining physiological homeostasis across multiple body systems. Clinical evidence shows that acupuncture significantly decreased anxiety symptoms in treated groups compared to controls at multiple time points, with the mechanism involving modulation of emotional stress responses. Beyond psychological benefits, acupuncture also improved sleep quality in patients, as confirmed by standardized assessment scales. These findings suggest that acupuncture, particularly through self-administration protocols, represents a promising complementary approach for managing the multifaceted psychological and quality-of-life impacts of endometriosis-related pain. The vagal pathway activation offers a mechanistic basis for why this intervention may be particularly effective for stress-related exacerbation of endometriosis symptoms.

title: Physiotherapy for endometriosis-associated pelvic pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Pain Medicine: The Official Journal of the American Academy of Pain Medicine
summary:
# Summary: Physiotherapy for Endometriosis-Associated Pelvic Pain

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition affecting approximately half of women with the disease, who experience debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. While pharmacological treatments such as NSAIDs and analgesics are commonly used, long-term use carries significant side effects including gastric and cardiovascular complications. This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that physiotherapy techniques offer effective non-pharmacological alternatives, with physiotherapy modalities—particularly electrotherapy and laser devices—achieving the greatest pain reduction compared to manual therapy and exercise interventions. Locally applied physiotherapy techniques produced superior outcomes, with specific modalities including transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS), transcranial electrotherapy, laser therapy, manual therapy protocols, and exercise-based approaches all significantly reducing pelvic pain. Beyond pain relief, physiotherapy improves quality of life, physical function, and mobility through its anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, making it a valuable conservative treatment option for managing endometriosis-associated pelvic pain while avoiding pharmaceutical side effects.

title: Patient Perspectives of Healthcare for Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder in Australia: A Mixed-Methods Study
summary:
# Summary

Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder (PMDD) is a luteal phase condition associated with severe psychological distress, suicidality (2.13 times higher odds of suicide attempts), and impaired social and occupational functioning, yet diagnosis and treatment substantially improve symptoms and quality of life while fostering self-compassion and coping. However, Australian patients face substantial diagnostic barriers, with over half experiencing medical gaslighting and misdiagnosis by general practitioners who often mistake PMDD for depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, or personality disorders due to limited PMDD awareness. Symptom tracking using period-tracking apps and prospective daily charting across menstrual cycles facilitate accurate diagnosis per DSM-5-TR criteria, enabling patients to recognize that their cyclical symptoms are real rather than signs of mental deterioration. Psychiatrists, endocrinologists, and gynecologists demonstrate significantly higher PMDD awareness and lower misdiagnosis rates compared to general practitioners, who serve as gatekeepers despite their limited diagnostic competency. The study highlights how formal diagnosis provides crucial psychological relief and enables patients to develop targeted management strategies, underscoring the need for improved healthcare provider education to reduce diagnostic delays and the substantial burden of untreated PMDD on patient functioning.

title: Pharmacological and nonpharmacological management of dysmenorrhea among senior high school girls in two districts in the Central Region of Ghana
summary: This study examined dysmenorrhea management strategies among senior high school girls in Ghana, revealing that dysmenorrhea causes significant school absenteeism, reduced academic concentration, and psychological effects including stress, anxiety, and depressive symptoms. Pain intensity emerged as the primary driver of pharmacological management, with girls experiencing moderate or severe dysmenorrhea showing substantially higher odds of using nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs or hormonal contraceptives compared to those with mild pain. Nonpharmacological approaches—including heat therapy, exercise, dietary adjustments, and traditional herbal remedies such as ginger, Aframomum melegueta, and Ocimum gratissimum—were instead influenced by educational exposure and field of study rather than pain severity. Notably, business students demonstrated significantly greater use of pharmacological treatments, while agricultural science students relied less on nonpharmacological practices, suggesting that educational background shapes management preferences independently of pain intensity. These findings indicate that dysmenorrhea management in adolescents follows a dual pathway where pain severity drives medication use while educational and sociodemographic factors influence the adoption of nonpharmacological strategies, highlighting the need for comprehensive, culturally-informed approaches to dysmenorrhea management in school-based populations.

title: Lifestyle and Selected Issues Related to Sexual Health: The Importance of Specialist Care in Balneology, Dietetics, and Physiotherapy
summary: This article emphasizes a multidisciplinary approach to managing endometriosis-related symptoms and sexual dysfunction through physiotherapy, balneological procedures, and dietary intervention. Physiotherapy modalities—including pelvic floor muscle training, manual therapy, neuromuscular electrical stimulation, magnetostimulation, and radiofrequency techniques—effectively reduce dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and urinary symptoms while improving sexual function and quality of life. Balneological procedures such as brine baths, irrigations, and crenotherapy, along with mineral-based therapies (radon, sulphide, and iodine-bromine applications), provide symptomatic relief for endometriosis, vulvodynia, and chronic pelvic pain. Dietary modifications—including increased fruit intake, adequate vitamin D, omega-3 supplementation, and Mediterranean dietary patterns with reduced red meat consumption—promote favorable endocrine-metabolic profiles and reduce disease risk. The article highlights that endometriosis triggers a cascade of pathophysiological effects including chronic inflammation, dyspareunia, and psychological stress that ultimately impair sexual health and quality of life, while unhealthy lifestyle factors (obesity, smoking, poor diet) further deteriorate sexual function through disruption of hormone and neurotransmitter production. This integrated lifestyle-based approach with specialist care in physiotherapy, balneology, and dietetics offers a comprehensive strategy to address both the physical symptoms and quality-of-life impacts of endometriosis.

title: The Role of Lifestyle and Diet in the Treatment of Endometriosis: A Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition characterized by ectopic endometrial lesions that cause chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, and infertility through dysregulated production of proinflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and progesterone resistance. Lifestyle interventions, particularly regular physical activity and Mediterranean-style anti-inflammatory diets, substantially reduce symptom severity through multiple mechanisms: exercise inhibits proinflammatory cytokines, upregulates sex hormone-binding globulin to reduce free estrogen, and increases endogenous opioids and serotonin; conversely, high consumption of red and processed meats increases disease risk. Specific dietary and supplement interventions including Mediterranean diet components, vitamins C, D, E, magnesium, zinc, omega-3 fatty acids, and curcumin all reduce endometriosis symptoms, with the Mediterranean diet demonstrating significant reductions in dyspareunia and dyschezia by inhibiting cyclooxygenase enzymes. Mind-body therapies such as Hatha yoga and progressive muscle relaxation effectively reduce pain, anxiety, and depression associated with endometriosis. First-line pharmacological treatments include combined oral contraceptives, progestogens, and the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system, while GnRH agonists, aromatase inhibitors, and laparoscopic surgery are reserved for more resistant cases, with physical activity enhancing treatment efficacy by reducing hormonal side effects.

title: Evaluation Starch-Based Hemostatic Agents “BioSight” as Adhesion Prevention Barrier Tested in an Adhesion Model in Rats
summary:
# Summary

BioSight, a novel plant-derived starch-based hemostatic powder, effectively prevents postoperative abdominal adhesions through rapid in situ hydrogel formation that provides temporary physical tissue separation while inhibiting fibrin deposition and inflammatory cell infiltration. In a rat peritoneal adhesion model, BioSight demonstrated anti-adhesion efficacy comparable to the commercial reference product 4DryField® PH, significantly reducing adhesion area, strength, and tissue thickness while simultaneously supporting tissue regeneration and wound healing. The mechanism involves the starch composition forming a hydrogel barrier that suppresses fibroblast infiltration and collagen deposition, key drivers of pathological adhesion formation. The material is gradually biodegraded in vivo by endogenous enzymes (α-amylase and glucoamylase) into non-toxic saccharides, with a controlled degradation profile that balances protective duration against complete absorption. These findings position plant starch-based hemostatic agents as promising biocompatible, absorbable alternatives for preventing postoperative complications including chronic pain and infertility associated with adhesion formation.

title: Exploring Australian Women’s Attitudes Towards Paid Menstrual Leave: A Mixed‐Methods Study
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary: This mixed-methods study examined Australian women's attitudes toward paid menstrual leave as a potential workplace accommodation for menstrual-related symptoms. The research demonstrates that endometriosis and dysmenorrhea cause severe menstrual pain affecting a substantial proportion of women, with approximately 90% of young women reporting debilitating pain during menstruation. Support for paid menstrual leave was significantly predicted by younger age and a personal history of missing work due to menstrual symptoms, indicating that women with direct experience of menstrual disability were more likely to endorse this policy. The study identified multiple potential benefits of paid menstrual leave, including improved workplace productivity, better work quality, enhanced workplace wellbeing, and promotion of women's health and safety. However, participants also expressed concerns that such leave could inadvertently promote employment discrimination against women by reducing their competitiveness in the job market. Overall, the findings suggest that while paid menstrual leave could support women with conditions like endometriosis, implementation would require careful consideration of safeguards against stigmatization and workplace inequity.

title: Effects of chamomile and l-theanine beverage on menstrual pain, menstrual symptoms, mood, and sleep quality in young women experiencing primary dysmenorrhea: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study
journal: Journal of Food and Drug Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea, affecting over 50% of reproductive-age women, causes menstrual pain, back pain, headaches, sleep disruption, and mood disturbances through increased prostaglandin production and enhanced pain sensitivity. This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study demonstrated that a chamomile and L-theanine beverage significantly reduced dysmenorrhea symptoms compared to baseline, including lower abdominal pain, back pain, loss of appetite, depression, and irritability, while also improving daytime dysfunction and vigor. The therapeutic mechanisms involve chamomile's inhibition of cyclooxygenase and prostaglandin synthesis, combined with apigenin's GABA receptor binding and pain perception reduction, while L-theanine enhances serotonin and dopamine production to improve mood and daytime function. The flavonoid constituents in chamomile can cross the blood-brain barrier to modulate neurotransmitters and regulate the brain microenvironment, providing complementary benefits to direct pain pathway inhibition. This herbal beverage offers a natural alternative to NSAIDs, which carry risks of gastrointestinal and organ damage, addressing both physical pain and psychological symptoms associated with primary dysmenorrhea.

title: Endometriosis: Pathophysiology and the Potential Role of Diet
summary:
# Summary: Endometriosis Pathophysiology and Dietary Management

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by cyclic and chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and systemic inflammation, with affected individuals experiencing additional symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and painful urination that significantly impact quality of life through increased anxiety, depression, and work absenteeism. The pathophysiology involves elevated systemic estrogen activating estrogen receptors α and β, which promote endometrial cell proliferation and angiogenesis while inhibiting apoptosis, creating a hypoxic microenvironment that recruits activated macrophages and triggers neurogenesis—ultimately generating nociceptive, neuropathic, and nociplastic pain through localized inflammation and altered central pain processing. Current pharmacological treatments include first-line hormonal therapies (combined oral contraceptives and progestin-only options), second-line GnRH agonists (which provide superior pain relief), and third-line aromatase inhibitors, complemented by nonmedical strategies such as pelvic floor physical therapy and exercise. Emerging evidence demonstrates that anti-inflammatory dietary approaches, particularly the Mediterranean diet and low-FODMAP diet, reduce symptom severity by lowering pro-inflammatory cytokines and oxidative stress while supporting gut microbiota diversity; additionally, omega-3 supplementation competitively inhibits prostaglandin synthesis, reducing both pain and lesion growth. These dietary interventions appear particularly beneficial for patients with concurrent irritable bowel syndrome, with specific Mediterranean dietary adherence correlating with reductions in dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and non-menstrual pelvic pain across prospective observational studies.

title: Identifying predictors of formal help-seeking for premenstrual symptoms: A machine learning analysis of symptom, functional impairment and barriers data
summary:
# Summary

This machine learning analysis identified key predictors of formal help-seeking for premenstrual symptoms and premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD). Premenstrual symptoms—including psychological manifestations such as low mood and irritability, along with physical symptoms like headaches and bloating occurring during the luteal phase—are strongly associated with functional impairment across work, studies, and social domains. The most robust predictor of help-seeking was impaired social functioning, while perceived symptom severity and prior help-seeking experiences also significantly influenced care-seeking behavior. Notably, a concerning proportion of individuals with premenstrual symptoms endorsed suicidality, particularly among those who had not sought formal help. Multiple evidence-based interventions including oral contraceptives, antidepressants, GnRH analogues, psychoeducation, and talking therapies effectively reduce symptoms, while period-tracking applications and mental health literacy interventions enhance awareness and help-seeking behavior. The findings suggest that addressing functional impairment and improving mental health literacy through school-based health education could increase early help-seeking and symptom management for this commonly underrecognized condition.

title: Botulinum toxin for endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain: a randomised, double-masked, parallel, phase 2 trial
summary: Endometriosis causes chronic pelvic pain in 40–87% of affected women through multiple mechanisms, including production of pro-inflammatory mediators (substance P, calcitonin gene-related peptide, prostaglandin E2, and cytokines) by lesions, which trigger both peripheral and central pain sensitization. Pelvic floor muscle spasm and myofascial dysfunction frequently develop in endometriosis patients and perpetuate pain even after lesion-directed treatment. This phase 2 randomized controlled trial tested onabotulinumtoxinA (botulinum toxin) injected into pelvic floor muscles, which works by inhibiting acetylcholine release and blocking pain neurotransmitters including substance P and calcitonin gene-related peptide. Women receiving botulinum toxin achieved greater pain relief with longer duration compared to placebo, demonstrated significant reductions in the number of spastic pelvic floor muscles and pain rating scores, and notably reduced pain medication usage at one year follow-up. The findings demonstrate that botulinum toxin is efficacious and safe for treating endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain by addressing the pelvic floor spasm component of this multifactorial condition.

title: Effects of Pilates and Yoga on Pain Intensity, Absenteeism, Quality of Life, and Sleep Quality among Primary Dysmenorrhea School Students
journal: Indian Journal of Community Medicine: Official Publication of Indian Association of Preventive & Social Medicine
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea is caused by prostaglandin-induced uterine contractions that trigger pelvic cramping and are worsened by modifiable risk factors including stress, depression, smoking, and higher BMI, as well as non-modifiable factors such as early menarche and family history. This study evaluated two mind-body interventions—Pilates and yoga—as alternatives to conventional treatments like NSAIDs and oral contraceptives in school-aged students with dysmenorrhea. Both interventions significantly improved pain intensity, quality of life, sleep quality, and school absenteeism, with yoga demonstrating a slightly superior effect on pain reduction. Mechanistically, yoga reduces dysmenorrhea by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis, improving uterine blood flow, and activating mood-enhancing neurotransmitters (serotonin, dopamine, β-endorphins), while Pilates improves outcomes through enhanced core strength, flexibility, and controlled breathing. These findings support the integration of non-pharmacological, mind-body practices as effective, accessible treatments for dysmenorrhea that simultaneously address both pain and psychological well-being in adolescent populations.

title: Chinese herbal medicine for endometriosis
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: Endometriosis, a common gynecological condition, causes menstrual pain, pelvic pain, and dysmenorrhea that significantly impact quality of life. This systematic review evaluated Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) as a treatment option, finding that CHM administered orally or combined with herbal enema was effective in reducing endometriosis-related pain and improving pregnancy rates comparable to pharmaceutical treatments like gestrinone and danazol. Notably, women treated with CHM experienced substantially fewer adverse effects than those receiving conventional hormonal therapies, while achieving similar symptomatic relief rates. CHM showed potential in preventing endometriosis relapse and improving fertility, addressing limitations of surgical and hormonal treatments that carry unpleasant side effects and high recurrence rates. However, the evidence base was limited by lack of placebo-controlled trials, which would be necessary to establish CHM's efficacy beyond comparative effectiveness against existing pharmaceutical options.

title: The efficacy and mechanism of an integrated mindfulness-based programme on posttraumatic stress disorder among people with HIV: a randomized controlled trial with 3-month follow up
summary: This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that an integrated mindfulness-based programme (IMBP) significantly reduces posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) symptoms and improves antiretroviral therapy adherence in people with HIV, a population experiencing PTSD prevalence approximately 28 times higher than the general population. PTSD in people with HIV arises from the trauma of HIV diagnosis combined with ongoing stressors including stigma and biological factors such as chronic immune activation and neuroinflammation, and it substantially impairs treatment outcomes by reducing medication adherence, elevating viral loads, and accelerating disease progression. The therapeutic mechanism operates through three mediation pathways: increased mindfulness, enhanced resilience, and decreased rumination, which collectively reshape trauma-related cognitions and foster adaptive coping. Notably, greater engagement in home practice sessions was associated with stronger reductions in PTSD symptoms, highlighting the importance of participant involvement for treatment efficacy. These findings position the IMBP as a promising integrated intervention that addresses both the psychological and medical sequelae of PTSD in HIV-positive individuals, offering advantages over traditional psychotherapies by simultaneously targeting symptom reduction and medication adherence at the 3-month follow-up.

title: Effect of Combined Music Therapy and Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction on Patients with Uterine Adenomyosis Following HIFU Treatment
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the combined effects of music therapy and mindfulness-based stress reduction in patients with uterine adenomyosis undergoing high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) ablation treatment. The combination therapy demonstrated superior efficacy compared to mindfulness alone, significantly reducing postoperative pain (VAS scores), anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbance (PSQI scores) while improving self-care and overall quality of life. These psychological and physical benefits likely stem from music's capacity to regulate emotions and reduce physiological arousal, while mindfulness techniques lower stress and stabilize emotional responses—mechanisms that collectively mitigate postoperative complications by preserving immune function and facilitating faster wound healing. The combined intervention group achieved notably higher utility values and better recovery outcomes, supporting the therapeutic value of integrating music and mindfulness-based approaches in adenomyosis management.

title: Rehabilitative Ultrasound Imaging as Visual Biofeedback in Pelvic Floor Dysfunction: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Rehabilitative Ultrasound Imaging as Visual Biofeedback in Pelvic Floor Dysfunction

Pelvic floor dysfunction comprises a spectrum of impairments including urinary incontinence, pelvic pain, and impaired sphincter control that affect both men and women through disrupted muscle coordination patterns. Rehabilitative ultrasound imaging (RUSI) serves as a visual biofeedback tool that enhances pelvic floor muscle training by providing real-time visualization of muscle morphology and function, thereby improving motor learning and patient awareness. Evidence demonstrates that RUSI-guided interventions outperform verbal instruction alone, significantly improving muscle control, contraction quality, and sustained strength gains across diverse populations including post-prostatectomy men and postpartum women with pelvic girdle pain. For hyperactive pelvic floor disorders such as endometriosis-related pelvic pain, RUSI facilitates relaxation training by enabling patients to visualize their muscle behavior and achieve better coordination. Transperineal and transabdominal ultrasound approaches have both demonstrated efficacy in improving pelvic floor muscle function and reducing symptoms, with transperineal guidance showing particular promise in achieving faster continence recovery in post-prostatectomy patients. Overall, RUSI represents a clinically valuable, non-invasive assessment and intervention tool that supports both patient comprehension and optimal rehabilitation outcomes by bridging the gap between clinical instruction and physiological pelvic floor behavior.

title: Optimizing inpatient access to oral contraceptives: A quality improvement approach in behavioral health
journal: Exploratory Research in Clinical and Social Pharmacy
summary:
# Summary

This quality improvement initiative addresses reproductive health disparities among women with behavioral health diagnoses by implementing a standardized oral contraceptive procurement workflow in an inpatient setting. Interruptions in oral contraceptive use pose significant clinical risks, including unintended pregnancies, hormonal imbalances, and exacerbation of conditions such as endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, and menstrual migraines. The structured workflow, supported by multidisciplinary collaboration and pharmacist involvement, substantially improved medication continuity and administration rates, increasing oral contraceptive administration from 33% to 78% and reducing missed doses from 67% to 22%. The initiative incorporated venous thromboembolism risk assessment using the Padua Prediction Score to ensure safe contraceptive eligibility for low-risk patients. By systematizing medication reconciliation and improving access to oral contraceptives for hospitalized women with mental health conditions, this approach successfully enhanced reproductive autonomy and continuity of care in a population facing compounded barriers to healthcare.

title: Recognition of Juvenile Ankylosing Spondylitis in an Adolescent Female Presenting to a Chiropractor
summary: Juvenile axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory disease affecting the sacroiliac joints and spine, causing chronic back pain, morning stiffness, nocturnal pain, and hip pain that can persist for years before diagnosis. This case describes a 15-year-old female whose presentation included lumbosacral pain, hip pain, prolonged morning stiffness, and sacroiliac joint tenderness with MRI findings of bilateral sacroiliac sclerosis, fatty metaplasia, subchondral edema, and hip enthesitis. Diagnostic delay occurred due to normal inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate), lack of radiographic abnormalities, and over-reliance on incidental lumbar disc findings, highlighting that conventional radiographs often appear normal in juvenile axSpA whereas MRI is more sensitive for detecting early sacroiliitis. Following rheumatologic evaluation and diagnosis, the patient was treated with etanercept, a TNF-α inhibitor that requires tuberculosis screening before initiation, resulting in partial pain reduction and improved daily function within three months. Clinicians, including chiropractors, should recognize key pediatric axSpA features—such as prolonged morning stiffness, nocturnal pain, enthesitis, hip pain, and poor response to conservative care—to facilitate earlier diagnosis and appropriate biologic therapy initiation.

title: Clinical characterization and management of persistent genital arousal disorder/genito-pelvic dysesthesia (PGAD/GPD): a registry study
summary:
# Summary

This registry study characterizes persistent genital arousal disorder/genito-pelvic dysesthesia (PGAD/GPD), a condition predominantly involving tingling sensations in the clitoris or glans penis, often accompanied by genital swelling, spontaneous orgasms, and extraordinary lubrication. Mental stress, sitting, tight clothing, and vibration emerge as primary symptom triggers, while distraction, physical exercise, and warmth provide relief. The disorder exhibits substantial comorbidity, with nearly 70% of patients presenting with psychiatric diagnoses including depression, anxiety, and panic disorder, and approximately 75% experiencing somatic comorbidities such as neurological conditions, herniated discs, and migraine. Management approaches include non-pharmacological interventions—psychotherapy, physiotherapy, pelvic floor exercises, relaxation techniques, and acupuncture were employed by 63% of patients—alongside invasive procedures including pudendal blocks and neurosurgical interventions in select cases. The condition significantly impairs sexuality in over 73% of patients and is associated with dyspareunia and endometriosis, highlighting the complex interplay between neurological, psychological, and gynecological factors in PGAD/GPD pathophysiology.

title: TREATMENT METHODS AND ATTITUDES TOWARDS PRIMARY DYSMENORRHEA AMONG CROATIAN STUDENTS: A CROSS-SECTIONAL STUDY
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea, characterized by cramping abdominal pain accompanied by back pain, fatigue, depressive mood, nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, and dizziness, is mechanistically driven by elevated uterine prostaglandin production during menstruation. Prostaglandins trigger myometrial contractions and vasoconstriction, leading to uterine ischemia and subsequent pain. This cross-sectional study of Croatian students revealed that ibuprofen (84.7%) and paracetamol (29.1%) serve as the most commonly used pharmacological treatments, while non-pharmacological approaches including local heat application (78.7%) and antalgic positioning (75.2%) are frequently employed. Students with severe dysmenorrhea were significantly more likely to seek medical advice, use both pharmacological and non-pharmacological treatments, and paradoxically use fewer oral hormonal contraceptives despite their documented efficacy in reducing dysmenorrhea symptoms. The findings highlight that dysmenorrhea management in this population relies predominantly on over-the-counter NSAIDs and simple thermal interventions rather than evidence-based hormonal or complementary therapies.

title: Optimizing butorphanol-dexmedetomidine dosing for managing catheter-related bladder discomfort post-anesthesia: a randomized trial
summary:
# Summary

Catheter-related bladder discomfort (CRBD) is a significant postoperative complication characterized by suprapubic pain, urgency, and frequency that can lead to agitation, catheter dislodgement, and haemodynamic instability. This randomized controlled trial evaluated three drug regimens for managing CRBD: dexmedetomidine-sufentanil (DSF), dexmedetomidine-butorphanol with lidocaine (DLB), and dexmedetomidine-butorphanol with hexamethonium (DHB). The DLB combination proved superior, achieving the highest CRBD response rates (83.3% at 15 minutes) while demonstrating enhanced postoperative analgesia with lower pain scores, reduced respiratory depression (13.9% versus 41.2% in DSF), and substantially lower oversedation rates (52.8% versus 82.4% in DSF). These advantages reflect complementary pharmacology: butorphanol's κ-opioid receptor agonism directly targets bladder sensation pathways with superior visceral analgesia, while dexmedetomidine's α2-adrenergic agonism enhances opioid effects and reduces cognitive impairment, enabling effective low-dose combinations. The DLB regimen of 0.2 µg/kg dexmedetomidine combined with 10 µg/kg butorphanol represents an optimized, safe approach to CRBD management with superior haemodynamic stability and minimal adverse effects compared to higher-dose monotherapies.

title: Precision nutrition guided by genomic and functional assessments in postmenopause: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates the clinical utility of precision nutrition informed by genomic and functional assessments in managing postmenopausal symptoms. The patient presented with vasomotor symptoms, musculoskeletal discomfort, weight gain, and gastrointestinal disturbances—complications driven by declining ovarian estrogen, progesterone, and androgens, alongside multiple genetic vulnerabilities including variants in estrogen metabolism genes (CYP17A1, CYP19A1), reduced methylation capacity (FUT2, MTR, MTRR), compromised antioxidant defenses (GSTM1, GSTT1, GPX, SOD2), and insulin resistance susceptibility (FTO, APOA2, TCF7L2). Functional testing revealed pancreatic insufficiency, intestinal dysbiosis with elevated zonulin and pathogenic overgrowth, and vitamin D insufficiency. A multimodal intervention combining dietary modification, bioidentical hormone replacement therapy, targeted supplementation (butyrate, synbiotics, magnesium), phytoestrogens, and lifestyle optimization (stress management, sleep optimization, resistance training, sauna therapy) resulted in marked clinical improvements: 50% reduction in joint pain, complete resolution of vasomotor symptoms and GI bloating, and substantial decreases in total cholesterol (227 to 195 mg/dL), LDL cholesterol (130 to 100 mg/dL), and menopausal symptom severity scores (69 to 27). This case illustrates how integrating genomic profiling with functional biomarkers enables individualized risk stratification and intervention prioritization to optimize both metabolic health and quality of life in postmenopausal women.

title: Menstrual Pain Management, School Absenteeism and Educational Performance Among Adolescent Students: Cross‐Sectional Mixed‐Methods Analysis Nested Within a Cluster Randomised Trial
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined menstrual pain management and its impact on school attendance and academic performance among adolescent students in Uganda, nested within a cluster randomized trial. Menstrual pain (dysmenorrhea) was strongly associated with school absenteeism and reduced classroom engagement, with students experiencing severe pain significantly more likely to miss school days and report decreased focus and motivation during classes. Effective pain management—including both pharmacological interventions (NSAIDs, paracetamol, ibuprofen) and non-pharmacological strategies (heat application, stretching, exercise)—significantly reduced absenteeism and improved school attendance. Key barriers to effective pain management included poor menstrual knowledge, low menstrual self-efficacy, inadequate social support, and mental health problems, with these factors independently associated with reduced pain relief and ineffective coping strategies. The menstrual health intervention, which combined education on pain management with access to analgesics, substantially improved participants' pain management knowledge, attitudes, and practices while reducing misconceptions and school absenteeism. These findings demonstrate that structured pain management training and education can serve as an effective public health intervention to support adolescent academic achievement by mitigating the educational impact of dysmenorrhea.

title: XR body illusion for managing pain in fibromyalgia: examining optimal duration
summary: Fibromyalgia is a chronic condition characterized by widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, and cognitive difficulties, conditions theorized to arise from altered multisensory brain representations of the body. This study investigated cardio-visual full body illusion (FBI)—a virtual reality intervention that synchronizes visual perception of a virtual body with heartbeat sensations—as a potential treatment approach. The research identified an optimal intervention duration, finding a quadratic relationship between session length and pain reduction, with 12-minute sessions producing the most sustained pain relief at 24-hour follow-up. The cardio-visual FBI is hypothesized to reduce fibromyalgia pain by recalibrating the distorted internal body representation characteristic of the condition, similar to mechanisms demonstrated by previous virtual embodiment illusions and visuo-tactile interventions that achieved up to 37% pain reduction in chronic pain populations. This approach also increases self-identification with the virtual body through cardio-visual synchrony, potentially facilitating dissociation from painful experiences. The findings support extended reality as an effective tool for chronic pain management, with the 12-minute cardio-visual FBI recommended as an optimized protocol for future research in fibromyalgia treatment.

title: Effect of Trendelenburg positioning on post-operative shoulder pain after gynecological laparoscopy: a randomized clinical trial
summary: Laparoscopic surgery, the gold standard for endometriosis treatment, commonly causes post-operative shoulder pain in 35–80% of patients due to residual carbon dioxide irritating the diaphragm and subsequently the phrenic nerve. This study demonstrates that 20° Trendelenburg positioning significantly reduces this shoulder pain and accelerates pain resolution by enhancing vascular absorption of CO2 through pelvic pooling and mechanical decompression of diaphragmatic tension. The intervention group receiving Trendelenburg positioning exhibited substantially lower pain scores at all timepoints, markedly reduced analgesic requirements, and a substantially lower incidence of post-operative nausea and vomiting (29.3% versus 58.5%). These findings suggest that positional therapy represents an effective, noninvasive strategy to mitigate CO2-related post-operative morbidity following gynecological laparoscopy, complementing enhanced recovery principles and reducing patient discomfort during the critical early mobilization phase.

title: Collection of human bacteria at University of Tartu, Estonia
summary:
# Summary

The University of Tartu bacterial collection houses multiple lactic acid bacteria (LAB) strains with therapeutic potential for probiotic and functional food development. Notably, *Lactobacillus fermentum* ME-3 exhibits both antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, positioning it as a promising candidate for addressing inflammatory conditions. Specific *Lactobacillus plantarum* strains demonstrate clinical benefits: Tensia reduces diastolic blood pressure while Inducia lowers both total and oxidized LDL cholesterol levels. The collection systematically monitors opportunistic bacteria for drug resistance and virulence markers to ensure safety of probiotic candidates. These characterized LAB strains represent valuable biological resources for developing novel probiotics and functional foods with therapeutic applications in metabolic and inflammatory disease management.

title: Empowering Women Through Pharmaceutical Education: A New Approach to Premenstrual Syndrome
summary: Premenstrual syndrome involves complex interactions between estrogen, progesterone, and neurotransmitters such as serotonin and gamma-aminobutyric acid, with genetic predisposition, lifestyle factors including caffeine and smoking, and comorbidities like endometriosis significantly increasing risk. The study demonstrated that a pharmacist-led educational intervention substantially reduced PMS symptoms, with pain intensity decreasing from 6.86 to 3.26 and quality-of-life impairment declining from 97.0% to 60.8%, alongside improvements in irritability, insomnia, and fatigue. Multiple micronutrient supplements—including vitamins B6, C, E, magnesium, iron, omega-3 fatty acids, and tryptophan—were associated with lower odds of PMS, while oral contraceptives provided protective effects. First-line management strategies included lifestyle modifications such as exercise, stress management, and dietary changes, with NSAIDs and targeted supplementation recommended for moderate-to-severe pain. The findings emphasize that evidence-based pharmaceutical education delivered through accessible visual tools and personalized counseling effectively empowers women to adopt multi-modal treatment approaches that address both symptom management and quality of life.

title: Hormonal contraception and hemoglobin levels in urban Malawi: a longitudinal analysis
summary: This longitudinal study in urban Malawi demonstrates that hormonal contraceptive use provides substantial health benefits beyond contraception, particularly through improvements in hemoglobin levels and reduction of anemia-related symptoms. Injectable contraceptives increased hemoglobin by 3.7 g/dL while contraceptive implants produced even greater increases of 5.7 g/dL, directly counteracting the anemia that results from heavy menstrual bleeding. Oral contraceptive pills, IUDs, and other hormonal methods effectively treat irregular and heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and endometriosis, conditions that disproportionately affect women's quality of life and productivity. By reducing menstrual blood loss, these contraceptive methods address a primary cause of anemia in women and prevent the serious pregnancy complications of maternal mortality and morbidity associated with anemia during gestation. The findings highlight how hormonal contraception's physiological effects on menstrual flow translate into tangible clinical benefits, including reduced fatigue, improved energy levels, and enhanced productivity. These results underscore that hormonal contraceptive methods function as multipurpose therapeutic agents with significant health advantages extending well beyond pregnancy prevention.

title: Reproductive Health in Young and Adolescent Females with Inflammatory Bowel Disease
summary: Inflammatory bowel disease, particularly Crohn's disease, significantly impacts reproductive health in young women through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Active IBD and its treatments—including malnutrition, chronic inflammation, and corticosteroid use—delay pubertal development by suppressing growth factors like insulin growth factor I and leptin while elevating hunger signals such as neuropeptide Y. Women with IBD experience menstrual cycle disorders at high rates, with active disease causing irregular cycles, heavy bleeding, and dysmenorrhea affecting 40-62% of patients, while cyclical worsening of gastrointestinal symptoms during menses results from increased endometrial prostaglandin release and estrogen receptor-mediated visceral hypersensitivity. The therapeutic management of these conditions is complicated by the fact that NSAIDs, the standard treatment for dysmenorrhea, can exacerbate IBD, making hormonal contraception a preferred option as it prevents endometrial proliferation, suppresses prostaglandins and leukotrienes, and reduces menstrual bleeding. Active disease and prior abdominal surgery impair fertility and increase risk of unintended pregnancy with adverse outcomes, though optimal disease control through medical and nutritional optimization significantly improves fertility and pubertal development. These findings underscore the critical importance of aggressive IBD management in adolescent and young women to preserve reproductive health and quality of life.

title: Silent struggles: association between primary dysmenorrhea and health-related quality of life among adolescent girls in Lusaka District, Zambia
summary: Primary dysmenorrhea, characterized by painful menstrual cramps accompanied by nausea, vomiting, headache, and dizziness, significantly impairs health-related quality of life in adolescent girls in Zambia, with 86.9% of affected girls experiencing poor quality of life outcomes. The condition substantially reduces functioning across multiple domains including physical functioning, role physical performance, bodily pain perception, general health, vitality, social functioning, emotional role functioning, and mental health, with girls with dysmenorrhea consistently reporting median scores less than half those of unaffected peers. Pain severity directly correlates with quality of life impairment, demonstrating a dose-response relationship between symptom intensity and life impact. Beyond physical symptoms, primary dysmenorrhea significantly affects daily activities and academic performance, indicating far-reaching consequences for adolescent well-being and development. The study emphasizes that primary dysmenorrhea represents not merely a physical health issue but a substantial public health concern affecting both the physical and mental component summaries of quality of life in this population. Notably, menstruation education was associated with quality of life, suggesting potential avenues for intervention and support to mitigate the burden of dysmenorrhea on adolescent health outcomes.

title: Efficacy comparison of different external traditional Chinese medicine therapies as monotherapy or in combination for premenstrual syndrome: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Premenstrual syndrome is a menstrual cycle-related disorder characterized by cyclic psychological symptoms including anxiety, depression, and mood swings, alongside physical manifestations such as breast tenderness, edema, and sleep disturbances, with approximately 3–10% of patients experiencing severe emotional dysregulation. While conventional pharmacotherapies including oral contraceptive pills, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists effectively treat PMS, their long-term use carries significant risks including adverse gastrointestinal and cardiovascular effects. This network meta-analysis demonstrates that external traditional Chinese medicine therapies, particularly Jianpi-Shugan acupuncture (SUCRA = 95.7%) and ear acupressure (SUCRA = 71.2), achieve superior efficacy in improving overall effective rates and reducing symptom severity compared to conventional Western medicine. Multiple acupuncture techniques including the "8" shaped ring method, scalp acupuncture with electroacupuncture, and hypodermic catgut embedding significantly reduced PMS symptoms, with combinatorial approaches such as ear acupressure plus moxibustion demonstrating enhanced effectiveness. Notably, external TCM therapies are associated with minimal adverse effects, with only localized subcutaneous hematomas documented at 5.56% incidence, compared to 45% gastrointestinal disturbances and migraines associated with conventional Western medications. These findings support external TCM therapies as effective, safe complementary or alternative interventions for premenstrual syndrome management.

title: Telehealth cognitive behavioural therapy improves health-related quality of life and pain in endometriosis: the Healing Pelvic Pain Intervention (HaPPI)—a randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a burdensome disease affecting one in nine reproductive-aged women that commonly presents with pelvic pain and diminished health-related quality of life (HRQoL), often complicated by associated anxiety and depression. The Healing Pelvic Pain Intervention (HaPPI) trial demonstrated that telehealth-delivered cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) significantly improved both endometriosis-specific and general HRQoL compared to an education control, with improvements mediated by reduced pain catastrophizing and increased pain self-efficacy across multiple pain types (menstrual, bowel, bladder, and sexual pain). Yoga also showed efficacy as a complementary intervention, particularly for menstrual symptoms and sexual pain, with benefits potentially derived from pelvic tension relief through mindful movement and breathing exercises. These findings are particularly significant given that conventional biomedical treatments—including hormonal therapy, analgesics, and surgery—show inconsistent efficacy and are associated with adverse effects. The results support an interdisciplinary care approach incorporating psychological interventions to address both the pain severity and cognitive-emotional aspects of endometriosis. This represents the first randomized controlled trial establishing CBT and yoga as evidence-based psychological treatments for endometriosis, offering accessible virtual delivery options that can augment standard biomedical care.

title: Safety of individualized herbal medicine for dysmenorrhea: pharmacovigilance from South Korea’s national pilot
summary: Dysmenorrhea is a prevalent condition with significant socioeconomic impact, causing school absenteeism in adolescents and reduced labor productivity in adults, while underlying pathophysiology involves elevated prostaglandins (F2α and E2) and vasopressin that promote uterine and vascular constriction. Although conventional treatments including nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and hormonal contraceptives are effective, their long-term use is associated with serious adverse effects such as hepatic and renal disorders, gastrointestinal complications, thromboembolism, and nausea. Chinese herbal medicine decoctions demonstrate therapeutic efficacy comparable to conventional approaches, improving pain relief and overall dysmenorrhea symptoms in large-scale clinical trials. Pharmacovigilance data from South Korea's national pilot program confirm that reimbursed herbal medicine decoctions carry a favorable safety profile, with no increased risk of severe adverse events including hepatotoxicity, renal failure, allergic reactions, or hospital/emergency department admissions compared to conventional treatments. The adverse effects associated with herbal medicine are limited to minor, transient symptoms such as gastrointestinal discomfort and mild allergic reactions. These findings support individualized herbal medicine as a safer alternative for dysmenorrhea management, particularly for patients requiring long-term pain control or those intolerant to conventional pharmacotherapy.

title: Comparative Effectiveness of Non-Pharmacological Interventions for Pain and Quality of Life in Women with Endometriosis: A Systematic Review and Network Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes multiple debilitating pain symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia, often accompanied by anxiety and mood disturbances. A network meta-analysis comparing non-pharmacological interventions found that physical therapy, acupuncture, and psychological interventions all significantly reduced overall pain, with physical therapy demonstrating the greatest effect. Acupuncture proved most effective for pelvic pain specifically, while physical therapy was optimal for dysmenorrhea; both acupuncture and physical therapy substantially improved quality of life compared to control. Acupuncture's pain-relieving effects operate through neuromodulatory and anti-inflammatory mechanisms, and complementary approaches including nutritional supplementation, pelvic floor rehabilitation, exercise, and psychological strategies such as cognitive-behavioral therapy and mindfulness-based interventions provided additional benefits for pain and anxiety. These non-pharmacological options offer meaningful alternatives to conventional treatments, which are limited by significant adverse effects including gastrointestinal and renal complications from analgesics and hormonal side effects from pharmacological agents.

title: Participant perspectives on a multimodal program for neuropathic pain after spinal cord injury
summary:
# Summary

Spinal cord injury causes neuropathic pain in approximately 60% of individuals, significantly compromising quality of life despite standard pharmacological treatments such as pregabalin, gabapentin, and amitriptyline. This study evaluated a multimodal 12-week pain program combining pain education, exercise, and walking illusions—a sensory-motor intervention that activates the somatosensory cortex to correct sensory-motor mismatch and reduce pain sensation. Participants demonstrated significant reductions in neuropathic pain severity and pain interference following the program, with exercise serving as a primary therapeutic driver through multiple mechanisms including neuroimmune modulation, anti-inflammatory effects, and activity-dependent neuronal plasticity. Beyond pain reduction, participants experienced substantial improvements in functional outcomes including upper body strength, blood pressure, cholesterol levels, and cardiovascular fitness, while also reporting enhanced pain management understanding and reduced medication dependence. These findings indicate that multimodal interventions incorporating behavioral and sensorimotor components offer complementary benefits to pharmacological management, improving both neuropathic pain outcomes and overall quality of life in spinal cord injury populations.

title: Vagus nerve stimulation: An update of currently registered clinical trials on ClinicalTrials.gov
summary:
# Vagus Nerve Stimulation: Clinical Applications and Mechanisms

Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) is a neuromodulatory intervention with FDA approval for drug-resistant epilepsy and stroke that operates through activation of parasympathetic neural circuits and promotion of serotonin and norepinephrine release. The vagus nerve's effects on the nucleus tractus solitarius mediate both cardiovascular function and anti-inflammatory responses, establishing mechanistic pathways for treating diverse conditions. Current clinical trials spanning 147 completed studies demonstrate VNS efficacy across multiple therapeutic domains, with strongest evidence for reducing seizure activity by at least 50% in one-third of epilepsy patients, reducing migraine episodes and intensity, and improving cardiac parameters including left ventricular ejection fraction and atrial fibrillation burden in heart failure patients. Emerging applications show promise in treatment-resistant depression, fibromyalgia, inflammatory bowel disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and substance use disorder, with VNS reducing opioid withdrawal symptoms and pain while potentially reversing neurobiological changes associated with post-traumatic stress disorder. Beyond seizure control, VNS improves quality of life, cognitive function, mood, alertness, and work performance in epilepsy patients, while cardiovascular trials report enhanced autonomic function and global longitudinal strain. These comprehensive clinical trial data establish VNS as a multi-system intervention with growing therapeutic potential across neurological, psychiatric, cardiovascular, and inflammatory conditions.

title: Motion Mitigation Techniques for Abdominal and Cardiac MR Imaging
summary:
# Summary

Motion artifacts represent a significant challenge in abdominal and cardiac MR imaging, with physiological movements from cardiac contractions, respiration, peristalsis, and bulk patient motion causing degradation in image quality and necessitating repeat scans in approximately 20% of acquisitions. Multiple complementary strategies have been developed to mitigate these artifacts, including physiological synchronization techniques such as ECG gating for cardiac motion and respiratory gating with navigator echoes for breathing-related artifacts, as well as pharmacological interventions like hyoscine butylbromide for peristaltic suppression and glucagon for duodenal motion control. Patient preparation measures including fasting and breath-holding further reduce motion-related degradation. Advanced imaging acquisition methods such as fast imaging sequences, parallel imaging, and compressed sensing accelerate data collection to outpace motion effects, while post-acquisition approaches including image registration and deep learning-based reconstructions provide additional artifact correction. These integrated approaches are essential for enabling accurate cardiac cine MRI and flow quantification, where temporal alignment across cardiac and respiratory cycles is critical for precise functional assessment.

title: Optimization of nursing care for patients with acute intestinal obstruction using dedicated multidisciplinary team
summary: Acute intestinal obstruction is a surgical emergency that rapidly progresses to serious complications including intestinal necrosis, perforation, and infectious shock, with mortality significantly reduced by timely and effective emergency intervention. Implementation of a multidisciplinary team (MDT)-based emergency nursing pathway substantially improved clinical outcomes in patients with acute intestinal obstruction by reducing triage, assessment, and treatment times while accelerating symptom relief through coordinated, patient-centered care. The MDT approach enhanced interdepartmental cooperation and information sharing, enabling tailored treatment plans that addressed patients' physical, psychological, and social needs simultaneously. Patients receiving MDT-based care demonstrated significantly faster relief of abdominal pain and vomiting, earlier restoration of bowel function, and notably lower early complication rates (2.27% versus 10.23% in controls). The intervention group also achieved substantially higher nursing satisfaction scores (96.59% versus 81.82%) and improved quality-of-life measures (SF-36 scores), demonstrating that integrated multidisciplinary nursing care optimizes both clinical efficiency and patient outcomes in acute intestinal obstruction management.

title: Efficacy and safety of Dysmenorrhea Patch acupoint application in women with primary dysmenorrhea: a randomized double-blind controlled trial
summary: Primary dysmenorrhea, characterized by menstrual cramp-like pain without identifiable pelvic pathology, involves a dysregulated prostaglandin pathway where excessive prostaglandin F2α (PGF2α) induces uterine hypercontractility, ischemia, and pain, while prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) provides protective effects through smooth muscle relaxation and analgesia. This randomized double-blind controlled trial evaluated a Dysmenorrhea Patch containing typhae pollen, frankincense, myrrh, and dragon's blood applied to four acupoints (SP6, SP10, CV4, and CV8) that are traditionally associated with blood nourishment and qi regulation. After three menstrual cycles, the patch treatment significantly improved clinical response rates (60.00% versus 34.55% in placebo), reduced pain intensity scores, decreased traditional Chinese medicine syndrome scores, and lowered rescue ibuprofen use compared to controls. Mechanistically, the patch reduced serum PGF2α levels while increasing PGE2, thereby improving the PGF2α/PGE2 ratio—an objective biomarker of treatment efficacy. These findings demonstrate that acupoint application with herbal medicated plasters offers an effective non-pharmacological alternative for managing primary dysmenorrhea by modulating the underlying prostaglandin imbalance and improving quality of life with favorable safety profiles.

title: Association between physical activity and mortality in postmenopausal women: evidence from NHANES 2007–2018
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the relationship between physical activity and mortality outcomes in postmenopausal women using NHANES data from 2007–2018. Postmenopause is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, central adiposity, insulin resistance, and metabolic syndrome due to hormonal changes. The research found that both total physical activity and leisure-time physical activity were significantly associated with reduced all-cause, cardiovascular, and non-cardiovascular mortality, with higher activity levels conferring greater protection. Even modest increases in physical activity, from inactivity to insufficient activity levels, demonstrated meaningful mortality risk reduction in this population. Occupational physical activity showed a paradoxical pattern, with lower levels associated with reduced mortality risks. Overall, the findings support promoting adequate physical activity, particularly leisure-time activity, as an effective strategy to support healthy aging and reduce mortality risk in postmenopausal women.

title: Barriers to contraception access and use among youth: A scoping review in high‐income countries
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This scoping review examines barriers to contraception access and use among youth in high-income countries, with particular relevance to managing gynecological conditions beyond pregnancy prevention. Contraceptive methods, especially long-acting reversible contraception (LARC), effectively reduce symptoms of endometriosis and polycystic ovarian syndrome, contributing significantly to patients' overall wellbeing. Youth demonstrate substantial interest in LARC specifically for non-contraceptive therapeutic purposes, including management of menstruation-related symptoms and cramping. The review highlights that contraception serves a dual purpose in youth healthcare, functioning both as a reproductive choice and as a treatment modality for chronic gynecological pain conditions. Understanding these multiple benefits is critical for addressing access barriers, as youth motivation to use contraception extends well beyond contraception itself to encompass symptom management and quality of life improvements.

title: Precision application of target-controlled infusion of esketamine combined with sufentanil in anesthesia for thoracoscopic surgery and its effect on hemodynamics, postoperative pain, and safety
journal: Brazilian Journal of Medical and Biological Research
summary: This study evaluated target-controlled infusion (TCI) of esketamine combined with sufentanil for thoracoscopic surgery anesthesia. Esketamine, the S(+)-isomer of ketamine, demonstrates superior NMDA receptor affinity (3-4 times greater than racemic ketamine) while minimizing respiratory depression and exhibiting sympathomimetic effects that maintain hemodynamic stability. The TCI regimen of esketamine and sufentanil, complemented by propofol for sedation, significantly improved intraoperative hemodynamic control with reduced fluctuations in mean arterial pressure, heart rate, stroke volume, and cardiac output compared to conventional anesthesia. Patients receiving this precision anesthetic approach experienced substantially lower postoperative pain scores at 24 and 48 hours, faster recovery with shorter post-anesthesia care unit stays and awakening times, and reduced adverse event rates. The anesthetic strategy also reduced opioid requirements while maintaining higher oxygen saturation levels. These findings demonstrate that TCI of esketamine-sufentanil represents an effective perioperative pain management approach with enhanced safety and hemodynamic stability for thoracoscopic procedures.

title: Metabolomics Analysis Reveals Gut Microbiota-Associated Sakuranin Modulates Endometrial Stem Cell Differentiation and Inflammation to Alleviate Pain in Endometriosis [Letter]
summary: This study identifies sakuranin, a gut-derived metabolite, as a novel therapeutic candidate for endometriosis-associated pain through a microbiota-dependent mechanism. Patients with endometriosis and dysmenorrhea exhibit deficient fecal sakuranin levels, and administration of this flavonoid metabolite alleviates pain, reduces ectopic lesion weight, and modulates endometrial stem cell differentiation in disease models. Sakuranin appears to exert its effects both through direct biological activity and by entering systemic circulation to act on ectopic lesions while simultaneously regulating sympathetic (TH) and sensory (SP) nerve markers that contribute to pain sensation. The metabolite requires microbial deglycosylation within the gastrointestinal tract for bioavailability, highlighting the importance of gut-host interactions in endometriosis pathogenesis. These findings suggest that sakuranin deficiency represents a key microbiota-associated dysbiosis feature in endometriosis patients, positioning gut-derived metabolites as promising therapeutic targets for chronic pain management in this condition.

title: Exploration of Acupoint Compatibility Patterns in Acupuncture Treatment for Infertility Based on Data Mining
summary: This data mining study identified optimal acupuncture treatment patterns for female infertility by analyzing acupoint compatibility rules across multiple infertility etiologies, including polycystic ovary syndrome, diminished ovarian reserve, endometriosis-related infertility, and unexplained infertility. The analysis revealed that acupuncture effectively treats infertility while simultaneously reducing associated psychological burdens such as anxiety and depression, with documented improvements in fertility rates and live birth outcomes. A core group of 16 acupoints emerged as foundational to treatment, with Guanyuan (CV4) and Sanyinjiao (SP6) being most frequently used and showing the strongest compatibility patterns, achieving 92.09% confidence when combined. The three most frequently involved meridians were the Conception Vessel, Spleen Meridian of Foot-Taiyin, and Stomach Meridian of Foot-Yangming, with front Mu points being the predominant point category selected. The therapeutic mechanism operates through regulating the Flush Vessel and Conception Vessel while harmonizing qi and blood circulation. These data-driven acupoint compatibility patterns provide evidence-based guidance for acupuncture protocol optimization in infertility treatment, offering a complementary approach to conventional pharmacological and assisted reproductive interventions that carry risks such as ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome.

title: Tuina Alleviates Pain Associated with Lumbar Disc Herniation by Regulating Functional Connectivity Between Inferior Frontal Triangularis and Multiple Brain Networks: A Randomized Controlled fMRI Study
summary:
# Summary

Tuina, a traditional manual therapy technique, effectively alleviates pain and dysfunction in lumbar disc herniation patients by modulating activity in pain-processing brain regions, particularly the inferior frontal gyrus triangularis. Lumbar disc herniation causes mechanical compression of nerve roots, leading to low back and radiating leg pain that triggers persistent nociceptive signals and central sensitization—a process involving reorganization of brain networks and enhanced pain sensitivity. This randomized controlled fMRI study demonstrated that Tuina inhibited spontaneous neural activity in the bilateral inferior frontal gyrus triangular region and suppressed its functional connectivity with other brain regions, resulting in significant improvements in pain scores and emotional symptoms. Notably, changes in functional connectivity between the right inferior frontal triangularis and superior temporal gyrus pole correlated positively with pain reduction, while alterations in connectivity between left and right inferior frontal triangularis regions associated with decreased pain sensitivity in the gluteus maximus.
