These findings suggest Tuina provides a non-pharmacological alternative to opioid medications for managing chronic pain, offering therapeutic benefits without the cardiovascular and renal side effects associated with long-term opioid use.

title: Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid intake and pain, inflammatory cytokines, and quality of life in endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory gynecologic disorder, is characterized by pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and impaired quality of life, with immune dysregulation and elevated inflammatory mediators (IL-6, TNF-α, C-reactive protein) driving lesion persistence, neuroangiogenesis, and pain sensitization. This study evaluated adjunctive omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) supplementation as medical nutrition therapy, with the omega-3 group receiving fish oil containing eicosapentaenoic acid and docosahexaenoic acid alongside conventional therapy. Compared to controls, omega-3 PUFA supplementation produced significantly greater reductions in pain scores (visual analog scale decrease of 3.0 versus 1.5), inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, and C-reactive protein all p < 0.001), and across both physical and mental health components of quality of life (SF-36 Physical Component Summary improvement of 12.1 versus 5.3; Mental Component Summary improvement of 10.3 versus 4.8). Omega-3 PUFA intake was independently associated with clinically meaningful improvements including ≥2-point pain reduction and ≥5-point quality of life improvements. These findings suggest that adjunctive omega-3 PUFA supplementation, through modulation of inflammatory tone and oxidative stress, represents an effective complementary strategy for managing endometriosis-related pain and improving health-related quality of life.

title: A Narrative Review and Risk-Stratification Framework for Preventing LNG-IUS Expulsion in Adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) is an effective non-surgical treatment for adenomyosis that reduces dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, pain, and uterine volume, yet adenomyosis patients experience markedly elevated expulsion rates (15–37.5%) compared to the general population. Adenomyosis-induced structural changes—including increased uterine volume, irregular myometrial contractions, and myometrial heterogeneity—substantially increase expulsion risk, as do comorbid conditions such as uterine fibroids, severe dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia. The review establishes a risk-stratification framework identifying high-risk features (uterine volume >150 mL, previous expulsion, severe symptoms, younger age, nulliparity) and recommends tailored interventions including GnRH agonist pretreatment for three cycles, transabdominal ultrasound-guided insertion, and insertion timing after day 9 of the menstrual cycle to optimize device retention. For high-risk patients, emerging techniques such as hysteroscopic suture fixation and high-intensity focused ultrasound pretreatment offer additional protection. Device expulsion triggers rapid symptom recurrence, increased healthcare costs, and reduced treatment adherence, making prevention strategies critical for improving patient outcomes and quality of life in adenomyosis management.

title: Core Acupoint Prescription Patterns for Primary Dysmenorrhea: Data Mining and Network-Based Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea is fundamentally associated with prostaglandin imbalance, which drives intense uterine contractions and localized ischemia during menstruation. While NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase to reduce prostaglandin production, acupuncture offers an alternative mechanism through improving uterine blood flow and modulating prostaglandin levels, with meta-analytic evidence suggesting superior efficacy and safety compared to conventional medications. Data mining analysis identified a core acupoint combination of Sanyinjiao (SP6), Guanyuan (CV4), and Diji (SP8) as the most consistently prescribed formula for primary dysmenorrhea treatment. Network-based pathway analysis revealed that this core combination engages multiple biological systems including inflammation, hormone, and pain signaling pathways—particularly PI3K-Akt, cAMP, and AGE-RAGE cascades—through 29 active compounds targeting 94 primary dysmenorrhea-related genes among 640 potential targets. The mechanistic insights highlight how acupuncture prescription patterns operate through integrated neuroendocrine-immune signaling to therapeutically address the multifactorial pathophysiology of primary dysmenorrhea.

title: The role of vitamin D for COVID-19
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: This review examines the critical role of vitamin D in COVID-19 outcomes, demonstrating a strong inverse relationship between vitamin D deficiency and disease severity. Patients with vitamin D deficiency (serum levels <20 ng/mL) exhibit significantly increased risks across multiple adverse outcomes, including 1.26- to 2.18-fold increased infection risk, 1.50- to 5.57-fold increased severe illness risk, greater than twofold increased ICU admission risk, and 1.22- to 4.15-fold increased mortality risk compared to those with adequate vitamin D levels. Notably, vitamin D deficiency is particularly associated with acute respiratory distress syndrome development, a major determinant of COVID-19 severity. The findings suggest that maintaining serum vitamin D concentrations above 30 ng/mL substantially reduces COVID-19 severity, mortality risk, and clinical complications including ICU admissions and need for mechanical ventilation. Vitamin D supplementation emerges as a preventive strategy that could mitigate both COVID-19 incidence and disease progression by supporting immune function and reducing severe respiratory complications.

title: Digital health technologies in pain management for patients with endometriosis: A scoping review
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease that causes severe pain symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and dyspareunia, significantly impacting patient quality of life. This scoping review examined how digital health technologies address endometriosis-related pain management across five core dimensions: pain assessment, monitoring, diagnosis, intervention, and education. Multiple digital health approaches demonstrated efficacy, including remote network platforms, mobile applications (such as the Endo app), virtual reality-based interventions, artificial intelligence tools, and psychological interventions incorporating digital storytelling and meditation. Evidence from seven studies showed that digital health technology-based interventions reduce pain intensity and medication utilization while improving self-efficacy and quality of life, with additional benefits including reduced anxiety and depression. Specialized assessment tools such as the Visual Analogue Scale, Endometriosis Symptom Diary, and web-based patient-reported outcome measurement systems enable continuous monitoring and real-time tracking of pain symptoms. These findings underscore digital health technologies as promising, multifaceted solutions for comprehensive pain management in endometriosis patients, offering accessible alternatives that enhance both clinical outcomes and psychological well-being.

title: Acupuncture combined with conventional therapy versus conventional therapy alone for chronic pelvic pain in women
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that combining acupuncture with conventional pharmacotherapy substantially outperforms conventional therapy alone in treating chronic pelvic pain, a condition frequently associated with endometriosis. The combined treatment achieved a superior response rate of 94.55% compared to 78.18% in controls, with greater reductions in visual analog scale pain scores (4.68 vs. 3.91 points). Beyond pain relief, combined therapy produced markedly superior anti-inflammatory effects, reducing key cytokines IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α by 55.7%, 55.3%, and 61.1% respectively, compared to 32-38% reductions with conventional therapy alone. The combination treatment also yielded clinically meaningful improvements in quality of life metrics, including sleep quality and physical functioning, with SF-36 physical and mental component scores improving by 32.3 and 30.6 points respectively. Acupuncture's mechanisms likely involve modulation of endogenous opioid systems, neurotransmitter regulation, and anti-inflammatory effects that modify pain processing at spinal and supraspinal levels, offering a multimodal approach to the complex pathophysiology of chronic pelvic pain.

title: Deep neuromuscular blockade improves surgical conditions during laparoendoscopic single-site (LESS) surgery for total hysterectomy and reduces postoperative pain: a randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial demonstrated that deep neuromuscular blockade (NMB) significantly improves surgical conditions during laparoendoscopic single-site hysterectomy while reducing postoperative pain compared to moderate NMB. Surgeons rated operating conditions as "good" or "optimal" in 90.83% of patients receiving deep NMB, substantially higher than the moderate NMB group, with cisatracurium used to achieve and maintain the blockade. Patients in the deep NMB group experienced lower postoperative shoulder pain across all time points (6, 24, and 48 hours postoperatively) and reduced morphine consumption. However, deep NMB was associated with prolonged neuromuscular recovery, including longer times to achieve train-of-four ratios of 25% and 90%, as well as delayed extubation, requiring reversal with neostigmine and atropine. No significant differences emerged between groups regarding postoperative nausea, vomiting, pulmonary infection, or length of hospital stay, indicating that the improved surgical conditions and pain reduction do not come at the cost of increased major complications.

title: Integrated network pharmacology and metabolomics to analyze the mechanism of Shaofu Zhuyu Decoction in treatment of dysmenorrhea of cold coagulation and blood stasis
summary:
# Summary

Shaofu Zhuyu Decoction (SFZYD), a traditional Chinese herbal formula composed of ten medicinal plants, effectively treats primary dysmenorrhea associated with cold coagulation and blood stasis through multi-component, multi-target, and multi-pathway mechanisms. The decoction inhibits key inflammatory mediators including TNF-α and IL-6, and reduces the expression of COX-2 and phosphorylated ERK2 in uterine tissue, thereby suppressing uterine inflammation and excessive smooth muscle contraction. SFZYD downregulates phosphorylated myosin light chain (p-MLC) expression, which directly reduces the strong uterine contractions that characterize dysmenorrhea pain. Network pharmacological analysis identified 93 chemical components from SFZYD acting on 52 potential targets involved in thrombosis, angiogenesis, vasodilation, and inflammatory immune pathways, while metabolomic analysis revealed 12 endogenous differential metabolites associated with four metabolic pathways affected by treatment. These findings establish a comprehensive mechanistic basis for SFZYD's therapeutic efficacy in dysmenorrhea, supporting its use as an alternative to conventional NSAIDs and prostaglandin antagonists.

title: Prevalence and associated factors of primary dysmenorrhea among women in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis examined the prevalence and risk factors for primary dysmenorrhea (menstrual pain without pelvic pathology) in sub-Saharan African women. The primary biological mechanism underlying dysmenorrhea involves excessive production of uterine prostaglandins, which trigger abnormal uterine contractions and pain, with genetic factors influencing prostaglandin production, pain sensitivity, and hormonal regulation. Strong epidemiological associations were identified including family history of dysmenorrhea (5.2-fold increased odds), irregular menstrual cycles (2.57-fold increased odds), and short cycle length less than 21 days (3.31-fold increased odds), while sexual intercourse initiation was protective, reducing dysmenorrhea odds by 61%. Additional risk factors encompassed younger age, early menarche, psychological stress, poor dietary habits, caffeine intake, and prolonged menstrual flow. Beyond its biological manifestations, primary dysmenorrhea substantially impacts quality of life and academic performance, causing school absenteeism and reduced academic engagement, with particular burden among adolescents and young women experiencing moderate to severe pain.

title: Pelvic floor exercise: Awareness, knowledge, beliefs and practices among pregnant women in a Ghanaian setting
summary: Pregnancy and childbirth impose significant mechanical and hormonal stress on the pelvic floor, with increased intra-abdominal pressure and elevated relaxin levels contributing to pelvic floor muscle weakness and dysfunction. Pelvic floor muscle exercises (PFMEs), including Kegel exercises, serve as an evidence-based preventive and therapeutic intervention that strengthens pelvic floor muscles and reduces the incidence of stress urinary incontinence, fecal incontinence, and pelvic organ prolapse. Among pregnant women in Ghana, approximately 80–83% believe that PFMEs facilitate vaginal delivery, reduce vaginal trauma, and improve postpartum recovery, though knowledge and awareness remain suboptimal in this population. Healthcare providers and media sources play critical roles in disseminating information about pelvic floor exercises, yet the study suggests variable uptake of these practices among pregnant women. As the primary first-line behavioral treatment for pelvic floor dysfunction, PFMEs are recommended to prevent and manage complications within 6–12 months postpartum, making provider education and patient awareness essential for optimizing maternal pelvic health outcomes.

title: Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome
summary:
# Summary: Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome

Systemic nickel allergy syndrome (SNAS) results from a complex immune response initiated when nickel ions bind to toll-like receptor 4 on dendritic cells, activating them to present antigen to naive T cells and generate memory T cells that trigger widespread inflammation upon subsequent nickel exposure. Internal nickel exposure—through dietary sources such as chocolate, leafy greens, legumes, nuts, and canned foods, or implanted medical devices containing nickel-titanium alloys—activates tissue-resident memory T cells, producing diverse systemic manifestations including gastrointestinal symptoms (bloating, abdominal pain, heartburn), dermatologic conditions (contact dermatitis, urticaria, angioedema), and neurological effects (migraines, headaches, chronic fatigue). The syndrome shows significant associations with multiple comorbidities including irritable bowel syndrome, lactose intolerance, gastroesophageal reflux disease, obesity, thyroid autoimmunity, and notably endometriosis in women, possibly mediated by nickel's estrogenic properties. Notably, women demonstrate higher prevalence of nickel allergy than men, and patients with pre-existing nickel allergy experience substantially increased adverse effects following implantation of nickel-containing medical devices such as patent foramen ovale closure devices. Treatment with a low-nickel diet—the current standard of care—effectively reduces both cutaneous and extracutaneous symptoms, producing marked improvement in gastrointestinal manifestations and dermatitis.
